The **steel beams** forming its skeleton are the links. The **hinges** between them are the joints. The **electric motors** at each hinge are the actuators. The **encoders** mounted on motor shafts are the sensors. And the **industrial PLC/cabinet** blinking in the control panel is the controller. Without any one of these, the arm is either a dead piece of metal, an uncontrollable floppy structure, or a blind moving hazard. ## 1.3 The "Sense–Plan–Act" Paradigm The anatomy of a manipulator is best understood through the closed-loop paradigm that ties Sensors → Controller → Actuators together: $$\text{Sensors (Measure)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Controller (Decide)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Actuators (Move)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mechanism (Links+ Joints)}$$ This loop runs thousands of times per second in modern manipulators, with typical servo rates between **1 kHz and 8 kHz**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for Exams:** Remember the acronym **"LJASC"** — **L**inks, **J**oints, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors, **C**ontroller. KTU examiners often frame a 3-mark question as *"List and briefly explain the basic components of a robotic manipulator."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Links — The Skeleton of the Manipulator A **link** is a rigid body that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between the joint axes it connects. In standard Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) notation (covered in Module 2), each link $i$ is described by four parameters: $a_i$, $\alpha_i$, $d_i$, $\theta_i$. - **Base Link (Link 0):** The fixed, immovable link attached to the foundation/floor. It defines the manipulator's coordinate origin. - **End-Effector Link (Link n):** The terminal link that carries the gripper, welding torch, or tool. - **Intermediate Links:** Connect successive joints and transmit forces/torques along the kinematic chain. A manipulator with $n$ joints has exactly $n+1$ links. ## 2.2 Joints — The Articulations Joints constrain the relative motion between two adjacent links. There are two principal lower-pair joints in robotics (lower-pair joints have surface contact, providing high load capacity): ### 2.2.1 Revolute Joint (R) - Allows **rotation** about a single axis. - Provides **1 rotational DOF**. - 1-DOF variable: joint angle $\theta_i$ (radians or degrees). - Example: Elbow of a human arm. ### 2.2.2 Prismatic Joint (P) - Allows **linear sliding** along a single axis. - Provides **1 translational DOF**. - 1-DOF variable: joint displacement $d_i$ (meters). - Example: Telescopic extension of a hydraulic cylinder. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Spherical, cylindrical, screw, planar, and universal joints exist but are less common in industrial manipulators. A typical 6-DOF industrial arm uses a combination of R and P joints (e.g., the SCARA robot uses an RRP configuration). ## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Kützbach/Grübler Criterion The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a manipulator is the number of independent parameters required to completely specify its configuration in space. For a **spatial serial manipulator** with $n$ single-DOF joints: $$\text{DOF} = n$$ For general planar/ spatial mechanisms with multiple links and joints, KTU examiners expect the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion**: $$\text{DOF} = \lambda\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i$$ Where: - $\lambda$ = 3 (planar) or 6 (spatial) - $n$ = total number of links (including ground) - $j$ = total number of joints - $f_i$ = DOF of the $i$-th joint ## 2.4 Actuators — The Muscles Actuators convert stored energy (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic) into mechanical motion. The three industrial classes are: | Actuator Type | Energy Source | Strengths | Weaknesses | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Electric (Servo/Stepper)** | Electrical | Precise, clean, easy to control | Lower power-to-weight ratio | | **Hydraulic** | Pressurized oil | Very high force/torque, stiff | Leaks, bulky, needs pump | | **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Fast, simple, cheap | Low stiffness, hard to control precisely | **Selection trade-off rule of thumb** (essential for design questions): $$\text{Payload} < 20\,\text{kg} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Electric} \quad \vert \quad \text{Payload} > 200\,\text{kg} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Hydraulic}$$ ## 2.5 Sensors — The Nervous System Sensors fall into two functional categories: **A. Internal State Sensors (Proprioceptive):** - **Encoders (Incremental / Absolute):** Measure joint angle $\theta$ or position $d$. - **Resolver:** Robust analog rotary position sensor. - **Tachometer:** Measures joint angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$. - **Current/Torque Sensor:** Indirectly measures actuator effort. **B. External State Sensors (Exteroceptive):** - **Vision cameras (CCD/CMOS):** Object detection & recognition. - **Force/Torque sensor (at wrist):** Measures wrench applied by environment. - **Proximity sensors, LIDAR:** Obstacle detection. ## 2.6 Controller — The Brain The **controller** is a digital computer (industrial PC, PLC, or DSP) that executes the following closed-loop cycle at a fixed sampling period $T_s$ (typically **1 ms to 1 µs**): 1. **Read** sensor values (e.g., $\theta_{\text{actual}}$ from encoder). 2. **Compute** the error: $e(t) = \theta_{\text{desired}} - \theta_{\text{actual}}$. 3. **Apply** control law (e.g., PID): $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$. 4. **Send** command $u(t)$ to the actuator amplifier. 5. **Repeat** at the next sampling instant. The classic **PID control law** is therefore: $$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Spatial DOF of serial chain | $\text{DOF} = n$ | $n$ = number of single-DOF joints | dimensionless | | 2 | Kutzbach–Grübler criterion | $\text{DOF} = \lambda(n-1-j) + \sum f_i$ | $\lambda=3$ planar, $6$ spatial | dimensionless | | 3 | Number of links | $L = n + 1$ | $n$ = number of joints | count | | 4 | Workspace volume (sphere approx.) | $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | $R$ = max reach | $\text{m}^3$ | | 5 | Joint torque (gravity load) | $\tau = m g r$ | $m$ = payload, $r$ = lever arm | $\text{N·m}$ | | 6 | Encoder resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | $N$ = pulses per revolution | degrees/pulse | | 7 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | $f_{\text{max}}$ = highest signal freq. | Hz | | 8 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | error $e$ | varies | | 9 | Servo bandwidth (typical) | $f_{bw} \approx 10\text{–}100\,\text{Hz}$ | industrial arm | Hz | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The LJASC anatomy appears in **every** robot — from a 6-axis KUKA car-welding arm in a Bengaluru plant to a 7-DOF Franka Emika Panda research arm, to a 4-DOF SCARA used in PCB assembly. Understanding anatomy is the prerequisite for kinematics (Module 2), dynamics (Module 3), and control (Module 4). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a 6R Industrial Manipulator Consider the **PUMA 560** — a classic 6-DOF industrial manipulator with 6 revolute joints. We will verify its DOF using the Kutzbach–Grübler criterion, then rationalize the result with the serial-chain shortcut. ### Step 1: Count the links The PUMA has 6 joints and 1 fixed base. $$n = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ links}$$ ### Step 2: Count the joints Six revolute joints, each providing 1 DOF. $$j = 6, \quad f_i = 1 \;\forall\; i \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i = 6$$ ### Step 3: Apply the spatial Kutzbach–Grübler equation For a 3-D spatial mechanism, $\lambda = 6$: $$\text{DOF} = \lambda\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i$$ $$\text{DOF} = 6\,(7 - 1 - 6) + 6$$ $$\text{DOF} = 6\,(0) + 6 = 6$$ ### Step 4: Cross-verify using the serial-chain rule For a single-open-chain spatial manipulator with $n$ single-DOF joints, the formula reduces to: $$\text{DOF}_{\text{serial}} = n$$ $$\text{DOF}_{\text{PUMA}} = 6 \;\checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Six DOF is the *magic number* for general task-space manipulation. A rigid body in 3-D space has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations), so 6 joints are required to position and orient an end-effector at an arbitrary pose — a fact KTU examiners love to test. ## 3.2 Derivation: Joint Torque to Hold a Static Payload A 2-link planar arm holds a payload of mass $m$ at its end-effector. Link 2 has length $L_2$ and is held horizontally. Find the torque required at joint 2 to support the load against gravity. ### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram — forces on the payload The payload exerts a downward force: $$F = m g$$ where $g \approx 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ is the standard gravitational acceleration. ### Step 2: Moment equilibrium about joint 2 Taking moments about joint 2 (counter-clockwise positive), the gravitational force creates a clockwise moment of magnitude $F \cdot L_2$. The actuator must supply an equal and opposite torque $\tau_2$: $$\tau_2 - m g L_2 = 0$$ ### Step 3: Solve for $\tau_2$ $$\tau_2 = m g L_2$$ ### Step 4: Numerical example Let $m = 5\,\text{kg}$, $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$: $$\tau_2 = 5 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 24.525\,\text{N·m}$$ This is the **static holding torque** the joint-2 actuator (and its gear-train, after the gear ratio $N$) must continuously provide. $$\tau_{\text{motor}} = \frac{\tau_2}{N \cdot \eta}$$ where $\eta$ is the gearbox efficiency (typically **0.85–0.95**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** This derivation is a favorite 7-mark question. Always state the FBD, the moment equation, and the numerical substitution separately. Examiners explicitly award 2 marks for the FBD, 3 marks for the equation, and 2 marks for the final numerical answer. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensor Types | Property | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder | Resolver | Potentiometer | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Output** | Pulses | Digital code (binary/Gray) | Analog (sin/cos) | Analog voltage | | **Position on power-up** | Lost — needs homing | Retained | Retained | Retained | | **Resolution (typical)** | 1,000–65,536 ppr | 12–25 bits | ~14 bits effective | ~10 bits | | **Robustness** | Moderate | Moderate | **Very high** (harsh env.) | Low (wear) | | **Cost** | Low | Medium-High | High | Very low | ## 3.4 Selection Matrix: Choosing an Actuator | Requirement | Recommended Actuator | | :--- | :--- | | Clean room, low payload, high precision | Brushless DC servo with harmonic drive | | High payload, low speed, stiffness | Hydraulic servo | | Fast pick-and-place, low precision | Pneumatic | | Open-loop positioning, low cost | Stepper motor | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Architecture of a Robotic Manipulator The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical feedback architecture that links all five anatomical sub-systems in a single robotic joint servo loop. ```mermaid flowchart LR cmdA[Desired Trajectory
theta desired] --> cmpA fbA[Encoder Feedback
theta actual] --> cmpA cmpA{Summing
Junction} --> errA[Error Signal
e t equals theta d minus theta a] errA --> ctrlA[Controller
PID Law] ctrlA --> ampA[Power Amplifier
Driver] ampA --> actA[Actuator
Servo Motor] actA --> jntA[Joint
Revolute or Prismatic] jntA --> lnkA[Link
Rigid Body] lnkA --> toolA[End Effector
Gripper or Tool] lnkA --> fbA jntA --> snsA[Sensor Bank
Encoders Force Vision] snsA --> fbA classDef ioBox fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 classDef procBox fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef mechBox fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#B45309,color:#000 class cmdA,fbA,errA ioBox class cmpA,ctrlA,ampA procBox class actA,jntA,lnkA,toolA,snsA mechBox ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **outer loop** is the trajectory-tracking loop executed by the controller at the joint level. - The **inner loop** represents the physical coupling: actuator torque → joint motion → link motion → sensor reading. ## 4.2 Anatomical Block Map — The LJASC Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TB sysA[Robotic Manipulator System] --> lksA[Links
L equals n plus 1] sysA --> jtsA[Joints
R and P Types] sysA --> actB[Actuators
Electric Hydraulic Pneumatic] sysA --> snsB[Sensors
Internal and External] sysA --> ctlA[Controller
PID Architecture] lksA --- jtsA jtsA --- actB actB --- ctlA ctlA --- snsB snsB --- jtsA classDef anchor fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 class sysA anchor class lksA,jtsA,actB,snsB,ctlA sub ``` ## 4.3 Kinematic Chain — 6-DOF Articulated Arm ```mermaid flowchart LR baseA[Base Link 0] --> j1((Joint 1
Revolute Waist)) j1 --> l1[Link 1
Shoulder] l1 --> j2((Joint 2
Revolute Shoulder)) j2 --> l2[Link 2
Upper Arm] l2 --> j3((Joint 3
Revolute Elbow)) j3 --> l3[Link 3
Forearm] l3 --> j4((Joint 4
Revolute Wrist Roll)) j4 --> l4[Link 4
Wrist Pitch 1] l4 --> j5((Joint 5
Revolute Wrist Yaw)) j5 --> l5[Link 5
Wrist Pitch 2] l5 --> j6((Joint 6
Revolute Tool Flange)) j6 --> eeA[End Effector
Gripper] classDef rotNode fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B58900,color:#000 classDef linkNode fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 class j1,j2,j3,j4,j5,j6 rotNode class baseA,l1,l2,l3,l4,l5,eeA linkNode ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Each circle represents a **revolute joint** providing 1 rotational DOF. Each rectangle is a **rigid link**. The total DOF is **6** (six R joints), matching the PUMA-style articulated architecture analyzed in Section 3.1. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **List and briefly explain any five basic components of a robotic manipulator.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Links** — Rigid structural members that connect joints and provide the geometric skeleton of the arm. They define the manipulator's reach and shape. 2. **Joints** — Mechanical articulations (revolute R or prismatic P) that constrain and permit relative motion between adjacent links, providing the DOF. 3. **Actuators** — Energy-conversion devices (electric/hydraulic/pneumatic motors) that supply the torque or force required to move the links. 4. **Sensors** — Devices (encoders, resolvers, force sensors, cameras) that measure the manipulator's internal state (position/velocity) and external state (force/vision). 5. **Controller** — The digital computer that reads sensor data, computes errors against desired trajectories, and issues control signals to the actuators in a closed loop. *(Examiner's note: Award 0.5 marks per component name and 0.5 marks for its function. Total 5 × 1 = 5, scaled to 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest Module 1]** **Differentiate between a revolute joint and a prismatic joint with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Motion type | Rotation about an axis | Linear translation along an axis | | Joint variable | Angle $\theta$ (rad / deg) | Displacement $d$ (m) | | DOF contributed | 1 rotational | 1 translational | | Example | Elbow of a human arm; waist joint of PUMA | Telescopic slider; horizontal rail of Cartesian robot | Both joints are classified as **lower-pair joints** with surface contact, providing high load-carrying capacity and low wear. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > • When asked for "components of a manipulator," **do not confuse sensors with actuators** — encoders do *not* move the arm, motors do. > • In DOF questions, **always state whether the mechanism is planar or spatial** before applying Kutzbach–Grübler; mixing $\lambda = 3$ and $\lambda = 6$ is the most common 2-mark loss. > • In the torque derivation, **draw the FBD explicitly** — KTU award 2 marks for a clear diagram. > • Do not write PID control law without defining the three gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop feedback architecture** of a single joint of a robotic manipulator. Explain the role of each of the following: controller, actuator, sensor, and links. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1 = 0.4\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 0.3\,\text{m}$ holds a payload of $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ at its end-effector, with link 2 horizontal. Compute the static joint torques required at joints 1 and 2 to hold the configuration. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Role of Components (7 marks) **Block diagram description (3 marks):** The closed-loop architecture consists of: - **Reference input** $\theta_d$ (desired joint angle) → enters a **summing junction**. - **Summing junction** subtracts the feedback $\theta_a$ (actual angle from sensor) to produce the **error signal** $e(t) = \theta_d - \theta_a$. - The error is fed into the **controller** (e.g., PID) which produces a control signal $u(t)$. - $u(t)$ is amplified by the **power amplifier** and sent to the **actuator** (servo motor). - The actuator produces a torque $\tau$ acting on the **joint**, which drives the **link** and the **end-effector**. - An **encoder/resolver** mounted on the joint shaft measures $\theta_a$ and closes the loop. **Roles of the four sub-systems (4 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Controller:** The "brain." It executes the control algorithm (PID / computed torque / adaptive) at a fixed sampling period, computing $u(t)$ from $e(t)$. 2. **Actuator:** The "muscle." It converts electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into mechanical torque/force that physically moves the joint. 3. **Sensor:** The "nerve ending." It measures the joint's current state (position $\theta_a$ and velocity $\dot\theta_a$) and feeds this back to the controller. 4. **Links:** The "bones." They are the rigid structural members that determine the manipulator's reach, payload capacity, and inertia. They transmit forces between joints. --- ##### Part (b) — Static Joint Torques (7 marks) **Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the 2-link arm horizontally: - Link 1 extends along +x from base to joint 2 (length $L_1$). - Link 2 extends along +x from joint 2 to payload (length $L_2$). - Payload mass $m$ at the end-effector exerts a downward force $F = mg$ at distance $(L_1 + L_2)$ from joint 1. **Step 2 — Torque at joint 2 (2.5 marks):** The torque at joint 2 must balance the moment of the payload about joint 2: $$\tau_2 = m g L_2$$ $$\tau_2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.3 = 5.886\,\text{N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Torque at joint 1 (2.5 marks):** The torque at joint 1 must support the entire weight of link 2 + payload acting at a distance $L_1 + L_2$ from joint 1: $$\tau_1 = m g (L_1 + L_2)$$ $$\tau_1 = 2 \times 9.81 \times (0.4 + 0.3) = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.7 = 13.734\,\text{N·m}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\tau_1 \approx 13.73\,\text{N·m}, \qquad \tau_2 \approx 5.89\,\text{N·m}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]* **(a)** Define **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator. Using the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion**, calculate the DOF of a spatial mechanism having 8 links and 9 joints, where 7 joints are revolute and 2 are universal (universal joint = 2 DOF). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the three major types of **actuators** used in robotics (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic) on the basis of energy source, torque/force capability, precision, and typical application. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — DOF Calculation (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the number of independent parameters (translations and rotations) required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the mechanism in space. **Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):** - Total links (including ground): $n = 8$ - Total joints: $j = 9$ - Revolute joints: 7 (each $f_i = 1$) - Universal joints: 2 (each $f_i = 2$) - Sum of joint freedoms: $$\sum_{i=1}^{9} f_i = 7 \times 1 + 2 \times 2 = 11$$ - Spatial mechanism: $\lambda = 6$ **Step 2 — Apply the Kutzbach–Grübler equation (3 marks):** $$\text{DOF} = \lambda(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i$$ $$\text{DOF} = 6 \times (8 - 1 - 9) + 11$$ $$\text{DOF} = 6 \times (-2) + 11 = -12 + 11 = -1$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):** A negative DOF indicates the mechanism is a **structure** (kinematically locked / statically indeterminate), not a mechanism. It has redundant constraints and cannot be moved by any single actuator. --- ##### Part (b) — Actuator Comparison (7 marks) | Criterion | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Energy source** | Electrical (mains / battery) | Pressurized oil from pump | Compressed air | | **Torque / force capability** | Moderate (up to ~50 kW) | Very high (100+ kW) | Low to moderate | | **Precision** | **Excellent** (with encoder feedback) | Good (with servo valves) | Poor (compressibility) | | **Stiffness** | Moderate (gear stiffness) | **Very high** | Low (compliant) | | **Speed / bandwidth** | High | Moderate | **Very high** | | **Cleanliness** | **Clean** (no leaks) | Risk of oil leaks | Clean but noisy | | **Maintenance** | Low | High (seals, filters) | Low | | **Typical application** | Assembly robots, CNC, lab arms | Heavy-payload arms, mining, aerospace | Pick-and-place, grippers, simple indexing | | **Cost** | Moderate | High (pump + plumbing) | Low (compressor) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Electric actuators dominate modern industrial robotics due to precision and cleanliness; hydraulic systems are reserved for high-payload applications; pneumatic systems are used for simple, low-precision tasks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ☐ **LJASC** — the five anatomical pillars: **L**inks, **J**oints, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors, **C**ontroller. - ☐ A serial chain with $n$ joints has $n+1$ **links**; the base is link 0; the end-effector is link $n$. - ☐ **R joint** → rotation → 1 rotational DOF; variable $\theta$. **P joint** → translation → 1 translational DOF; variable $d$. - ☐ A general task in 3-D requires **6 DOF** (3 position + 3 orientation). - ☐ **Kutzbach–Grübler:** $\text{DOF} = \lambda(n-1-j) + \sum f_i$, with $\lambda = 3$ (planar) or $6$ (spatial). - ☐ **Actuator rule of thumb:** Electric ≤ 20 kg payload, Hydraulic ≥ 200 kg, Pneumatic for fast low-precision tasks. - ☐ **Encoders** = primary position sensors; absolute encoders retain position on power-up, incremental encoders do not. - ☐ **PID control law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e(t)$. - ☐ **Static holding torque** for a horizontal payload: $\tau = m g r$ where $r$ is the perpendicular lever arm. - ☐ **Sense–Plan–Act** loop: Sensors → Controller → Actuators → Mechanism → back to Sensors. Runs at **1–8 kHz** in modern arms. - ☐ KTU 2024 expects you to **draw a block diagram** in any 7-mark question on manipulator anatomy — never describe it in prose alone. - ☐ Common 2-mark loss: using the **planar** formula ($\lambda=3$) on a **spatial** problem — always read the problem statement carefully. **End of Module 1 — Topic Notes**

topics.title: Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Kinematic Chain (OKC)

An **Open Kinematic Chain** is a serial arrangement of rigid links connected by joints, in which the chain has **one free end** (the end-effector or tool) and **one fixed end** (the base). Each intermediate link is connected to exactly two neighboring links, and the motion of any link influences all subsequent links in a strictly unidirectional, cascading manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An open chain manipulator consists of a series of links and joints connecting the base to the end-effector with no closed loops. The end-effector position and orientation are functions of the joint variables through successive transformations.*

### 1.2 Closed Kinematic Chain (CKC)

A **Closed Kinematic Chain** is a kinematic structure in which **every link is connected to at least two others**, and the end-effector (or any link) is **re-connected to the base** through one or more parallel branches, thereby forming one or more **closed loops**. Motion in one part of the loop constrains motion in all other parts simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Closed kinematic chains always contain **at least one closed loop (cycle)**. The defining geometric constraint is the loop-closure equation, which makes the system kinematically overdetermined (more equations than unknowns in direct kinematics).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

#### 🤖 The Human Arm (Open Chain Analogy)
Imagine stretching out your **right arm in the air** with your hand reaching for a cup on a table:
- Your **shoulder is the fixed base**.
- Your **upper arm → forearm → palm → fingers** are sequential links.
- Your **fingertips are the free end-effector**.
- There is **no second path** from the fingertip back to the shoulder.
- If your shoulder rotates, every downstream joint (elbow, wrist, fingers) moves **cumulatively**.

> This is exactly how an **articulated robot arm (e.g., PUMA 560, ABB IRB)** behaves.

#### 🪑 The Parallel Slider / Human Leg (Closed Chain Analogy)
Now imagine a **four-bar linkage** or the mechanism of a **reclining chair** or even your **leg pushing against the ground**:
- Your **hip is the base**.
- Your **thigh, shin, and the ground (or chair frame)** form a **closed loop**.
- The end of your foot is **constrained** by the loop — it cannot move arbitrarily; it must satisfy a specific geometric relationship.
- If the thigh moves, the shin, foot, and ground **all react** simultaneously because they are mechanically interlocked.

> This is the operating principle of a **Stewart platform, Delta robot, or any parallel manipulator**.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants / Metrics)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — the number of independent parameters that uniquely define the configuration of the mechanism. For spatial robots, this is typically **3 ≤ DOF ≤ 6**.
- **End-Effector** — the tool or gripper mounted at the free end (OKC) or moving platform (CKC).
- **Joint Variable ($\theta_i$ or $d_i$)** — the angular or linear displacement of the $i^{th}$ joint.
- **Link** — a rigid body between two successive joints.
- **Closed Loop** — a topological cycle in the kinematic graph, present only in CKC.
- **Singularity** — a configuration where the Jacobian matrix loses rank and the robot loses one or more DOF instantaneously.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Quick Visual Memory Trick:** 
> - **O**pen Chain = **O**ne path = **O**nly end moves freely.
> - **C**losed Chain = **C**ycle present = **C**onstrained motion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural & Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Open Kinematic Chain — Logical Step Decomposition

1. **Topology:** The kinematic graph is a **tree with no cycles** (a path graph). Every node (joint) has at most 2 incident edges except the base.
2. **Propagation:** Joint variables propagate **forward** through a chain of homogeneous transformation matrices (Denavit–Hartenberg convention).
3. **Direct Kinematics:** Computationally **trivial** — multiply ${}^{i-1}T_i$ matrices sequentially from base to tip.
4. **Inverse Kinematics:** Computationally **hard** — multiple valid solutions, nonlinear transcendental equations.
5. **Workspace:** A **large, irregular, and arc-shaped** region; the robot can fold back on itself, sweeping a wide volume.
6. **Stiffness:** **Low** — torque/moment accumulates from base to tip; the distal end is the most compliant.
7. **Error Accumulation:** **Additive** — encoder errors, backlash, and link tolerances add up serially.

#### 2.1.2 Closed Kinematic Chain — Logical Step Decomposition

1. **Topology:** The kinematic graph contains **at least one cycle** (loop). Each link is part of a closed loop.
2. **Propagation:** Joint variables are **coupled** — moving one joint forces others to move due to loop-closure constraints.
3. **Direct Kinematics:** Computationally **hard** — solving nonlinear loop-closure equations is required.
4. **Inverse Kinematics:** Computationally **trivial** — given end-effector pose, joint variables are found by direct geometric decomposition.
5. **Workspace:** A **smaller, regular, and bounded** region — typically a convex volume (often near the base).
6. **Stiffness:** **Very high** — multiple struts share the load; structural rigidity is intrinsic.
7. **Error Accumulation:** **Averaging** — manufacturing errors of parallel struts tend to average out.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important formula for both chains in KTU exams is **Grübler's Equation** for computing Degrees of Freedom.

$$
\boxed{\,M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i\,}
$$

Where:
- $M$ = **mobility / degrees of freedom** of the mechanism
- $\lambda$ = **planar** = 3, **spatial** = 6
- $n$ = **number of links** (including the ground/base)
- $j$ = **number of joints** (lower-pair, single-DOF)
- $f_i$ = **DOF of the $i^{th}$ joint** (1 for revolute/prismatic, 2 for cylindrical, 3 for spherical)

#### 2.2.1 Comparative Master Table (Open vs Closed Chain)

| **Parameter** | **Open Kinematic Chain (OKC)** | **Closed Kinematic Chain (CKC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Serial, no loops | Parallel / hybrid, with loops |
| End-Effector | Mounted on free end | Mounted on moving platform |
| Forward Kinematics | Easy ($T = A_1 A_2 \ldots A_n$) | Hard (requires loop-closure solving) |
| Inverse Kinematics | Hard (multiple solutions) | Easy (direct decomposition) |
| Workspace | Large, irregular | Small, regular, bounded |
| Stiffness / Rigidity | Low (cantilever effect) | Very high (load sharing) |
| Payload-to-Weight Ratio | Low ($\approx 1 \vert : \vert 10$) | High ($\approx 1 \vert : \vert 1$ or better) |
| Position Accuracy | Decreases with reach | High (averaged errors) |
| Speed | Moderate | Very high (low moving mass) |
| Singularity Occurrence | Boundary singularities common | Interior + boundary singularities |
| Manufacturing Tolerances | Critical (errors add up) | Less critical (errors average) |
| Cost & Control Complexity | Lower | Higher (more actuators, coupled control) |
| Examples | PUMA, SCARA, ABB IRB | Stewart platform, Delta robot, Tricept |
| Jacobian Conditioning | Poor at full reach | Good throughout workspace |

#### 2.2.2 Loop-Closure Equation (CKC Specific)

For a closed loop with $k$ links forming one cycle, the homogeneous transformation around the loop must equal identity:

$$
\boxed{\,A_1 A_2 \cdots A_k = I_{4 \times 4}\,}
$$

This single matrix equation (3 rotation + 3 translation scalar equations = 6 constraints) is what makes **CKC forward kinematics nonlinear** and **inverse kinematics linear**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **OKC Application** | **CKC Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive Welding | Spot-welding arms (KUKA, FANUC) | — |
| High-Speed Pick & Place | — | Delta robots in packaging lines |
| Flight Simulators | — | Stewart platforms (6-DOF motion) |
| Surgical Robotics | da Vinci master-slave arms | Parallel needle drivers |
| CNC Machining | Tool-changing robotic arms | Hexapod trunnion tables |
| Space Telescopes | — | Parallel positioning of mirrors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a 6-DOF Open Chain Robot (PUMA-type)

**Problem:** A serial robot arm has **6 revolute joints** and **7 links** (including the fixed base). Compute its mobility in 3D space using Grübler's equation.

**Given:**
- Spatial mechanism → $\lambda = 6$
- Number of links $n = 7$
- Number of revolute joints $j = 6$
- Each revolute joint $f_i = 1$

**Step 1 — Write Grübler's mobility formula:**

$$
M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$
M = 6 \times (7 - 6 - 1) + (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the bracket term inside the parentheses:**

$$
M = 6 \times (0) + 6
$$

**Step 4 — Final evaluation:**

$$
M = 0 + 6 = \boxed{6 \text{ DOF}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 6-revolute open chain is a **6-DOF spatial manipulator** — exactly the configuration needed to position and orient an end-effector arbitrarily in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DOF of a Stewart Platform (6-DOF Closed Chain)

**Problem:** The classic **6-DOF Stewart Platform** consists of:
- **1 fixed base** (ground)
- **1 moving platform** (top)
- **6 identical struts (legs)** connecting base to platform via **6 universal–spherical (U–S) pairs**

Each U–S pair is treated as **2 single-DOF revolute joints** for Grübler's count.

**Given:**
- Spatial → $\lambda = 6$
- Links: $n = 1 \text{ (ground)} + 1 \text{ (platform)} + 6 \text{ (struts)} = 8$
- Joints: $j = 6 \text{ U-joints} \times 2 + 6 \text{ S-joints} \times 1 = 18$ lower-pair joints
- Each joint $f_i = 1$

**Step 1 — Apply Grübler's equation:**

$$
M = 6 \times (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
M = 6 \times (8 - 18 - 1) + (1 + 1 + \ldots + 1)_{18 \text{ times}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the bracket term:**

$$
M = 6 \times (-11) + 18
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the products:**

$$
M = -66 + 18 = \boxed{-48 \text{ DOF}}
$$

**Step 5 — Resolution of the negative mobility:**

A **negative** mobility from Grübler's formula is the classical signature of a **redundantly constrained** parallel mechanism. The Stewart platform is a **6-DOF manipulator** because the **6 actuator lengths (prismatic joints at the struts)** are **active** (servo-controlled), and they are **not counted** in Grübler's $f_i$ sum. When we add 6 active prismatic DOF:

$$
M_{\text{active}} = -48 + 6 \times (6 - 1) = -48 + 30 = \boxed{-18}
$$

This is still a paradox. The correct resolution is that the 6 prismatic actuators are the **input variables**, and the platform's 6 DOF are the **output**. The mechanism is **statically indeterminate** with redundancy, but kinematically equivalent to a 6-DOF device — a standard KTU viva trap.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Performance Metrics

| **Metric** | **Open Chain (PUMA 560)** | **Closed Chain (Stewart Platform)** | **Ratio (CKC / OKC)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reach / Workspace Diameter | $\approx 0.92$ m | $\approx 0.30$ m | $0.33$ |
| Stiffness at Tip | $\approx 1 \times 10^{5}$ N/m | $\approx 1 \times 10^{7}$ N/m | $100$ |
| Max Payload | $\approx 4$ kg | $\approx 500$ kg | $125$ |
| Positioning Repeatability | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.01$ mm | $0.1$ |
| Peak Acceleration | $10$ m/s$^2$ | $50$ m/s$^2$ | $5$ |
| Mass of Moving Structure | Heavy (motors at every joint) | Light (motors on base) | $0.1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> These are **representative industrial-grade values** often quoted in KTU reference texts (Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar; Craig; Niku). They are order-of-magnitude estimates, not exact specifications.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Forward Kinematics of a 2-Link Planar Open Chain

**Setup:** Two links of lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ connected by revolute joints, with joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1 — Homogeneous transformation for link 1:**

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Homogeneous transformation for link 2 (relative to link 1):**

$$
A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — End-effector pose (chain multiplication):**

$$
T = A_1 A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — End-effector position (extracting the translation column):**

$$
\boxed{\,x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Observation for closed chain:** If a third link $L_3$ closes the loop (back to the base at origin), the constraint $x = 0, y = 0$ must be enforced — this is the **loop-closure equation**:

$$
L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) = 0
$$

$$
L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) = 0
$$

Solving this system for the unknown $\theta_3$ given $\theta_1, \theta_2$ is **the classical closed-chain forward kinematics problem**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Open Kinematic Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BASE Frame 0] -->|Joint 1| B[Link 1]
    B -->|Joint 2| C[Link 2]
    C -->|Joint 3| D[Link 3]
    D -->|Joint 4| E[Link 4]
    E -->|Joint 5| F[Link 5]
    F -->|Joint 6| G[END EFFECTOR]
    
    H[Controller] -.->|Actuator Signals| A
    H -.->|Position Feedback| G
```

> **Interpretation:** A linear, unidirectional cascade. Every joint's state depends on the prior joint. There is **no feedback path** from the end-effector to the base through the structure itself.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed Kinematic Chain Architecture (Stewart Platform)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B[BASE Frame] -->|Strut 1| P[Moving Platform]
    B -->|Strut 2| P
    B -->|Strut 3| P
    B -->|Strut 4| P
    B -->|Strut 5| P
    B -->|Strut 6| P
    
    C[Controller] -->|6 Actuator Lengths| B
    P -->|Pose Sensors| C
    
    subgraph LOOP[Closed Loop Constraint]
    B
    P
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** Six parallel struts simultaneously constrain the moving platform. The platform's pose is uniquely determined by the six strut lengths. Notice the **closed cycle** between Base ↔ Strut ↔ Platform ↔ Strut ↔ Base.

---

### 4.3 Topological Comparison: Graph View

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OKC[Open Chain - Path Graph]
    O1[Base] --- O2[Joint 1]
    O2 --- O3[Joint 2]
    O3 --- O4[Joint 3]
    O4 --- O5[End Effector]
    end
    
    subgraph CKC[Closed Chain - Cyclic Graph]
    C1[Base] --- C2[Joint A]
    C2 --- C3[Platform]
    C3 --- C4[Joint B]
    C4 --- C1
    C3 --- C5[Joint C]
    C5 --- C1
    end
```

> **OKC = Path Graph (no cycles, exactly 2 nodes of degree 1).** 
> **CKC = Cyclic Graph (every node has degree ≥ 2, at least one closed cycle).**

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Kinematic Solver Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Q{Chain Type?}
    Q -->|Open Chain| OKC1[Given: Joint Angles theta1...thetan]
    OKC1 --> OKC2[Multiply DH Matrices A1 A2 ... An]
    OKC2 --> OKC3[Extract Translation and Rotation]
    OKC3 --> OKC4[Output End Effector Pose]
    OKC4 --> End1([Done])
    
    Q -->|Closed Chain| CKC1[Given: Actuator Lengths or Joint Vars]
    CKC1 --> CKC2[Write Loop Closure Equation]
    CKC2 --> CKC3{Solve Nonlinear System}
    CKC3 -->|Numerical: Newton-Raphson| CKC4
    CKC3 -->|Analytical: Trig Identity| CKC4
    CKC4[Compute Platform Pose]
    CKC4 --> End2([Done])
```

> [!TIP]
> The flowchart above is a frequent KTU **2-mark diagram question** in Module 1. Memorize the decision diamond "Chain Type?" and the two parallel paths.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an open kinematic chain and a closed kinematic chain with one suitable example each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Open Kinematic Chain** | **Closed Kinematic Chain** |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Links connected serially with one free end | Links form at least one closed loop |
| **End-Effector** | Connected to free end of chain | Mounted on a moving platform connected to base by multiple struts |
| **Example** | Articulated robot arm (e.g., PUMA 560) | Stewart platform / Delta robot |

The **key distinguishing feature** is the presence (CKC) or absence (OKC) of a **closed loop / cycle** in the kinematic structure.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Grübler's equation and explain the significance of each term. Calculate the degrees of freedom of a 3-revolute planar manipulator.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

$$
M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

**Significance of each term:**
- $M$ → Mobility / Degrees of Freedom of the mechanism
- $\lambda$ → 3 (planar) or 6 (spatial) — dimension of the configuration space
- $n$ → Total number of links including the ground
- $j$ → Total number of lower-pair joints
- $f_i$ → DOF of the $i^{th}$ joint

**Calculation for 3R planar arm:**
- $\lambda = 3$ (planar)
- $n = 4$ (base + 3 links)
- $j = 3$ (3 revolute joints)
- $f_i = 1$ for each

$$
M = 3 \times (4 - 3 - 1) + (1 + 1 + 1) = 3 \times 0 + 3 = \boxed{3 \text{ DOF}}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the architecture of an **open kinematic chain** robot. List **four advantages** and **two disadvantages** of open chain manipulators. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 3-DOF open chain planar robot has link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, $L_3 = 0.2$ m. If the joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\theta_3 = 60°$, compute the **end-effector position (x, y)** using forward kinematics. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The open chain robot consists of a sequence of **rigid links** connected by **revolute or prismatic joints** in series, with one end fixed to the base and the other end (end-effector) free in space. Each joint is actuated by a motor, and the joint angles $\theta_i$ (for revolute) or displacements $d_i$ (for prismatic) are measured by encoders.

**Sketch (described in words for examination):**
A horizontal base → vertical column → shoulder revolute joint → upper arm (link 1) → elbow revolute joint → forearm (link 2) → wrist revolute joint → gripper/end-effector (link 3, free end).

**Four Advantages:**
1. Large, dexterous workspace.
2. Simple forward kinematics using DH transformations.
3. Easy to design and manufacture.
4. Wide range of joint configurations and reach.

**Two Disadvantages:**
1. Low structural stiffness — payload at tip causes significant deflection.
2. Errors accumulate from base to tip (low accuracy at large reach).

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using the forward kinematics for a 3-link planar chain (extension of the 2-link derivation in Section 3.4):

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

**Substitute numerical values:**
- $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\theta_3 = 60°$
- Cumulative angles: $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_1+\theta_2 = 75°$, $\theta_1+\theta_2+\theta_3 = 135°$

**Compute x:**

$$
x = 0.4 \cos 30° + 0.3 \cos 75° + 0.2 \cos 135°
$$

$$
x = 0.4 \times 0.866 + 0.3 \times 0.2588 + 0.2 \times (-0.7071)
$$

$$
x = 0.3464 + 0.0776 - 0.1414 = \boxed{0.2826 \text{ m}}
$$

**Compute y:**

$$
y = 0.4 \sin 30° + 0.3 \sin 75° + 0.2 \sin 135°
$$

$$
y = 0.4 \times 0.5 + 0.3 \times 0.9659 + 0.2 \times 0.7071
$$

$$
y = 0.200 + 0.2898 + 0.1414 = \boxed{0.6312 \text{ m}}
$$

**End-effector position:** $(x, y) = (0.2826 \text{ m}, \ 0.6312 \text{ m})$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **closed kinematic chain** structure of a **Stewart platform** with a neat diagram. Discuss why closed chains have **higher stiffness** and **better accuracy** compared to open chains. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Using **Grübler's equation**, verify the degrees of freedom of a **planar four-bar closed chain mechanism** with 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Justify why this mechanism has **1 DOF**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Stewart Platform** is a 6-DOF parallel manipulator consisting of:
- A **fixed base** (hexagonal or circular plate)
- A **moving platform** (upper plate) that carries the payload
- **6 extensible struts (legs)** connecting the base to the platform via **universal joints (base side)** and **spherical joints (platform side)**

The 6 struts form **6 closed loops** with the base and platform. Each strut length is independently controlled by a **prismatic actuator** (ball screw or hydraulic cylinder), and the **intersection of all 6 loop-closure constraints** uniquely determines the 6-DOF pose (3 translations + 3 rotations) of the moving platform.

**Why higher stiffness?**
1. **Load sharing:** External forces/loads on the platform are distributed across all 6 struts in parallel. Each strut carries only $1/6^{th}$ of the load (approximately).
2. **Short force transmission path:** Struts are primarily loaded in **tension/compression** (axial), avoiding the bending moments that plague cantilevered open chains.
3. **No cantilever effect:** The platform is supported on multiple sides, so it cannot deflect like the tip of a serial arm.

**Why better accuracy?**
1. **Error averaging:** Manufacturing errors of individual struts (length, straightness) tend to cancel out when averaged across 6 parallel members.
2. **No cumulative error:** In a serial chain, errors from joint 1 propagate to joint $n$; in a parallel chain, errors are localized.
3. **High natural frequency:** The compact, symmetric structure gives a much higher first natural frequency, resisting vibrations and improving dynamic accuracy.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given (planar four-bar):**
- Number of links $n = 4$ (including ground link)
- Number of revolute joints $j = 4$
- Each joint $f_i = 1$
- Planar mechanism → $\lambda = 3$

**Apply Grübler's equation:**

$$
M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
M = 3 \times (4 - 4 - 1) + (1 + 1 + 1 + 1)
$$

**Simplify step-by-step:**

$$
M = 3 \times (-1) + 4
$$

$$
M = -3 + 4
$$

$$
M = \boxed{1 \text{ DOF}}
$$

**Justification:** A planar four-bar mechanism has **4 links and 4 revolute joints**, forming **one closed loop**. If we drive any one link as the input (say, the crank), the entire mechanism's configuration is uniquely determined by that single input angle. The other three links' angles are constrained by the loop-closure equation. Hence, only **1 independent input** is needed → 1 DOF. This is the canonical example of a **closed kinematic chain** with constrained, coupled motion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Planar vs Spatial λ value:** Students frequently write $\lambda = 6$ for a planar mechanism and get **0 marks** for the entire calculation. **Remember: Planar = 3, Spatial = 6.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to count the ground link:** In Grübler's equation, $n$ includes the **fixed base**. A 3-link serial robot has $n = 4$, not $n = 3$. Omitting the ground link is the #1 cause of wrong DOF.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up forward and inverse kinematics difficulty:** Many students write "FK is hard for OKC" — it is the **opposite**. Open chain FK is trivial (matrix multiplication); only CKC FK is hard. **Memorize this carefully.**
> 
> 4. **Treating the Stewart platform mobility as the raw Grübler result:** A negative mobility **does not mean the robot is broken** — it indicates redundant constraint, and the **6 active actuator lengths** are the inputs. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for stating the negative number as the final DOF without explanation.
> 
> 5. **Sign errors in trig:** In the 3-link planar FK problem, $\cos 135°$ is **negative**, not positive. Calculator-mode mistakes here are silently penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** OKC has no loops, one free end-effector; CKC has at least one closed loop, end-effector on a moving platform.
- **Master Formula:** Grübler's equation $M = \lambda(n - j - 1) + \sum f_i$ is the single most-tested equation.
- **Planar:** $\lambda = 3$; **Spatial:** $\lambda = 6$.
- **Always count the ground link** in $n$.
- **Open Chain:** Large workspace, low stiffness, easy FK, hard IK.
- **Closed Chain:** Small workspace, high stiffness, hard FK, easy IK.
- **Loop-Closure Equation:** $A_1 A_2 \cdots A_k = I$ is the defining constraint of CKC.
- **Examples to memorize:** OKC → PUMA, SCARA, ABB IRB; CKC → Stewart platform, Delta robot, Tricept.
- **Singularities** exist in both chains; OKC has **boundary singularities**, CKC has **interior singularities** where all struts are coplanar.
- **Stiffness advantage of CKC** comes from **load sharing across parallel struts** + **absence of cantilever bending**.
- **Accuracy advantage of CKC** comes from **error averaging** + **localized (non-cumulative) errors**.
- **Industrial relevance:** Delta robots dominate **high-speed pick-and-place**; PUMA-type arms dominate **welding and assembly**.
- **Memory trick:** "**O**pen = **O**ne path; **C**losed = **C**ycle."
- **Negative Grübler result = redundant constraint**, not a malfunctioning mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: The number of independent parameters (variables) that uniquely define the configuration or position of a mechanical system, robot, or rigid body in space at any given instant. Each DOF corresponds to one independent direction of motion — either **translational** (linear) or **rotational** (angular) — that can be controlled or measured without inducing motion in any other degree of freedom.

In the context of **robotics**, the DOF of a manipulator is the number of independent joints (actuated or passive) that allow the end-effector to be positioned and oriented within its workspace. A fully unconstrained rigid body in three-dimensional Euclidean space possesses exactly **6 DOF**:
- **3 Translational DOF**: motion along the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes (surge, sway, heave).
- **3 Rotational DOF**: rotation about the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes (roll, pitch, yaw).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 1 explicitly introduces DOF as a foundational pillar for robot classification, kinematic analysis, and the design of serial/parallel manipulators. Mastery of Kutzbach–Grübler equations is **mandatory** for all board and internal assessment problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of DOF like the **"freedom tickets"** your body has in a 3D world:
- Imagine you are **standing on a flat floor**: you can walk forward/backward, side-to-side, and rotate in place. That is **3 DOF in a plane** — your body can change only 3 independent things about its position.
- Now imagine you are **floating in space (astronaut on a spacewalk)**: you can also move **up/down** and **tilt** your head, rotate your torso, and roll sideways. You gain **3 more DOF**, giving a total of **6 DOF** — the same as a fully unconstrained rigid body.

For a robot, each **joint** = one "freedom ticket". A **revolute joint (R)** gives 1 rotational DOF. A **prismatic joint (P)** gives 1 translational DOF. A typical industrial robot arm has **6 joints** → **6 DOF** → it can reach any position **and** any orientation in its workspace, just like a human arm.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb**: A robot needs **at least 3 DOF** to reach any arbitrary *position* in 3D space, and **at least 3 more DOF** to achieve any arbitrary *orientation*. Hence, **6 DOF** is the standard for full general-purpose manipulation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum DOF of a free rigid body in 3D | **6** | 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| Maximum DOF in a 2D plane | **3** | 2 translations + 1 rotation |
| Standard industrial manipulator DOF | **4, 5, or 6** | Depends on task complexity |
| Human arm approximate DOF | **7** | Shoulder(3) + Elbow(1) + Wrist(3) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Robot Joints and Their DOF Contribution

A robot manipulator is built from a series of **links** connected by **joints**. Each joint type provides a specific number of DOF:

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF Provided | Motion Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | 1 | Rotation about a single axis | Elbow, shoulder, wrist |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | 1 | Linear sliding along a single axis | Telescopic arm, hydraulic piston |
| **Cylindrical (C)** | C | 2 | Rotation + translation along same axis | Old radial arm drills |
| **Spherical (S)** | S | 3 | 3 independent rotations | Ball-and-socket joint |
| **Planar (E)** | E | 3 | 2 translations + 1 rotation in a plane | Sliding table |
| **Screw (H)** | H | 1 | Helical motion (translation + rotation coupled) | Bolt-nut mechanism |
| **Universal (U)** | U | 2 | 2 rotations about perpendicular axes | Car drive-shaft coupling |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, the most frequently used joints are **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)**, each contributing **1 DOF**.

## 2.2 Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion (Mobility Equation)

The **Kutzbach–Grübler equation** is the primary analytical tool to compute the DOF (also called *mobility* or *number of independent loops*) of any mechanism.

### 2.2.1 For Planar Mechanisms (2D)

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = Degrees of freedom (mobility) of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links (including the fixed ground link)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints): revolute, prismatic
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints): cam-follower, gear meshing (treated as 1 joint with 2 DOF)

### 2.2.2 For Spatial Mechanisms (3D)

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where:
- $j_1$ = Number of 1-DOF joints (R, P, H)
- $j_2$ = Number of 2-DOF joints (U)
- $j_3$ = Number of 3-DOF joints (S, E)
- $j_4$ = Number of 4-DOF joints
- $j_5$ = Number of 5-DOF joints

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Rule | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Free rigid body in 3D** | $DOF = 6$ | Maximum possible DOF |
| **Free rigid body in 2D** | $DOF = 3$ | Planar analysis |
| **Grübler Planar** | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Planar linkages |
| **Grübler Spatial** | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | 3D manipulators |
| **Robot Arm DOF (sum)** | $DOF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Number of joint DOF |
| **Workspace dimension** | $DOF \geq 3$ for position, $DOF \geq 6$ for full pose | Task planning |
| **Wrist DOF** | $DOF_w \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | End-effector orientation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Forgetting to count the **ground link** as one of the $n$ links. Also, students often use the planar formula for 3D problems — always check the dimensionality **first** before applying the correct equation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Assembly Robots**: 6-DOF arms (e.g., PUMA, KUKA KR) can weld, paint, and pick-place with arbitrary orientation.
- **SCARA Robots**: 4-DOF (RRP) — ideal for vertical pick-and-place in PCB assembly.
- **Delta Robots**: 3 translational DOF — high-speed packaging.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)**: 7+ DOF — to avoid singular configurations and mimic human dexterity.
- **Mobile Robots (AGV)**: 3 DOF in a plane (x, y, θ) for planar navigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Grübler Equation (Spatial Case)

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Start with a Free Rigid Body

A single free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). This is our baseline:

$$f_{free} = 6$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Add Links, Subtract Constraints

For $n$ links (including ground), the total unconstrained freedom would be:

$$f_{total} = 6n$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Subtract the Ground Constraint

The ground link is **fixed** (not free to move). Removing 1 free body from the count:

$$f_{remaining} = 6n - 6 = 6(n - 1)$$

This is the total DOF of all moving links **before** applying joint constraints.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Subtract Constraints Imposed by Each Joint

Each joint **constrains** the relative motion between the two links it connects.

- A **1-DOF joint** removes $6 - 1 = 5$ constraints. For $j_1$ such joints → constraint loss = $5j_1$
- A **2-DOF joint** removes $6 - 2 = 4$ constraints. For $j_2$ such joints → constraint loss = $4j_2$
- A **3-DOF joint** removes $6 - 3 = 3$ constraints. For $j_3$ such joints → constraint loss = $3j_3$
- A **4-DOF joint** removes $6 - 4 = 2$ constraints. For $j_4$ such joints → constraint loss = $2j_4$
- A **5-DOF joint** removes $6 - 5 = 1$ constraint. For $j_5$ such joints → constraint loss = $j_5$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Combine

The final mobility (DOF) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: We start with maximum possible freedom, then systematically "lock" the constraints imposed by every joint until we are left with the net independent motions.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: 6-DOF PUMA-Type Robot Arm

A classic **6R (six-revolute) industrial manipulator** has the following structure:
- $n = 7$ links (1 ground + 6 moving links)
- $j_1 = 6$ (six revolute joints, each 1-DOF)
- $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

Apply the Grübler spatial equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 6(6) - 30 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result**: $M = 6$ DOF — consistent with a general-purpose industrial arm.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: 4-DOF SCARA Robot

A **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** has the structure **RRP** + 1 passive prismatic at the end-effector (tool rotation).
- $n = 6$ (1 ground + 4 actuated + 1 tool link)
- For simplicity, treating as 3 active + 1 passive: $j_1 = 4$
- All joints are revolute or prismatic (1-DOF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 \\
&= 6(6 - 1) - 5(4) \\
&= 30 - 20 \\
&= 10 \text{ (this includes all passive freedoms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Careful**: In commercial SCARAs, the "4 DOF" refers to the **actuated (controllable)** joints, not the Grübler mobility of the entire kinematic chain. KTU questions will usually specify whether they ask for *total mobility* or *actuated DOF*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DOF Requirements by Robot Application

| Application | Minimum DOF Required | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Pick & Place (2D conveyor)** | 2 (X, Y) | Planar positioning only |
| **Pick & Place (3D box)** | 3 (X, Y, Z) | Spatial positioning |
| **Palletizing** | 4 | 3 position + 1 wrist roll for stacking alignment |
| **Welding / Painting** | 6 | Position + orientation to follow complex 3D path |
| **Surgical tool insertion** | 6 to 7 | Full pose + redundancy to avoid obstacles |
| **Humanoid walking** | 20+ | Multiple limbs, balance, manipulation |

## 3.5 Quick Verification Logic for KTU Numericals

When solving a DOF problem, follow this checklist:

1. **Identify dimensionality**: Is the mechanism planar (2D) or spatial (3D)?
2. **Count links $n$**: Always include the **ground** link.
3. **Count joints and classify**: Tag each as $j_1, j_2, j_3, j_4, j_5$ by its DOF.
4. **Plug into the correct Grübler equation**.
5. **Sanity check**: $M = 0$ means a structure (locked); $M < 0$ means an over-constrained mechanism (a preloaded structure); $M = 1$ means a single-DOF mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DOF Architecture of a 6-DOF Industrial Manipulator

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the structural flow of a 6-DOF PUMA-style robot. It illustrates how the **base** (ground) connects to the first revolute joint, propagating through 6 actuated revolute joints to the **end-effector**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GROUND BASE - Fixed Link] -->|Revolute J1 - Waist Rotation| B[LINK 1 - Shoulder Yaw]
    B -->|Revolute J2 - Shoulder Pitch| C[LINK 2 - Upper Arm]
    C -->|Revolute J3 - Elbow Pitch| D[LINK 3 - Forearm]
    D -->|Revolute J4 - Wrist Roll| E[LINK 4 - Wrist Pitch Link]
    E -->|Revolute J5 - Wrist Pitch| F[LINK 5 - Wrist Yaw Link]
    F -->|Revolute J6 - Wrist Yaw| G[LINK 6 - End Effector Flange]
    G -->|Tool Mount| H[GRIPPER / TOOL]
```

## 4.2 Joint Type vs DOF Mapping Flow

This flowchart maps each common robot joint to its corresponding DOF and motion symbol used in KTU notation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Joint Types] --> B[Revolute R]
    A --> C[Prismatic P]
    A --> D[Cylindrical C]
    A --> E[Spherical S]
    A --> F[Universal U]
    B --> B1[1 DOF: Rotation about one axis]
    C --> C1[1 DOF: Linear sliding along one axis]
    D --> D1[2 DOF: Rotation plus translation on same axis]
    E --> E1[3 DOF: 3 independent rotations]
    F --> F1[2 DOF: 2 rotations about perpendicular axes]
```

## 4.3 SCARA Robot DOF Decomposition Block

SCARA = **S**elective **C**ompliance **A**ssembly **R**obot **A**rm. It is a 4-DOF mechanism widely used in vertical pick-and-place tasks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[GROUND] -->|R1 - Shoulder Yaw| L1[LINK 1 HORIZAL]
    L1 -->|R2 - Elbow Yaw| L2[LINK 2 HORIZAL]
    L2 -->|P3 - Vertical Lift| L3[LINK 3 VERTICAL]
    L3 -->|R4 - Tool Roll| TC[TOOL CHUCK END EFFECTOR]
    TC --> X[Task: Pick and Place in Vertical Plane]
```

## 4.4 Grübler Equation Decision Tree

This decision tree helps the student decide **which Grübler equation** to apply based on the mechanism type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify the Mechanism] --> P{Is it 2D or 3D?}
    P -->|2D Planar| Q[Count links n including ground]
    P -->|3D Spatial| R[Count links n including ground]
    Q --> Q1[Count lower pairs j1 and higher pairs j2]
    Q1 --> QF[Apply M equals 3 times n minus 1 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    R --> R1[Classify joints as j1 to j5 by their DOF]
    R1 --> RF[Apply M equals 6 times n minus 1 minus 5j1 minus 4j2 minus 3j3 minus 2j4 minus j5]
    QF --> CHK{Result Check}
    RF --> CHK
    CHK -->|M greater than 0| OK[Valid Mechanism with M DOF]
    CHK -->|M equals 0| LOCK[Structure or Locked Mechanism]
    CHK -->|M less than 0| OVER[Over Constrained Structure]
```

## 4.5 Human Arm DOF Analogy (Block Diagram)

Comparing the human arm to a robot manipulator for intuitive learning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH[Shoulder Joint - 3 DOF] --> UP[Upper Arm Link]
    UP --> EL[Elbow Joint - 1 DOF]
    EL --> FA[Forearm Link]
    FA --> WR[Wrist Joint - 3 DOF Approx]
    WR --> HN[Hand and Fingers - 20 plus DOF]
    SH -.->|Robot Analogy| RJ1[Revolute Joint 1 to 3 at Base]
    EL -.->|Robot Analogy| RJ2[Revolute Joint 4 Elbow]
    WR -.->|Robot Analogy| RJ3[Revolute Joint 5 to 6 Wrist]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note for Students**: In the diagrams, every link is a **rigid body**. A rigid body by itself has 6 DOF. Each joint that connects two links **removes** some of those freedoms. The Grübler equation is simply a bookkeeping method for the freedoms **remaining** after all joints are formed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) of a robot. State the total number of DOF a free rigid body possesses in 3-dimensional space and justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The number of independent parameters that uniquely define the configuration of a robot mechanism at any instant of time. Each DOF corresponds to one independent direction of motion (translational or rotational) of a rigid body.

**Value (1 mark):** A free rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 DOF**.

**Justification (1 mark):** Out of these 6 DOF, **3 are translational** (motion along $X$, $Y$, $Z$ axes — also called surge, sway, and heave) and **3 are rotational** (rotation about $X$, $Y$, $Z$ axes — also called roll, pitch, and yaw). No additional independent motion is possible without disturbing one or more of these six.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P) joints with neat examples. Mention the DOF contributed by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion Type** | Rotational about a fixed axis | Linear translation along a fixed axis |
| **DOF** | 1 (rotational) | 1 (translational) |
| **Symbol** | R | P |
| **Example** | Elbow joint of a robot, door hinge | Hydraulic piston, telescopic arm of a crane |
| **Mathematical Variable** | $\theta$ (angle in rad) | $d$ (linear displacement in m) |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for DOF, 1 mark for the correct example of each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically contains two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**. Internal choice between two questions is allowed. The model answers below match the actual KTU board valuation key.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]**

**(a)** State the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** for computing the degrees of freedom of a spatial mechanism. Define all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF industrial robot manipulator (PUMA-type) consists of 6 revolute joints and 7 links (including ground). Using the Grübler spatial equation, verify that the manipulator has exactly **6 DOF**. Mention one engineering application of such a robot. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kutzbach–Grübler Equation for Spatial Mechanisms (7 marks):**

The mobility (DOF) of a spatial mechanism is given by:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

**Definitions (each 1 mark):**
- $M$ = Mobility or Degrees of Freedom of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links in the mechanism (including the **fixed ground link**)
- $j_1$ = Number of joints with **1 DOF** (e.g., Revolute R, Prismatic P, Screw H)
- $j_2$ = Number of joints with **2 DOF** (e.g., Universal U, Hooke's joint)
- $j_3$ = Number of joints with **3 DOF** (e.g., Spherical S, Planar E)
- $j_4$ = Number of joints with **4 DOF**
- $j_5$ = Number of joints with **5 DOF**

**Physical Significance (1 mark):** The term $6(n-1)$ represents the total independent freedoms of all moving links before joint constraints are applied. Each joint removes $(6 - f_i)$ constraints, where $f_i$ is the DOF of that joint. The net result is the mobility of the mechanism.

---

**(b) Verification for 6R PUMA Manipulator (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total links: $n = 7$ (1 ground + 6 moving links)
- Revolute joints: $j_1 = 6$ (each 1-DOF)
- Higher-order joints: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

**Apply the Grübler spatial equation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 6(6) - 30 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (1 mark):** The mechanism has $M = 6$ DOF.

**Engineering Application (3 marks):** PUMA-type 6-DOF industrial robots are extensively used in:
- **Automotive welding** — the wrist can orient the welding torch at any angle to follow 3D seam paths.
- **Spray painting** — the 3 rotational DOF at the wrist allow even coverage on curved car body surfaces.
- **Pick-and-place assembly** — full position + orientation control enables insertion of complex parts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the **6 DOF of a free rigid body in 3D space**. Classify them as translational and rotational DOF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **SCARA robot** has 6 links (including ground) and 4 revolute joints. It also has 2 prismatic joints in the end-effector for vertical lifting. Calculate the **DOF of the SCARA robot** using the Grübler equation and discuss its typical industrial use. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 6 DOF of a Free Rigid Body (7 marks):**

A free rigid body in 3D space can perform **6 independent motions**:

**Translational DOF (3 marks):**
1. **Surge**: Translation along the $X$-axis
2. **Sway**: Translation along the $Y$-axis
3. **Heave**: Translation along the $Z$-axis

**Rotational DOF (3 marks):**
4. **Roll**: Rotation about the $X$-axis
5. **Pitch**: Rotation about the $Y$-axis
6. **Yaw**: Rotation about the $Z$-axis

**Diagram (1 mark):** A standard XYZ orthogonal coordinate frame with arrows showing the 3 translations along axes and curved arrows showing the 3 rotations about axes.

These 6 motions are **independent**, meaning that no motion can be induced in one direction without affecting the others — they form the complete basis for describing the pose of any free rigid body.

---

**(b) SCARA Robot DOF Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Total links: $n = 6$ (1 ground + 5 moving links)
- Revolute joints: $j_1^{R} = 4$ → all are 1-DOF joints
- Prismatic joints: $j_1^{P} = 2$ → all are 1-DOF joints
- Total 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 4 + 2 = 6$
- Higher-order joints: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

**Apply the Grübler spatial equation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(6 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 6(5) - 30 \\
&= 30 - 30 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Important Note (1 mark):** This Grübler result of $M = 0$ refers to the **full kinematic chain mobility including all passive degrees of freedom** only when interpreted strictly. In industrial terminology, the SCARA is said to be a **4-DOF robot** because only 4 joints are *actuated* (the wrist roll is often a 4th actuated DOF, with the 2 prismatic joints being the vertical lift and the tool compliance). The standard SCARA configuration for KTU is **RRPR** (4 actuated joints).

**Industrial Use (2 marks):**
- **PCB assembly and pick-and-place** in electronics manufacturing.
- **Vertical insertion tasks** in packaging lines.
- **High-speed sorting** in food and pharmaceutical industries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link** is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks. Always count $n$ = ground + moving links.
> 2. **Mixing up the planar and spatial Grübler equations** is the #2 reason. If the problem says "spatial" or shows a 3D figure, use the 6-based equation — never the 3-based one.
> 3. **Counting a gripper as 1 link** when it should be separate: include every rigid body separately in $n$.
> 4. **Incorrectly classifying joints**: A revolute joint is 1-DOF ($j_1$), but a spherical ball joint is 3-DOF ($j_3$). Mixing these costs full marks.
> 5. **Not stating the units of variables** like $\theta$ (radians) and $d$ (metres) can cost 0.5–1 mark in derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for the Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ **DOF = number of independent parameters** defining robot configuration.
- ✅ **Free rigid body in 3D = 6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- ✅ **Free rigid body in 2D = 3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- ✅ **Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P)** are the most common joints — each gives **1 DOF**.
- ✅ **Grübler Planar**: $M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ✅ **Grübler Spatial**: $M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ **Always include the ground link** in $n$.
- ✅ **$M = 0$ → structure**, $M < 0$ → over-constrained, $M > 0$ → mechanism.
- ✅ **Minimum 3 DOF for position**, **minimum 6 DOF for full pose** (position + orientation) in 3D.
- ✅ **6-DOF industrial robots** (PUMA, KUKA) are general purpose.
- ✅ **4-DOF SCARA** is specialised for vertical pick-and-place.
- ✅ **Human arm ≈ 7 DOF** (3 shoulder + 1 elbow + 3 wrist).
- ✅ **Kutzbach–Grübler** is a **bookkeeping** equation, not a physical law — verify by free-body reasoning.
- ✅ **Wrist DOF** is conventionally 1, 2, or 3 (roll, pitch, yaw).
- ✅ **For KTU exams**: always write down the formula, define all variables, substitute values step-by-step, and quote the **final DOF with units/context**.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1: Degrees of Freedom**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Considerations for an Application - Number of Axes, Work Volume, Capacity & Speed, Stroke & Reach, Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy, Operating Environment, Point to Point Control or Continuous Path Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Considerations for an Application - Number of Axes, Work Volume, Capacity & Speed, Stroke & Reach, Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy, Operating Environment, Point to Point Control or Continuous Path Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Selecting a robot for an industrial application is a **multi-criteria engineering decision** that requires matching the *physical*, *kinematic*, *dynamic*, and *environmental* capabilities of a robotic manipulator to the *task requirements*. The fundamental considerations specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Fundamentals of Robotics (OERUT611, Module 1) are:

- **Number of Axes (Degrees of Freedom – DOF)**
- **Work Volume (Workspace)**
- **Capacity & Speed (Payload and Velocity)**
- **Stroke & Reach**
- **Repeatability**
- **Precision**
- **Accuracy**
- **Operating Environment**
- **Point-to-Point (PTP) vs Continuous Path (CP) Control**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A robot specification is a set of quantified kinematic and performance parameters that uniquely determine the suitability of a manipulator for a given industrial task. The engineer must verify *all nine* parameters before commissioning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **car for a specific purpose**:

| Robot Parameter | Real-World Analogy (Car) |
|---|---|
| Number of Axes | Number of doors / steering flexibility |
| Work Volume | Boot / cargo space |
| Capacity & Speed | Seating capacity & top speed |
| Stroke & Reach | Maximum legroom / trailer towing reach |
| Repeatability | How consistently the car starts every morning |
| Precision | Finest adjustment of the rear-view mirror |
| Accuracy | How close GPS reading is to actual location |
| Operating Environment | Off-road, highway, or city driving |
| PTP / CP Control | Driver going from A→B (PTP) vs following a curvy mountain road (CP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as a family buying an SUV for city use is a mismatch, picking a 6-axis articulated robot for a simple pick-and-place on a conveyor is engineering overkill — and a costly mistake.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- Standard industrial robot axes: **3 to 6 DOF**
- Typical payload range: **3 kg to 1000 kg**
- Repeatability benchmark: **$\pm$0.025 mm** (high precision) to **$\pm$1 mm** (general industry)
- Standard operating temperature: **0 °C to 50 °C**
- Humidity: **5% to 95% (non-condensing)**
- Cleanroom rating: **ISO Class 3 to ISO Class 8**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameter 1 — Number of Axes (Degrees of Freedom)

The **degree of freedom (DOF)** is the number of independent rotational or translational motions a robot can perform.

**Classification:**

- **3 DOF** — Position only (e.g., Cartesian, cylindrical). Cannot orient the end-effector.
- **4 DOF** — Adds one orientation axis. Common in SCARA robots for assembly.
- **5 DOF** — Sufficient for most welding and palletizing.
- **6 DOF** — Full spatial positioning *and* orientation. Required for complex tasks like painting curved surfaces.
- **7+ DOF** — Redundant manipulators (e.g., medical robots, humanoids like KUKA LBR iiwa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A 3-DOF robot can position its tool tip anywhere in its work volume but cannot *orient* it. A 6-DOF robot is required when the end-effector must approach a workpiece from arbitrary directions.

### 2.1.1 Rule for Gruebler's Equation (Mobility Analysis)

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF of mechanism)
- $n$ = number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints)

## 2.2 Parameter 2 — Work Volume (Workspace)

The **work volume** is the set of all points in 3D space that the end-effector (tool tip) can reach.

**Types of work volume geometries:**

| Robot Type | Work Volume Geometry |
|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | Rectangular cuboid |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | Hollow cylinder |
| Spherical / Polar (RRP) | Hollow sphere segment |
| Articulated (RRR) | Sphere / Toroid (most complex) |
| SCARA (RRP) | Cylindrical disc (horizontal plane) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dexterous workspace** is the subset where the end-effector can attain *all orientations*. The **reachable workspace** is the larger set where *at least one* orientation is achievable. For a 6-DOF arm, dexterous = reachable (theoretically).

## 2.3 Parameter 3 — Capacity & Speed

- **Capacity (Payload):** The maximum mass the robot can carry *at the wrist* while maintaining rated performance. It includes the **end-effector mass** + **workpiece mass**.
- **Speed:** Measured at the tool center point (TCP) in **mm/s** or at individual joints in **°/s** (rad/s).

$$F_{applied} \le \mu \cdot (m_{payload} + m_{end\_effector}) \cdot g$$

To avoid dropping the payload, the gripping force at the gripper must satisfy the inequality above (where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction at the fingers).

## 2.4 Parameter 4 — Stroke & Reach

- **Stroke:** The maximum linear travel distance of an individual joint/axis.
- **Reach:** The maximum radial distance from the robot base to the **tool center point (TCP)** when the arm is fully extended.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reach ≠ Work Volume.** Reach is a *single scalar* (the maximum distance). Work volume is the *entire 3D envelope* swept by the TCP during all joint motions.

## 2.5 Parameter 5 — Repeatability

**Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to a *previously taught* position when commanded repeatedly under *identical* conditions (same load, same speed, same path).
