Mathematically, it is the **dispersion** of the measured positions about the mean:

$$\text{Repeatability} = \pm 3\sigma_x, \, \pm 3\sigma_y, \, \pm 3\sigma_z$$

or the radial form:

$$R = \pm 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

Where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of repeated position measurements. The **$\pm 3\sigma$** bound corresponds to the **99.7% confidence interval** under Gaussian statistics.

## 2.6 Parameter 6 — Precision

**Precision (Resolution)** is the *smallest incremental movement* the robot's control system can command. It is governed by:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Total Joint Travel}}{\text{Encoder Pulses per Revolution} \times \text{Gear Reduction Ratio}}$$

It is **NOT** the same as accuracy or repeatability.

## 2.7 Parameter 7 — Accuracy

**Accuracy** is the deviation between a *commanded position* and the *actual achieved position* in the *absolute* (world) coordinate frame.

$$\text{Accuracy Error} = \sqrt{(x_{cmd} - x_{act})^2 + (y_{cmd} - y_{act})^2 + (z_{cmd} - z_{act})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction:**
> - **Repeatability** is a measure of *consistency* (close to itself).
> - **Accuracy** is a measure of *correctness* (close to the truth).
> A robot can be **highly repeatable but poorly accurate** (the most common industrial case). Robots are *calibrated* to compensate for accuracy errors but rely on mechanical stiffness and encoder resolution for repeatability.

## 2.8 Parameter 8 — Operating Environment

Environmental factors dictating robot selection:

| Environment | Required Protection |
|---|---|
| Cleanroom (semiconductor) | **ISO Class 3**, vacuum-compatible |
| Foundry / Casting | Heat-resistant, IP67 sealed, dust-proof |
| Food / Pharma | **Stainless steel 316L**, washdown (IP69K) |
| Explosive (paint booth) | **ATEX / IECEx certified**, intrinsically safe |
| Underwater | Pressure-balanced housing, corrosion-proof |
| Cryogenic | Special lubricants, low-temp electronics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating:** The two-digit code represents *solid particle* (first digit, 0–6) and *liquid ingress* (second digit, 0–9) protection. **IP67** = dust-tight + immersion to 1 m.

## 2.9 Parameter 9 — PTP vs CP Control

**Point-to-Point (PTP) Control:**
- Robot moves from point A to point B with **no control over the path in between**.
- The trajectory is *parametric* (each joint moves from initial to final angle, often synchronously).
- Used in: **spot welding, pick-and-place, palletizing, drilling, loading CNC machines**.

**Continuous Path (CP) Control:**
- Robot follows a **continuously defined path** (often via multiple interpolated points or spline).
- The path between points is mathematically guaranteed.
- Used in: **arc welding, spray painting, polishing, gluing, laser cutting, waterjet cutting**.

### 2.9.1 Linear Interpolation (Joint to Cartesian Mapping)

$$P(t) = P_0 + (P_f - P_0) \cdot \frac{t}{T}, \quad 0 \le t \le T$$

This is the **straight-line CP** motion between two taught points. The robot must solve inverse kinematics at *every* intermediate $P(t)$ — computationally heavier than PTP.

### 2.9.2 Joint-Space PTP Trajectory (Trapezoidal Velocity Profile)

For a single joint rotating from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$:

$$v(t) = \begin{cases} a \cdot t & 0 \le t \le t_a \\ v_{max} & t_a \le t \le T - t_a \\ a \cdot (T - t) & T - t_a \le t \le T \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $a$ = angular acceleration (constant)
- $t_a$ = acceleration time $= v_{max}/a$
- $T$ = total motion time
- $v_{max}$ = maximum joint velocity

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Every formula below is testable in KTU ESE Module 2024 Scheme.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Bounds |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mobility of mechanism | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless integer |
| 2 | Repeatability (radial) | $R = \pm 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$ | mm |
| 3 | Encoder resolution | $\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{N_{pulses} \cdot G}$ | rad |
| 4 | Cartesian position error | $e = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$ | mm |
| 5 | Linear interpolation | $P(t) = P_0 + (P_f - P_0) \cdot t/T$ | mm |
| 6 | Trapezoidal velocity | $v(t) = a \cdot t$ (accel phase) | rad/s |
| 7 | Gripper force bound | $F \ge \mu \cdot m \cdot g / n_f$ | N |
| 8 | Work volume (Cartesian) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | m³ |
| 9 | Work volume (Cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | m³ |
| 10 | Work volume (Spherical) | $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | m³ |
| 11 | Spherical reach envelope | $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 \le R^2_{reach}$ | m² |
| 12 | TCP velocity magnitude | $\vert v_{TCP} \vert = \sqrt{\dot x^2 + \dot y^2 + \dot z^2}$ | m/s |
| 13 | Inverse Jacobian | $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot v_{TCP}$ | rad/s |
| 14 | Payload acceleration limit | $a_{max} = \tau_{max} / (I + m r^2)$ | rad/s² |
| 15 | Cycle time estimation | $T_{cycle} = T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{settle}$ | s |

### 2.11 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive welding lines:** 6-DOF articulated robots with CP control for arc welding.
- **PCB assembly:** SCARA robots (4-DOF) with PTP control for pick-and-place SMD components.
- **Pharma packaging:** Delta robots (4-DOF) with PTP, food-grade lubrication.
- **Aerospace drilling:** 6-DOF articulated robots with **PTP + force feedback** for accurate hole-making on curved fuselage.
- **Semiconductor wafer handling:** Cartesian robots in ISO Class 3 cleanrooms with **vacuum end-effectors**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solver Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown in full. No skipped steps. Each block is followed by a plain-English explanation of *why* the operation was performed.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Volume of a Spherical (Polar) Robot

A spherical robot has a radial link of length $r_1$ (shoulder rotation axis) and a second link of length $r_2$ (elbow). The TCP position in spherical coordinates is:

$$x = r \sin\phi \cos\theta$$

$$y = r \sin\phi \sin\theta$$

$$z = r \cos\phi$$

Where:
- $r = r_1 + r_2$ (fully extended reach)
- $\phi \in [0, \pi]$ is the polar angle from the Z-axis
- $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ is the azimuthal angle in the XY-plane

**Derivation of the boundary condition:**

The reachable points satisfy:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2 \sin^2\phi \cos^2\theta + r^2 \sin^2\phi \sin^2\theta + r^2 \cos^2\phi$$

Group the $\sin^2\phi$ terms:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2 \sin^2\phi (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) + r^2 \cos^2\phi$$

Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2 \sin^2\phi + r^2 \cos^2\phi$$

Factor the right-hand side:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2 (\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi)$$

Apply the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2$$

**Conclusion:** The boundary of the work volume is a **sphere of radius $r$**. The volume enclosed is:

$$V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

*Logic:* We started with the parametric position equations, substituted them into the Cartesian sum of squares, and exploited the fundamental trigonometric identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ to collapse the expression into a clean sphere equation. The result is the **work envelope boundary** — anything outside is unreachable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repeatability from Statistical Measurements

Suppose 20 repeated measurements of TCP position are taken at the *same* taught point. The data yields:

- $\bar{x} = 500.012$ mm, $\sigma_x = 0.008$ mm
- $\bar{y} = 250.005$ mm, $\sigma_y = 0.006$ mm
- $\bar{z} = 100.003$ mm, $\sigma_z = 0.010$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate the 3σ confidence bounds per axis:**

$$R_x = \pm 3 \sigma_x = \pm 3 \times 0.008 = \pm 0.024 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_y = \pm 3 \sigma_y = \pm 3 \times 0.006 = \pm 0.018 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_z = \pm 3 \sigma_z = \pm 3 \times 0.010 = \pm 0.030 \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* The $\pm 3\sigma$ bound is the industry-standard definition of repeatability because, for a Gaussian distribution, it captures 99.7% of all possible measurements.

**Step 2 — Combine into the radial (3D) repeatability:**

$$R = \pm 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

$$R = \pm 3\sqrt{(0.008)^2 + (0.006)^2 + (0.010)^2}$$

$$R = \pm 3\sqrt{0.000064 + 0.000036 + 0.000100}$$

$$R = \pm 3\sqrt{0.000200}$$

$$R = \pm 3 \times 0.014142$$

$$R = \pm 0.04243 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The robot's repeatability specification is **$\pm$0.042 mm** (rounded to $\pm$0.05 mm by the manufacturer). This value is what appears on the manufacturer's datasheet.

*Logic:* The radial form conservatively aggregates the dispersion in all three axes, giving a single scalar suitable for vendor datasheets.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Interpolation CP Trajectory

Given two taught points:
- $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$ mm
- $P_f = (100, 200, 50)$ mm
- Total time $T = 4$ seconds

Find the TCP position at $t = 1.5$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute the displacement vector:**

$$\Delta P = P_f - P_0 = (100 - 0, \; 200 - 0, \; 50 - 0) = (100, 200, 50) \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* This vector points from the start to the finish. The CP motion is *linear*, meaning the TCP must trace a straight line between them.

**Step 2 — Compute the interpolation ratio:**

$$s = \frac{t}{T} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375$$

*Logic:* The parameter $s$ is dimensionless and lies in $[0, 1]$. $s=0$ means at the start, $s=1$ means at the finish.

**Step 3 — Apply the linear interpolation formula:**

$$P(t) = P_0 + s \cdot \Delta P$$

$$P(1.5) = (0,0,0) + 0.375 \cdot (100, 200, 50)$$

$$P(1.5) = (0,0,0) + (37.5, 75.0, 18.75)$$

$$P(1.5) = (37.5, \; 75.0, \; 18.75) \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* Multiplying the displacement vector by $s$ gives the partial displacement achieved by time $t$. Adding it to the start point yields the actual TCP location.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trapezoidal Velocity Profile for a PTP Joint Motion

A robot joint must rotate from $\theta_0 = 0°$ to $\theta_f = 90°$ in $T = 2$ s with a trapezoidal profile. The maximum angular velocity is $v_{max} = 60°/s$. Determine the acceleration time $t_a$ and the constant-velocity duration $t_c$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the total angular displacement:**

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 90° - 0° = 90°$$

*Logic:* This is the total angle the joint must sweep.

**Step 2 — Relate $t_a$ to $v_{max}$ and acceleration $a$:**

$$v_{max} = a \cdot t_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a}$$

*Logic:* During the acceleration phase, velocity increases linearly from 0 to $v_{max}$, so the time to reach $v_{max}$ is $v_{max}/a$.

**Step 3 — Express the area under the velocity-time curve:**

For a symmetric trapezoidal profile:

$$\Delta\theta = v_{max} \cdot (T - t_a)$$

*Logic:* The area of a trapezoid = (sum of parallel sides)/2 × height. The "parallel sides" are $v_{max}$ (top) and 0 (start of accel and end of decel, which sum to a triangle equivalent). Equivalently: $\Delta\theta = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{max} t_a + v_{max} t_c + \tfrac{1}{2} v_{max} t_a = v_{max}(t_a + t_c)$. With $T = 2t_a + t_c$ (symmetric), $t_a + t_c = T - t_a$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_a$:**

$$90 = 60 \cdot (2 - t_a)$$

$$90 = 120 - 60 \cdot t_a$$

$$60 \cdot t_a = 30$$

$$t_a = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the constant-velocity duration $t_c$:**

$$T = 2t_a + t_c$$

$$2 = 2(0.5) + t_c$$

$$t_c = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the acceleration $a$:**

$$a = \frac{v_{max}}{t_a} = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \, °/\text{s}^2$$

**Conclusion:** The joint accelerates for 0.5 s, cruises at 60°/s for 1.0 s, and decelerates for 0.5 s. Total time = 2.0 s, total displacement = 90°.

*Logic:* A trapezoidal profile is preferred over a triangular profile in PTP motion because it minimizes peak motor torque and mechanical jerk — extending gear and bearing life.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Consideration | Weld Car Door | Pick-Place PCB | Spray Paint Auto Body | Cleanroom Wafer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DOF (Axes)** | 6 | 4 (SCARA) | 6 | 3 (Cartesian) |
| **Work Volume** | Large sphere (~3 m) | Flat disc (~0.5 m) | Huge sphere (~6 m) | Tight cuboid (~0.3 m) |
| **Payload** | 10–20 kg | 1–3 kg | 10–30 kg | < 1 kg |
| **Speed** | Medium | Very high | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm$0.1 mm | $\pm$0.02 mm | $\pm$0.5 mm | $\pm$0.01 mm |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm$1 mm | $\pm$0.05 mm | $\pm$2 mm | $\pm$0.05 mm |
| **Environment** | Foundry-tolerant | ESD-safe, clean | Explosion-proof (ATEX) | ISO Class 3 |
| **Control** | CP (arc weld) | PTP | CP (paint path) | PTP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Robot Selection Flowchart

The following block diagram captures the **decision flow** a KTU engineer follows when specifying a robot for an application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Task Definition] --> B0{Determine Required DOF}
    B0 -- Position Only --> C0[3 DOF Cartesian]
    B0 -- Position and 1 Orientation --> D0[4 DOF SCARA]
    B0 -- Full 6D Pose --> E0[6 DOF Articulated]
    C0 --> F0[Compute Work Volume]
    D0 --> F0
    E0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0{Verify Reach and Stroke}
    G0 -- Inadequate --> H0[Reselect Larger Robot]
    G0 -- Adequate --> I0[Check Payload Capacity]
    I0 --> J0{Payload within Limit}
    J0 -- No --> H0
    J0 -- Yes --> K0[Verify Repeatability and Accuracy]
    K0 --> L0{Meets Tolerance}
    L0 -- No --> H0
    L0 -- Yes --> M0{Path Type}
    M0 -- Discrete Stations --> N0[PTP Control]
    M0 -- Continuous Curve --> O0[CP Control with Linear or Spline Interpolation]
    N0 --> P0[Check Operating Environment]
    O0 --> P0
    P0 --> Q0{Special Conditions}
    Q0 -- Cleanroom --> R0[ISO Rated Robot]
    Q0 -- Explosive --> S0[ATEX Certified Robot]
    Q0 -- Standard --> T0[IP65 Industrial Robot]
    R0 --> U0[FINAL ROBOT SELECTED]
    S0 --> U0
    T0 --> U0
```

*Logic:* The flowchart codifies the **top-down** selection methodology. A failure at *any* stage forces a reselection — there is no "good enough" compromise on safety-critical parameters like payload and repeatability.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Work Envelope Block Architecture

A functional architecture view of the **robot work volume computation pipeline**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Joint Angle Limits] --> B1[Forward Kinematics DH Matrix]
    B1 --> C1[TCP Position in Base Frame]
    C1 --> D1[Sample Many Configurations]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Boundary Filter]
    E1 --> F1{Inside Reachable Set}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Add to Work Volume Point Cloud]
    F1 -- No --> H1[Discard Sample]
    G1 --> I1[Aggregate All Reachable Points]
    I1 --> J1[Compute Envelope Geometry]
    J1 --> K1[Output Cuboid or Cylinder or Sphere or Toroid]
    K1 --> L1[Render Work Envelope for Path Planning]
```

*Logic:* Forward kinematics (Denavit–Hartenberg convention) maps each set of joint angles to a unique TCP pose. By sampling the joint space, we discover the *reachable* region — the work volume.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — PTP vs CP Control Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Task Input: Path Specification] --> B2{Is Path Geometry Critical}
    B2 -- No, Only Start and End Matter --> C2[PTP Control Mode]
    B2 -- Yes, Path Shape Must Be Followed --> D2[CP Control Mode]
    C2 --> E2[Compute Joint Interpolation]
    E2 --> F2[Trapezoidal Velocity Profile]
    F2 --> G2[Each Joint Reaches Final Angle Independently]
    D2 --> H2[Generate Dense Waypoint List]
    H2 --> I2[Apply Linear or Spline Interpolation]
    I2 --> J2[Inverse Kinematics at Every Waypoint]
    J2 --> K2[Continuous Smooth Trajectory]
    G2 --> L2[Typical Use: Spot Weld and Palletize and Pick Place]
    K2 --> M2[Typical Use: Arc Weld and Spray Paint and Polish]
```

*Logic:* PTP is computationally simple (one IK solve at the endpoint). CP requires real-time IK at *every* intermediate waypoint, demanding higher controller bandwidth and memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Differentiate between repeatability and accuracy of an industrial robot. Why is repeatability considered more critical than accuracy in most manufacturing applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability to return to a *previously taught* position | Closeness of *commanded* position to *true* position |
| Nature | Measure of *consistency* | Measure of *correctness* |
| Cause of error | Mechanical backlash, thermal drift, encoder noise | Kinematic model errors, calibration error, link tolerances |
| Compensation | Cannot be improved by software; requires better mechanics | Can be improved by *calibration* and software compensation |
| Industrial importance | **More critical** | Less critical in production |

**Why repeatability is more critical (1 mark):**
In a production line, a robot is *taught* a position (via teach pendant) and then repeats that position thousands of times. Even if the *absolute* location has a 1 mm calibration error, as long as the robot returns to the *same taught* position every cycle, the manufactured part is consistent. Hence, repeatability governs part quality.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Explain the difference between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Continuous Path (CP) control. Give two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Point-to-Point (PTP) Control:**
- The controller moves the robot from point A to point B.
- The path *between* A and B is not controlled — it depends on the joint motion profile.
- Cheaper, simpler controller.
- **Applications:** (1) Spot welding, (2) Pick-and-place on a conveyor, (3) Palletizing, (4) Loading/unloading CNC machines.

**Continuous Path (CP) Control:**
- The controller follows a *continuous* path with many intermediate points.
- Linear or spline interpolation is used.
- Requires a high-performance controller with real-time inverse kinematics.
- **Applications:** (1) Arc welding, (2) Spray painting, (3) Laser cutting, (4) Adhesive dispensing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: A manufacturing company intends to automate a spray-painting station for automobile car bodies. As a robotics engineer, justify the selection of a 6-DOF articulated robot for this application. Discuss the following specifications in detail:**
- **(a) Work volume and reach considerations. (7 marks)**
- **(b) Choice of Continuous Path (CP) control, operating environment, and precision/accuracy requirements. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Why 6-DOF is mandatory (2 marks):**
A car body is a *curved 3D surface*. The spray gun must:
- Position the nozzle at the correct point on the surface (3 translational DOF)
- Orient the nozzle *normal* to the surface at every point (3 rotational DOF)
Hence, **6 DOF** is the minimum requirement. A SCARA or Cartesian robot would be unable to maintain the required surface-normal orientation across a complex curved geometry.

**2. Work volume estimation (3 marks):**
A typical car body is approximately $4.5$ m long, $1.8$ m wide, $1.5$ m high.
Assuming the robot is floor-mounted at the center of a painting booth of radius $R$:

$$V_{paint} \approx L \times W \times H = 4.5 \times 1.8 \times 1.5 = 12.15 \text{ m}^3$$

The robot's *reach* must be $\ge \sqrt{(L/2)^2 + (W/2)^2 + H_{above}}$ plus the distance to the car surface. For a typical installation, **reach $\ge$ 2.5 m** is required, often with a long-reach arm (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R1610 has reach 1610 mm; larger models reach 3 m+).

**3. Stroke of each joint (2 marks):**
- Joint 1 (base rotation): full 360° → paint both sides of the car.
- Joint 2 (shoulder): 0° to 180° to reach roof and lower panels.
- Joint 3 (elbow): 0° to 270° for underbody extension.
- Joints 4, 5, 6 (wrist): each $\ge$ 360° for full nozzle orientation.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Why CP control (3 marks):**
Spray painting requires a *uniform coating thickness*. If the gun moves along a non-uniform path, the paint thickness varies, causing drips, orange peel, or dry spots. CP control guarantees:
- Linear or spline-interpolated path
- Constant TCP velocity (typically 500–1000 mm/s)
- Constant standoff distance from the surface
Therefore, **CP control with linear interpolation** is mandatory.

**2. Operating environment (2 marks):**
- Spray paint fumes are **explosive (ATEX Zone 1)**.
- The robot must be **ATEX-certified** (e.g., explosion-proof enclosures, intrinsically safe wiring, pneumatic or specially sealed hydraulic actuation — *not* standard electric motors with brushes that can spark).
- Humidity: must be controlled (40%–60%) to avoid paint defects.
- Temperature: 20–25 °C for consistent paint viscosity.

**3. Precision and accuracy (2 marks):**
- **Repeatability** requirement: $\pm$0.5 mm is sufficient (paint overlap is typically 50%, so small errors are absorbed).
- **Accuracy**: less critical because the path is taught manually by an expert using the actual car body.
- **TCP velocity control** must be $\pm$2% to maintain uniform film build.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: A pharmaceutical company plans to deploy a robot for pick-and-place of small vials (mass 50 g each) onto a packaging conveyor. The conveyor runs at 1.5 m/s and the robot must place one vial every 800 ms. Justify the selection of a SCARA robot and discuss:**
- **(a) Number of axes, work volume, stroke, reach, payload, and speed analysis. (7 marks)**
- **(b) PTP control logic, operating environment (cleanroom), and precision/repeatability specifications. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Number of axes (2 marks):**
Vial pick-and-place involves:
- Vertical descent to the conveyor (1 translational)
- Gripper actuation (1 DOF at the end-effector)
- Horizontal pick from one location, horizontal place at another
A **4-DOF SCARA** (RRP + Z-axis) is ideal: two parallel rotary joints for X-Y planar motion, one prismatic Z for vertical pick, and one rotational $\theta$ for vial orientation. No need for full 6-DOF — this is engineering optimization.

**2. Work volume, stroke, reach (2 marks):**
- Pick station: 200 mm above the floor.
- Place station: 800 mm away horizontally.
- Vertical stroke: $\ge$ 250 mm.
- Horizontal reach: $\ge$ 800 mm.
- The SCARA's cylindrical work disc of radius 400 mm easily accommodates this. The Z-stroke of 250 mm handles the vial height (50 mm) plus approach/retract margins.

**3. Payload and speed (3 marks):**
- **Payload:** Vial mass (50 g) + gripper (2 kg) = ~2.05 kg → SCARA with 3 kg payload rating is sufficient. Safety factor $\ge 1.5$ is recommended, so the design payload is 3 kg.
- **Speed:** The 800 ms cycle time must accommodate:
  - Move from home to pick: ~150 ms
  - Descend, grip, ascend: ~150 ms
  - Move to place: ~200 ms
  - Descend, release, ascend: ~150 ms
  - Return home: ~150 ms
  - Total: 800 ms.
- This demands joint accelerations of $\sim 5g$ and TCP speeds of $\sim 3$ m/s, well within SCARA capability.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. PTP control logic (3 marks):**
Vial placement requires *discrete* positions (pick station, place station) — the path between them is irrelevant because the conveyor moves in a straight line and the vials are released at a fixed point. PTP is:
- Simpler (no real-time IK during motion)
- Faster (joint-space trajectory optimization)
- More reliable for high-speed repetitive cycles
**Trapezoidal velocity profile** at each joint: accel → cruise → decel. Settle time at endpoints $\le$ 20 ms.

**2. Operating environment (2 marks):**
- **ISO Class 7 cleanroom** (or better).
- Robot must be **pharma-grade stainless steel 316L** with no exposed lubricants.
- **IP65 or IP66 sealed body** to withstand daily washdown with alcohol/hydrogen peroxide.
- **Food-grade grease** in gearboxes (e.g., Klüber or NSF H1 certified).

**3. Precision and repeatability (2 marks):**
- **Repeatability:** $\pm$0.02 mm (high precision required because vials are placed into blister packs with $\pm$0.05 mm pocket tolerance).
- **Accuracy:** Less critical; the SCARA is taught at fixed fixtures.
- **Resolution:** 16-bit encoders ($\Delta\theta = 2\pi/2^{16} \approx 0.0001$ rad) easily provide sub-mm positional resolution after gear reduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Confusing "Precision" and "Repeatability":** A surprising number of students write "precision = repeatability". They are *different*. Precision is *resolution* (smallest increment); repeatability is *consistency* (statistical dispersion).
> 2. **Forgetting units in repeatability:** $\pm$0.05 **mm**, not "0.05".
> 3. **Stating "6 DOF is always better":** Wrong! For a planar task, 4-DOF SCARA is *superior* (faster, cheaper, more accurate).
> 4. **PTP/CP confusion:** PTP is *not* "point to point movement between two specific points with control" — it is movement between two points *without* path control. Confusing this loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the safety factor in payload:** Industry always uses a **1.5× to 2×** safety factor on payload. A 50 g vial + 2 kg gripper means a 3 kg robot is the *bare minimum*; a 5 kg robot is preferred.
> 6. **Ignoring environment:** Even if the kinematics are correct, students often skip ATEX / IP / cleanroom classifications. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize This List Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **DOF** = number of independent motions. Industrial: 3, 4, 5, or 6. Redundant robots have 7+.
- [ ] **Work Volume** = 3D envelope reachable by the TCP. Cartesian → cuboid; Cylindrical → hollow cylinder; Spherical → sphere; Articulated → sphere/toroid; SCARA → disc.
- [ ] **Reach** = maximum single distance from base to TCP. **Stroke** = max travel of one joint.
- [ ] **Capacity (Payload)** = max wrist load. Apply a **1.5×–2× safety factor** in real designs.
- [ ] **Speed** = TCP velocity (mm/s) or joint velocity (°/s or rad/s).
- [ ] **Repeatability** = ability to return to a *taught* position. $R = \pm 3\sigma$ (statistical, 99.7% confidence).
- [ ] **Precision (Resolution)** = smallest increment commanded. Depends on encoder and gear ratio.
- [ ] **Accuracy** = deviation between commanded and *true* position. Can be improved by calibration; repeatability cannot.
- [ ] **Operating Environment** dictates IP rating, ATEX, cleanroom (ISO Class), temperature, humidity, and material (SS316L for pharma/food).
- [ ] **PTP Control** = discrete endpoints, no path control. Applications: spot weld, pick-place, palletize.
- [ ] **CP Control** = continuous path with linear or spline interpolation. Applications: arc weld, paint, polish, glue.
- [ ] **Repeatability $\ne$ Accuracy.** A robot can be highly repeatable but inaccurate; the reverse is rare.
- [ ] **Higher DOF $\nRightarrow$ better robot.** Choose the *minimum* DOF that satisfies the task.
- [ ] **Trapezoidal velocity profile** is the standard PTP trajectory (accel → cruise → decel).
- [ ] **Linear interpolation** $P(t) = P_0 + s \cdot \Delta P$ is the most common CP path.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 favourite formula:** $R = \pm 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$ — practice this calculation in numerical questions.
- [ ] **Remember to draw the work envelope** in diagram questions; examiners award 1–2 marks for clean, labeled diagrams.
- [ ] **Always cite the application** when justifying a specification (e.g., "$\pm$0.02 mm repeatability because the vial pocket tolerance is 0.05 mm").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Applications - Medical, Mining, Space, Defense, Security, Domestic, Entertainment, Industrial Applications - Material Handling, Welding, Spray Painting, Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Applications - Medical, Mining, Space, Defense, Security, Domestic, Entertainment, Industrial Applications - Material Handling, Welding, Spray Painting, Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robot Application?

A **Robot Application** refers to the purposeful deployment of a programmable, multi-axis, sensor-integrated mechatronic system (robot) to perform a defined industrial, commercial, humanitarian, or scientific task autonomously or semi-autonomously under computer control. According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)**, an industrial robot is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes*.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, robot applications are broadly classified into **two macro-domains**:
- **Non-Industrial Applications**: Medical, Mining, Space, Defense, Security, Domestic, Entertainment.
- **Industrial Applications**: Material Handling, Welding, Spray Painting, Machining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OERUT611):** Students must be able to enumerate, classify, and describe the operational characteristics, end-effectors used, sensor requirements, and degrees of freedom of robots in each of the listed application domains. Descriptive answers with specific **real-world examples** score higher marks in KTU valuations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot as a "Specialized Worker"

Imagine a hospital. A general-duty doctor treats many patients, but a cardiologist only does heart-related work — a **specialist**. Similarly, a robot is a *general-purpose machine* (it can be reprogrammed for many jobs) but in practice, it is fitted with a *specialized end-effector* and *task-specific software* to become an **expert at one job**.

- A **surgical robot** is like a **scalpel-wielding surgeon** with a tremor-free hand.
- A **welding robot** is like an **arc welder's apprentice** who never tires and produces identical welds 10,000 times.
- A **space robot (rover)** is like a **geologist on wheels** sent to a place too dangerous for humans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *application* of a robot is determined by three coupled choices — the **end-effector** (tool), the **work envelope** (reach), and the **control architecture** (program). Change any one, and the same mechanical arm can perform a new job.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Application Classification

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload** | **0.5 kg – 1500 kg** | Determines what the robot can carry/tool |
| **Reach** | **0.3 m – 6.5 m** | Defines the working volume |
| **Repeatability** | **±0.01 mm – ±0.5 mm** | Critical for precision jobs (surgery, machining) |
| **Number of Axes** | **3 to 7** | Defines dexterity |
| **Operating Environment** | Cleanroom, Explosive, Underwater, Vacuum | Determines IP rating / shielding |
| **Cycle Time** | **0.5 s – 60 s** | Throughput in industrial jobs |

---

## 1.4 Broad Taxonomy of Robot Applications

The figure below represents a high-level classification tree followed in modern KTU-approved robotics textbooks (e.g., Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar; Niku; Craig).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROBOT APPLICATIONS"]:::root
    B["NON-INDUSTRIAL"]
    C["INDUSTRIAL"]
    D["Medical"]
    E["Mining and Space"]
    F["Defense and Security"]
    G["Domestic and Entertainment"]
    H["Material Handling"]
    I["Welding"]
    J["Spray Painting"]
    K["Machining"]
    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    B --> F
    B --> G
    C --> H
    C --> I
    C --> J
    C --> K
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Application Matrix

## 2.1 Non-Industrial Applications — Structured Analysis

### 2.1.1 Medical Robotics

Medical robots assist, augment, or replace human surgeons and caregivers. They operate in sterile, high-precision environments where the cost of error is human life.

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Surgical Robots** — Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS), e.g., **da Vinci Surgical System** (Intuitive Surgical, USA).
2. **Rehabilitation Robots** — Exoskeletons for paralyzed patients (e.g., **ReWalk**, **Ekso GT**).
3. **Assistive / Service Robots** — Hospital pharmacy dispensing, patient lifting, disinfection (UV-C robots like **Xenex**).
4. **Prosthetic Robots** — Bionic limbs (e.g., **DEKA LUKE Arm**).
5. **Tele-surgery Robots** — Remote surgery over 5G/Internet (e.g., **Lindbergh Operation, 2001**).
6. **Diagnostic / Lab Automation** — Blood sample handling, pipetting.

**Engineering Characteristics Required:**
- **Repeatability: ±0.02 mm or better** (sub-pixel surgical accuracy).
- **Sterilizable end-effectors** (autoclavable, biocompatible).
- **Force feedback / haptic sensors** with **resolution ≤ 0.1 N**.
- **Redundant kinematics (7 DOF)** to avoid singular configurations inside the body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master-Slave Teleoperation Architecture:** A human surgeon at the *master console* controls the *slave robot arms* inserted into the patient. Hand tremors (8–12 Hz) are filtered out using **low-pass filters** with cutoff ≈ 6 Hz.

### 2.1.2 Mining Robotics

Mining environments are hazardous — toxic gases, collapses, floods, and explosive dust. Robots are used to remove humans from danger.

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Autonomous Haulage Trucks** — e.g., **Caterpillar 793F** at Rio Tinto mines (carrying **400 tonnes**).
2. **Underground Load-Haul-Dump (LHD) Robots** — e.g., **Sandvik AutoMine**.
3. **Drilling and Roof-Bolting Robots**.
4. **Exploration & Mapping Rovers** — e.g., NASA's **VIPER** (lunar water prospecting concept).
5. **Rescue Robots** — Search and rescue in collapsed mines.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **IP67 / IP68 ingress protection** (dust + water immersion).
- **Explosion-proof enclosures (ATEX Zone 1 / IECEx certified)**.
- **LIDAR + IMU + thermal imaging** for navigation in dust/smoke.
- **Payloads: 10–400 tonnes** for haul trucks.

### 2.1.3 Space Robotics

Space is the ultimate hostile environment — vacuum, radiation, extreme temperature ($-150^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+150^{\circ}\text{C}$), zero gravity, and communication latency (up to **24 minutes** one-way to Mars).

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Planetary Rovers** — e.g., **NASA's Perseverance (Mars 2020)**, **Curiosity**, China's **Zhurong**.
2. **Robotic Arms on Spacecraft** — e.g., **Canadarm2** on the ISS (7 DOF, 16 m reach, 116,000 kg payload).
3. **Satellite Servicing Robots** — e.g., **NASA's Restore-L** (refueling in orbit).
4. **Space Debris Capture Robots** — e.g., **ClearSpace-1** (ESA, 2026).
5. **Asteroid Sample Return** — e.g., **OSIRIS-REx** (returned samples from Bennu in 2023).
6. **Humanoid Robots for Spacewalks** — e.g., **NASA Valkyrie (R5)**, **Robonaut 2**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Radiation-hardened electronics** (TID > 100 krad).
- **Time-delayed teleoperation** with **shared autonomy** (Mars rovers plan their own paths using **AutoNav**).
- **Sterile assembly** (planetary protection — no Earth microbes).
- **Low-power consumption** (solar + RTG, 100–300 W typical).

### 2.1.4 Defense Robotics

Defense robots are designed for **reconnaissance, bomb disposal, combat support, and logistics** in hostile territory.

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs)** — e.g., **MAARS** (Modular Advanced Armed Robotic System), **Andros** (bomb disposal).
2. **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs / Drones)** — e.g., **MQ-9 Reaper**, **Bayraktar TB2**.
3. **Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUVs)** — e.g., **REMUS 600** for mine detection.
4. **Autonomous Convoy Vehicles** — Leader-follower truck platoons.
5. **Combat Robots** — e.g., **THeMIS** (Tracked Hybrid Modular Infantry System, Estonia).
6. **Sentry / Perimeter Robots** — e.g., **TALON SWORDS**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **MIL-STD-810 environmental qualification** (shock, vibration, temperature).
- **Secure encrypted communication links** (anti-jamming, frequency hopping).
- **Stealth signatures** (low acoustic, IR, and radar cross-section).
- **Endurance: 24–40 hours** continuous operation.

### 2.1.5 Security Robotics

Security robots provide **surveillance, intrusion detection, and patrol** in places like malls, airports, data centers, and warehouses.

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Indoor Patrol Robots** — e.g., **Knightscope K5** (autonomous mall/office patrol).
2. **Perimeter Surveillance** — e.g., **Cobalt Robotics** (uses thermal + 360° cameras).
3. **Bomb Detection / EOD** — e.g., **iRobot 510 PackBot**.
4. **Cybersecurity-aware Robots** — Integrates with SOCs to detect physical intrusions.
5. **Crowd Monitoring Robots** — e.g., **Promobot** used in Dubai malls.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Autonomous navigation in crowded environments** (SLAM + pedestrian detection).
- **Two-way audio/video** for remote guard interaction.
- **Long battery life: 12–18 hours** with autonomous re-docking.
- **GDPR/privacy compliance** with data encryption.

### 2.1.6 Domestic Robotics

Domestic robots perform household chores — the largest growing consumer market (CAGR ≈ 18%).

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Floor Cleaning Robots** — e.g., **iRobot Roomba**, **Roborock S8**.
2. **Window Cleaning Robots** — e.g., **WINBOT**.
3. **Lawn Mowing Robots** — e.g., **Husqvarna Automower**.
4. **Pool Cleaning Robots** — e.g., **Dolphin S300**.
5. **Gutter Cleaning & Window-Washing Robots**.
6. **Companion / Eldercare Robots** — e.g., **Pepper** (SoftBank), **LOVOT**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Low cost ($200 – $1500)**.
- **Single-task focused** with simple 2D/3D SLAM.
- **Anti-collision and anti-fall sensors** (cliff detection IR + bump sensors).
- **Self-docking for charging**.

### 2.1.7 Entertainment Robotics

Entertainment robots engage, educate, and amuse humans.

**Key Sub-Applications:**
1. **Toy Robots** — e.g., **LEGO Mindstorms**, **Sphero Bolt**.
2. **Humanoid Show Robots** — e.g., **Disney's Imagineers** (audio-animatronics), **Sophia (Hanson Robotics)**.
3. **Robot Pets** — e.g., **Sony AIBO**, **Zoomer**.
4. **Theme Park Rides** — Animatronic characters, autonomous ride vehicles.
5. **Competitive Robots** — e.g., **FIRST Robotics**, **BattleBots**, RoboCup soccer.
6. **Educational STEM Kits** — e.g., **VEX Robotics**, **NAO**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Expressive actuators** (servo motors with smooth motion profiles).
- **Facial recognition, NLP, and speech synthesis**.
- **Safe to interact with children** (soft skin, force-limited joints).
- **Wireless connectivity (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth)**.

---

## 2.2 Industrial Applications — Structured Analysis

Industrial robots account for the **largest installed base** — over **4.28 million units worldwide** (IFR World Robotics Report 2023).

### 2.2.1 Material Handling

Material handling is the **largest industrial application** — about **38% of all industrial robot installations**.

**Sub-Applications:**
- **Pick-and-Place** — Transferring parts between conveyor and pallet.
- **Palletizing / Depalletizing** — Stacking boxes on pallets.
- **Machine Tending** — Loading/unloading CNC machines.
- **Press Tending**.
- **Bin Picking** — Random 3D picking from a bin (vision-guided).
- **Packaging and Packing**.
- **Warehouse AGVs/AMRs** — e.g., **Amazon Kiva robots** (now Amazon Robotics).

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Payload range: 5 kg – 1500 kg** (largest are gantry-type).
- **High speed: 200–600 picks/hour**.
- **End-effectors: Grippers, vacuum cups, magnetic grippers**.
- **Accuracy: ±0.05 mm** for electronics, ±1 mm for heavy palletizing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2019 University Exam Insight:** "Explain the role of robots in material handling with examples." — A model answer should mention **4 sub-tasks** (pick/place, palletizing, machine tending, bin picking) with specific industry examples.

### 2.2.2 Welding

Welding is the **second-largest industrial application** — about **20% of installations**. Robots are used for **arc welding, spot welding, and laser welding**, primarily in the **automotive industry** (BIW — Body-in-White).

**Sub-Applications:**
- **Arc Welding (MIG/MAG/TIG)** — Continuous weld paths.
- **Spot Welding (Resistance Welding)** — Sheet metal joints in car bodies.
- **Laser Welding** — High-precision welds, deep penetration.
- **Plasma Welding**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **6-axis articulated arm** with **payload 6–20 kg**.
- **Repeatability: ±0.05 mm** (essential for seam quality).
- **Welding torch as end-effector**, with **wire-feed mechanism**.
- **Through-Arc Seam Tracking (TAST)** or **laser-vision tracking** for adaptive control.
- **Typical automotive body has 3000–5000 spot welds**, performed in ~60 seconds by 30+ robots.

**Example:** In a **Tesla Model Y** body shop, the **Frontal Mega Press** welds the front and rear underbody in one operation — using **six large 6-axis robots** working synchronously.

### 2.2.3 Spray Painting

Spray painting exposes human workers to **volatile organic compounds (VOCs), isocyanates, and explosive aerosols**. Robots eliminate the hazard and deliver a uniform coat.

**Sub-Applications:**
- **Automotive Body Painting** — Primer, basecoat, clearcoat.
- **Plastic Component Painting**.
- **Furniture & Cabinet Finishing**.
- **Aerospace Component Coating**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Explosion-proof (Ex-proof) joints** (pneumatic or hydraulic, with sealed enclosures).
- **Continuous-path (CP) control** — moves smoothly along complex curved surfaces.
- **Programming via manual lead-through** (operator physically moves arm to "teach" the path).
- **Typical 6–8 DOF** to maintain **constant standoff distance (200–300 mm)** and **perpendicular spray angle** to the surface.
- **Airflow and humidity control** in spray booth (downdraft ventilation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Teaching Method:** Spray-painting robots are often taught by **manual lead-through** because the free-form curves are too complex to program in text. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is lead-through programming preferred for spray-painting robots?"* — Answer: Because the trajectories are continuous, complex, and the operator can intuitively feel the right motion.

### 2.2.4 Machining

Robotic machining uses an industrial robot fitted with a **spindle** (drilling, milling, deburring, grinding, routing) as the end-effector. It offers **high flexibility, large workspace, and low cost** compared to a fixed CNC machine tool.

**Sub-Applications:**
- **Drilling and Tapping** — e.g., aerospace wing panels.
- **Milling of Large Structures** — e.g., ship hull foam patterns, wind turbine blades.
- **Deburring and Polishing** — Castings, machined parts.
- **Routing** — Composites, plastics, wood.
- **Grinding** — Weld seam grinding.
- **Water-jet / Abrasive Cutting**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **High stiffness and accuracy** — modern robotic machining cells achieve **±0.1 mm** accuracy.
- **Vibration suppression** using **active damping algorithms** and stiff mechanical design.
- **Force-controlled spindles** (compliance to part surface).
- **Pose accuracy compensation** using **kinematic calibration** and **laser tracker feedback**.

**Comparative Insight — Robot vs CNC:**
| Parameter | Industrial Robot | CNC Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | Large (5–6 m reach) | Smaller (1–2 m travel) |
| Cost (6-axis equivalent) | $80,000 – $150,000 | $200,000 – $800,000 |
| Stiffness | ~1 N/µm | ~50 N/µm |
| Accuracy | ±0.1 mm (calibrated) | ±0.005 mm |
| Best for | Large, soft materials (composites, foam) | Hard, precision parts (metal) |

- **Automotive plants** use 1000+ robots per facility — robots pay back in **1.5–2 years** for high-volume welding.
- **Hospitals** are deploying surgical robots to enable **1 mm incisions**, reducing patient recovery time by **40%**.
- **Mars rovers** have driven over **45 km** autonomously (Perseverance) — extending human scientific reach.
- **E-commerce warehouses** (Amazon) use 750,000+ mobile robots to **cut fulfillment time by 50%**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Application Engineering Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is **descriptive and application-oriented** (not a derivation-heavy topic), this section provides a **rigorous analytical decomposition** of one representative application (Robotic Welding) — chosen because KTU frequently asks 14-mark analytical questions on welding. The same analytical method applies to all other applications.

## 3.1 Analytical Problem — Robotic Arc Welding of an Automotive Door

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 14-mark Style):**
> A 6-axis articulated robot is used to perform MIG arc welding on a **car door seam of total length 1.8 m**. The seam is on a curved surface of radius **R = 0.4 m**, and the required **welding speed is 0.012 m/s** (720 mm/min). The robot has a **repeatability of ±0.05 mm** and is fitted with a **through-arc seam tracking (TAST) sensor** with a **lateral tracking accuracy of ±0.3 mm**.
> **(a)** Calculate the **time required** to complete the seam.
> **(b)** Determine the **total positioning tolerance band** and comment on weld quality.
> **(c)** List **two specific safety and sensor requirements** for this application.

---

### Part (a) — Time Calculation

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters**
- Total seam length: $L = 1.8 \text{ m}$
- Welding speed: $v = 0.012 \text{ m/s}$
- Number of seams (door has 1 continuous seam here): $n = 1$

**Step 2: Apply the basic time formula for linear motion**

$$t = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$t = \frac{1.8 \text{ m}}{0.012 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Perform the division**

$$t = 150 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Add auxiliary time (arc start/stop, corner dwell)**
In practice, add a **15% allowance** for arc ignition time, corner pause, and torch repositioning:

$$t_{\text{total}} = 1.15 \times 150 \text{ s} = 172.5 \text{ s} \approx 2 \text{ min } 53 \text{ s}$$

**Answer (a):** The welding cycle time per door is **≈ 172.5 seconds**.

---

### Part (b) — Tolerance Band Analysis

**Step 1: Identify tolerance contributors**
- Robot **repeatability**: $\pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$
- Seam tracking (TAST) **lateral error**: $\pm 0.3 \text{ mm}$
- Thermal drift of workpiece: typically $\pm 0.1 \text{ mm}$ (given the heat input)

**Step 2: Apply the RSS (Root-Sum-Square) method for independent errors**

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (0.3)^2 + (0.1)^2} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute**

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.09 + 0.01} \text{ mm}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{0.1025} \text{ mm}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 0.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compare with welding tolerance standard**
- For MIG welding on thin sheet steel (0.8–1.2 mm), the **acceptable lateral seam deviation** is typically **±0.5 mm** (AWS D11.2 standard).
- Our value of $\pm 0.32 \text{ mm}$ is well within tolerance.

**Answer (b):** Total tolerance band is **±0.32 mm** → weld quality is **acceptable**.

---

### Part (c) — Safety & Sensor Requirements

| Requirement | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Optical/IR arc-flash protection** at all viewing windows | Arc UV radiation causes "arc eye" — 100% preventable with proper shade 11–13 glass. |
| **Through-Arc Seam Tracking (TAST) sensor** | Compensates for thermal distortion of workpiece; ensures torch stays on seam. |
| **Emergency stop with rope-pull** around the cell | Worker may be entangled in fixtures; hardwired E-stop required. |
| **Light curtain + safety-rated interlocks** | Cat-3 PLd per ISO 13849-1 for collaborative cells. |
| **Fume extraction** | Welding fumes contain Mn, Cr, Ni → occupational asthma risk. |

---

## 3.2 General Analytical Template for Any Robot Application

For **any KTU 14-mark question** on a robot application, use this **5-step analytical structure** (this is exactly what the examiner expects):

**Step 1: Define the application** — State the task precisely (1 line).

**Step 2: Justify the use of a robot** — Why not manual labor? Mention 3 of: **safety, repeatability, speed, ergonomics, quality, cost**.

**Step 3: Specify the robot type** — Articulated (6-axis), Cartesian, SCARA, Delta, or mobile — justify with one sentence.

**Step 4: Describe the end-effector and sensors** — Gripper/torch/scalpel + vision/force/proximity — be specific.

**Step 5: Provide a real-world example with numbers** — At least one specific product (e.g., *da Vinci, KUKA KR 1000, Amazon Hercules*).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "robots are used because they are fast and accurate" — this is too generic. You MUST add a *quantified* justification, e.g., *"increases throughput from 15 parts/hour to 80 parts/hour"* or *"reduces operator exposure to isocyanate fumes by 95%."* Quantified answers score **15–20% higher marks** in KTU valuation.

---

|---|---|
| Robotic Welding | A **single spot welder** makes **30–60 welds/min** vs. 5–10 manual. |
| Spray Painting | Reduces paint wastage by **30%** and VOC emissions by **70%**. |
| Material Handling | Bin-picking robot achieves **1500 picks/hour** vs. 250 manual. |

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer, 40-50 words each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**List any three specific applications of robots in the medical field and mention the type of robot used in each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Minimally invasive surgery** — *da Vinci Surgical System* (master-slave teleoperated 7-DOF robot).
2. **Rehabilitation** — *ReWalk exoskeleton* (wearable 6-DOF robot for paraplegic gait assistance).
3. **Hospital pharmacy automation** — *Omnicell* dispensing robot (cartesian 3-DOF pick-and-place).
*(1 mark per correct pair, partial credit for examples.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Why is lead-through programming commonly used for spray-painting robots rather than textual programming?**

**Model Answer:**
Spray-painting trajectories are **continuous, free-form 3D curves** with a requirement to maintain **constant standoff distance** and **perpendicular orientation** to the surface. The painter can **physically demonstrate the path** by moving the robot arm through the motion (lead-through), which is faster and more intuitive than computing each waypoint manually. *(3 marks — 1 for reason, 1 for example, 1 for surface-orientation justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> The KTU ESE pattern offers an **internal choice** between two questions on the same module. The two questions below are designed to be **fully independent** — either may be asked.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **four main categories of industrial robot applications**, and for each, give **one specific sub-task and a real-world example**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robotic arc-welding cell** and discuss the role of **through-arc seam tracking (TAST)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Material Handling (Pick-and-Place / Palletizing / Bin Picking)**
- **Task:** Transferring parts from a conveyor to a pallet.
- **Example:** Amazon's **Hercules** mobile robot lifts shelves of up to **567 kg** across a fulfillment center.
- **Robot type:** 6-axis articulated arm with vacuum gripper; or AMR with roller deck.

**2. Welding (Arc / Spot / Laser)**
- **Task:** Joining two metal sheets by melting the interface.
- **Example:** A **FANUC ARC Mate 120iC** performs **MIG welding on a Ford F-150 chassis**, completing **120 welds in 50 seconds**.
- **Robot type:** 6-axis articulated, payload 6–20 kg, with arc-voltage-tracking sensor.

**3. Spray Painting**
- **Task:** Apply uniform paint layer to car bodies.
- **Example:** **ABB IRB 5500** in a BMW Leipzig plant paints an entire car body in **~70 seconds**, using **electrostatic bell applicators**.
- **Robot type:** Ex-proof 6–7 DOF, with lead-through teaching.

**4. Machining (Drilling / Milling / Deburring)**
- **Task:** Drill fastener holes in aerospace wings.
- **Example:** **KUKA KR 1000 L750 titan** drills thousands of holes in a **Boeing 787 CFRP wing** with ±0.2 mm positional accuracy.
- **Robot type:** High-payload 6-axis, with force-controlled spindle.

> **Mark Allocation (Part a):** 4 categories × 1.5 = 6 marks + 1 mark for diagram/example synthesis = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

The functional architecture of a robotic arc-welding cell has **four major subsystems** (refer Section 4.2 diagram):

**1. Manipulator Subsystem (3 marks)**
- 6-axis articulated arm with payload 6–20 kg.
- Welding torch end-effector with **gas nozzle**, **contact tip**, and **wire liner**.
- Wire feeder (Miller S-74, Lincoln LN-742) feeds consumable electrode at 5–15 m/min.

**2. Sensor Subsystem (2 marks)**
- **Through-Arc Seam Tracking (TAST)** — measures lateral current variations as the arc oscillates across the seam; corrective signal sent to controller.
- **Arc voltage sensor** maintains constant **arc length (typically 3–5 mm)** for uniform penetration.
- **Pyrometer / IR sensor** monitors interpass temperature to prevent overheating.

**3. Control Subsystem (1 mark)**
- PLC + Robot Controller coordinate: start signal → torch move → arc strike → tracking → arc extinguish.
- HMI allows operator to set parameters: voltage (V), wire feed speed (WFS), travel speed (TS).

**4. Safety Subsystem (1 mark)**
- Light curtains, E-stop, fume extractor, **arc-flash glass shade 11–13**, **curtained work envelope** to protect surrounding workers from UV radiation.

**Role of TAST (2 marks):**
- **Principle:** During welding, the arc itself acts as a sensor. The robot oscillates the torch in a small circular or transverse pattern. When the arc moves over the joint, the **current varies** because the **electrical resistance changes** with the gap. The controller measures this and **steers the torch laterally** to stay on the seam.
- **Benefit:** Compensates for **thermal distortion**, **fixture tolerance**, and **plate mismatch** — improving seam-tracking accuracy from ±1.0 mm (open-loop) to **±0.3 mm (closed-loop TAST)**.

> **Mark Allocation (Part b):** Subsystems 7 + TAST 2 = 9 → scale to **7 marks** (capped).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the applications of robots in **space exploration** and **defense**, citing at least **two real-world examples in each domain**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **industrial material handling with domestic cleaning robots** under the headings: **(i) task complexity, (ii) payload and reach, (iii) sensors used, (iv) cost**. Conclude with which one is more technically challenging. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Space Robotics (3.5 marks):**
1. **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** 6-wheeled rocker-bogie suspension; carries 7 scientific instruments; uses **AutoNav** software for autonomous path planning. The robotic arm has 5 DOF, carries a drill and sample-collection system. Powered by **MMRTG (110 W)**.
2. **On-Orbit Servicing (Canadarm2 on ISS):** 7-DOF, 16.6 m reach, 116,000 kg payload capacity — used to dock spacecraft and assist EVA astronauts.
3. **Asteroid Sample Return (OSIRIS-REx):** Touch-And-Go Sample Acquisition Mechanism (TAGSAM) extended a robotic arm to Bennu's surface in 2020, returning **~250 g of sample** to Earth in 2023.
4. **Space Debris Removal (ClearSpace-1, ESA):** 4-arm capture robot scheduled for 2026 launch to grab a **Vespa payload adapter**.

**Defense Robotics (3.5 marks):**
1. **EOD UGV (iRobot 510 PackBot):** Tracked 5-DOF robot with manipulator arm; used in Iraq/Afghanistan to disable IEDs at standoff distance of **>300 m**.
2. **Combat UAV (MQ-9 Reaper):** 20+ hour endurance, 1740 kg MTOW, carries **AGM-114 Hellfire** missiles and **GBU-12 Paveway** bombs.
3. **Autonomous Convoy (Lockheed SMSS):** Leader-follower truck platoon — one manned vehicle controls 6 unmanned followers in supply convoys.

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 domains × 3 examples × 1 mark (max 6) + 1 mark for clarity = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

| Parameter | Industrial Material Handling | Domestic Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Task Complexity** | High — multi-step, sequence-dependent, often coordinated with CNC/plc | Low–Medium — single repetitive task in 2D room |
| **(ii) Payload & Reach** | Payload **5–1500 kg**; reach **2–6 m** | Payload < 1 kg; reach < 0.5 m |
| **(iii) Sensors Used** | 3D vision (e.g., Cognex), force-torque, proximity, encoder, safety scanner | Bumper, IR cliff sensor, gyroscope, basic camera (some models) |
| **(iv) Cost** | **$50,000 – $500,000** per cell | **$200 – $1,500** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** **Industrial material handling is more technically challenging** because it must handle 3D vision-based random bin picking, 1-tonne payloads, hazardous environments (furnaces, freezers), and synchronise with upstream/downstream machines — domestic robots operate in controlled, low-risk indoor environments.

> **Mark Allocation (Part b):** Comparison table 4 marks + domain justification 2 marks + conclusion 1 mark = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Robot Application Questions (Reported by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Writing generic "robots are fast, accurate, and tireless"** — You lose 2–3 marks. You must **quantify** (e.g., "increases throughput by 300%" or "tolerance ±0.05 mm").
> 2. **Forgetting the end-effector** — Many students describe the robot but ignore the tool. Always state the **end-effector explicitly** (gripper, torch, scalpel, nozzle, spindle).
> 3. **No real-world example** — A description without an example is worth **at most 60%** of the marks. Always cite a named product/company.
> 4. **Confusing DOF and axes** — A *6-axis articulated robot has 6 DOF*; do not write "6 axis DOF" or "6 axes" without specifying they are *rotational*.
> 5. **Confusing AGV with AMR** — *Automated Guided Vehicle* uses **magnetic tape/wire paths** (no intelligence); *Autonomous Mobile Robot* uses **SLAM and vision** (truly autonomous). Examiners check this distinction.
> 6. **Missing safety subsystem** — In any welding, painting, or machining answer, mentioning **light curtains, E-stop, fume extraction, and arc-flash protection** adds **1–2 marks** instantly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before every KTU exam on Module 1.**

### A. Non-Industrial Applications — Key Facts
- **Medical:** Master-slave teleoperation (da Vinci), 7 DOF, ±0.02 mm accuracy, haptic feedback, tremor filter at 6 Hz.
- **Mining:** ATEX/IECEx certified, IP67+, haul trucks carry up to **400 tonnes**, use LIDAR + IMU + thermal sensors.
- **Space:** Canadarm2 has 7 DOF, 16.6 m reach, 116,000 kg payload; Perseverance rover has 7 instruments, MMRTG powered; teleoperation delayed (4–24 min to Mars) → uses **shared autonomy**.
- **Defense:** MQ-9 Reaper (UAV), PackBot (UGV), REMUS 600 (UUV), MIL-STD-810 certified, encrypted comms.
- **Security:** Knightscope K5 (indoor patrol), uses 360° thermal + optical cameras, 12–18 hr battery.
- **Domestic:** Roomba (vacuum), Husqvarna (lawn), Dolphin (pool) — all are **wheeled 2–3 DOF**, $200–$1500.
- **Entertainment:** AIBO (pet), NAO (humanoid), LEGO Mindstorms (educational), Disney animatronics.

### B. Industrial Applications — Key Facts
- **Material Handling:** 38% of installations, 1500 picks/hour (bin-picking), payloads up to 1500 kg, 4 sub-tasks: **pick-place, palletizing, machine tending, bin picking**.
- **Welding:** 20% of installations, ±0.05 mm repeatability, TAST/laser-vision tracking, MIG/MAG/TIG, spot, laser.
- **Spray Painting:** Ex-proof, lead-through programming, continuous-path control, 200–300 mm standoff, 30% paint saving.
- **Machining:** Large workspace + low cost vs CNC, ±0.1 mm calibrated, force-controlled spindle, best for **composites, foam, large parts**.

### C. Universal Vocabulary (Must Memorize)
- **AGV** = Automated Guided Vehicle (no intelligence, follows markers).
- **AMR** = Autonomous Mobile Robot (uses SLAM, intelligent).
- **UGV/UAV/UUV** = Unmanned Ground/Aerial/Underwater Vehicle.
- **DOF** = Degrees of Freedom (independent axes of motion).
- **Payload** = Max weight robot can carry at full reach.
- **Reach** = Max distance from robot base to wrist center.
- **Repeatability** = How closely the robot returns to a taught position (not the same as accuracy).
- **TAST** = Through-Arc Seam Tracking — arc itself as sensor.
- **MIG/MAG/TIG** = Metal Inert Gas / Metal Active Gas / Tungsten Inert Gas welding.
- **MIS** = Minimally Invasive Surgery.
- **EOD** = Explosive Ordnance Disposal.
- **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping.
- **BoW** = Body-in-White (car body before painting).
- **MMRTG** = Multi-Mission Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator.
- **ISO 8373:2021** = Standard defining industrial robot.
- **ATEX/IECEx** = Explosion-proof certifications for hazardous zones.

### D. Numerical Quick-Facts to Memorize
- Industrial robots installed worldwide: **>4.28 million** (IFR 2023).
- Average payback period: **1.5–2 years**.
- da Vinci systems installed globally: **>7,500** (2023).
- Perseverance rover distance driven: **>45 km** (autonomous).
- Spraying paint saving with robots: **~30%**.
- Welding seam tolerance: **±0.3 to ±0.5 mm** (typical automotive).
- Surgical incision size: **~1 cm** (robotic) vs **~15 cm** (open).
- Canadarm2 payload: **116,000 kg** (≈ 75 cars).
- Roomba cleaning area: **~150 m²** per charge.

### E. Examiner's Favourite Question Patterns
1. *"Compare industrial and non-industrial robot applications with examples."* — Always comes for 7 marks.
2. *"Explain the role of robots in [welding/material handling/medical] with diagrams."* — 14-mark analytical question.
3. *"List sensors used in [spray painting/welding/mining] applications."* — 3-mark short.
4. *"Why is lead-through programming used for spray painting?"* — frequently asked 3-mark.
5. *"Differentiate between AGV and AMR with examples."* — Frequently asked 3-mark.

> **End of Topic: Robot Applications — Module 1 — OERUT611 Fundamentals of Robotics**
