# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between **contact-type** and **non-contact-type** external sensors in robotics, citing one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Contact-Type | Non-Contact-Type |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Physical contact** | Required with target | Not required |
| **Working principle** | Mechanical deformation, strain, pressure | Electromagnetic, acoustic, optical field |
| **Example** | Tactile sensor (FSR) / Force-Torque sensor | Ultrasonic range finder / Inductive proximity sensor |
| **Output nature** | Often analog (force magnitude) | Often binary or distance (analog) |
| **Drawback** | Subject to wear, slower | Sensitive to environmental noise (light, dust) |

(Examiner's note: A clean table with one example of each is sufficient for full 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
State **any three characteristics** used to specify the performance of a robotic external sensor, with one-line definitions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Range** — The minimum and maximum values of the measured quantity over which the sensor operates correctly. (1 mark)
2. **Resolution** — The smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a detectable change in output. (1 mark)
3. **Repeatability** — The ability of the sensor to produce the same output for the same input under identical conditions over multiple cycles. (1 mark)

(Optional additional parameters for partial credit: Accuracy, Linearity, Hysteresis, Bandwidth, Response Time.)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Essay with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are given internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **6-axis Force/Torque (F/T) sensor** used in a robotic wrist. Describe how strain gauges and the **Wheatstone bridge** are used to convert mechanical force into an electrical signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial F/T sensor has a full-scale force of 500 N and 12-bit ADC resolution. Calculate: (i) the resolution per LSB in Newtons, and (ii) the minimum detectable change in force if the controller requires a minimum of 3 ADC counts to register a change. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A 6-axis F/T sensor is built around a precisely machined **elastic element**, typically in a "cross" or "spoke" configuration, made of a high-stiffness alloy such as aluminium or titanium. The element is designed to deform elastically (and **only** elastically) under load. On each of the four (or six) spokes of the cross, **semiconductor or foil strain gauges** are bonded. A total of **6 channels** (one per measured axis: $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$) are connected to **6 independent Wheatstone bridges**. The sensor is mounted between the robot's **wrist flange** and the **end-effector (gripper or tool)**.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When an external force or moment is applied at the tool, the elastic element undergoes micro-strain ($\varepsilon \approx 10^{-4}$). Each strain gauge experiences a change in resistance $\Delta R$ proportional to strain. Each Wheatstone bridge converts this small $\Delta R$ into a differential voltage $V_{out}$ using the formula:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)
$$

When a strain gauge stretches, $R_1$ increases, and when its companion gauge (perpendicular) compresses, $R_2$ decreases — the bridge becomes unbalanced, and $V_{out} \neq 0$. The output is amplified, digitised by an ADC, and processed by a microcontroller. A **decoupling matrix** (precomputed via calibration) inverts the cross-coupled signals to recover the six independent components $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the F/T block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes. The student should draw a labelled diagram showing: External force → Elastic element → 6 strain gauges → 6 bridges → ADC → Decoupling matrix → Controller.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Full-scale force $F_{FS} = 500$ N
- ADC resolution $N = 12$ bits

**Step 1: Total number of codes per full scale** (1 mark):

$$
\text{Codes} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2: Resolution per LSB** (2 marks):

$$
R_{LSB} = \frac{F_{FS}}{\text{Codes}} = \frac{500}{4096} = 0.1221 \, \text{N/LSB}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum detectable change (3 LSB threshold)** (2 marks):

$$
\Delta F_{min} = 3 \times R_{LSB} = 3 \times 0.1221 = 0.3663 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation** (2 marks):

The robot controller can resolve changes in force as small as **0.1221 N per ADC count**, and it requires a 3-LSB swing to trigger a control update, so the **minimum controllable force increment is 0.3663 N**. This is well below the 1–2 N contact force required for safe human-robot collaboration under ISO/TS 15066, confirming the sensor is suitable for cobot applications.

**Final Answer (boxed):** Resolution = **0.1221 N/LSB**; Minimum detectable change = **0.3663 N**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the working of an **ultrasonic range finder** used as a non-contact external sensor in a robot. Derive the expression for the measured distance in terms of the speed of sound and the echo time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses an ultrasonic sensor to detect an obstacle. The echo time measured is **8.75 ms**. Assuming the speed of sound in air is **343 m/s**, calculate the distance to the obstacle. If the robot moves at **0.5 m/s** towards the obstacle, how long will it take to reach it from the current position? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
An ultrasonic range finder consists of a **piezoelectric transducer** (a single unit acts as both transmitter and receiver), a **timer circuit**, and an **amplifier**. The operating frequency is typically **40 kHz** (above the human hearing range of 20 kHz, hence "ultra"-sonic). Operation occurs in three steps:

1. The controller triggers a **pulse generator** that excites the piezo crystal, producing a short burst of **~8 to 16 cycles** of 40 kHz sound.
2. The sound wave propagates through the air at the speed of sound $c$, strikes the target, and a portion of the acoustic energy is **reflected** back as an **echo**.
3. The same transducer (now acting as a receiver) detects the echo. The time elapsed between emission and reception is $\Delta t$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The sound travels to the target and back, covering a **round-trip distance** of $2d$ in time $\Delta t$:

$$
2d = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
\boxed{\,d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 appears **only** because the pulse travels to the object **and back** — the actual one-way distance is half the round-trip path.

**Advantages:** Works in dark, on transparent objects, dust-penetrative.
**Disadvantages:** Poor angular resolution (wide beam cone ~15°), cannot detect soft/foam surfaces, slow at long ranges.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Echo time $\Delta t = 8.75$ ms $= 8.75 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s
- Robot speed $v = 0.5$ m/s

**Step 1: Distance calculation** (3 marks):

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
343 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3} = 3.001 \, \text{m}
$$

Divide by 2:

$$
d = \frac{3.001}{2} = 1.5005 \, \text{m} \approx 1.5 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Time to reach obstacle** (3 marks):

Using $d = v \cdot t \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{d}{v}$:

$$
t_{reach} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3.0 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Safety margin (1 mark):**
The robot must slow down before reaching 1.5 m. In practice, ultrasonic sensors are used for **collision avoidance**, not direct docking. A safety stopping distance of 0.3–0.5 m must be programmed, so the robot should initiate its braking routine at:

$$
d_{brake} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** Distance to obstacle = **1.5 m**; Time to reach = **3.0 s** (with a recommended braking initiation at **2.0 m** for safe deceleration).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Examiner Observations)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance derivations: Students often write $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$. This single error costs **2–3 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question. Always remember: the pulse goes **out AND back**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $F/T$ sensor with a simple load cell:** A 6-axis F/T sensor measures **3 forces + 3 moments**, not just one axial force. Drawing only a single strain gauge arrangement instead of a cross/spoke with 6 channels will lose **2–3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up contact and non-contact classifications:** A common error is listing **Hall-effect** as a contact sensor — it is **non-contact** (magnetic field). Similarly, **limit switches** are contact, not non-contact. Mislabelling costs **1 mark** per error.
>
> 4. **No unit conversions:** Writing $5.83 \, \text{ms}$ without converting to seconds ($5.83 \times 10^{-3}$ s) before plugging into formulas. Examiners expect SI units throughout. **0.5–1 mark** deduction.
>
> 5. **Missing the resolution-bit conversion:** Confusing the formula $\dfrac{\text{FS}}{2^N}$ with $\dfrac{\text{FS}}{2^N - 1}$. For sensor resolution calculations, $\dfrac{\text{FS}}{2^N}$ is the KTU-accepted convention.
>
> 6. **No diagram for a 7-mark construction question:** A textual description without a labelled block diagram will receive at most **5/7 marks**. Always include the diagram.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — External Sensors**

### Core Concepts to Memorise
- **Definition:** External sensors perceive the **environment**, internal sensors perceive the **robot's own state**.
- **Two families:** Contact-type (touch required) and Non-contact-type (field/beam required).
- **Contact examples:** Limit switches, tactile sensors, F/T sensors, pressure sensors, whiskers.
- **Non-contact examples:** Inductive, capacitive, Hall-effect, optical, ultrasonic, IR, laser/LiDAR, vision (CCD/CMOS).

### Critical Formulas (Must-Memorise Set)
- Ultrasonic distance: $\quad d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$
- Hooke's Law: $\quad \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
- Wheatstone bridge output: $\quad V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right)$
- Capacitive plate: $\quad C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$
- ADC resolution: $\quad R_{LSB} = \dfrac{F_{FS}}{2^N}$
- Beam divergence: $\quad r(z) = r_0 + z \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$

### Key Numbers to Remember
- Speed of sound in air @ 20°C: **c = 343 m/s**
- Speed of light (vacuum): **c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Strain Gauge Gauge Factor (metallic): **G ≈ 2**
- Standard ultrasonic frequency: **40 kHz**
- 1 atmosphere pressure: **101.325 kPa**

### One-Liner Comparisons (For Quick Recall)
- Inductive sensors detect **metals only**; capacitive sensors detect **all materials**.
- Piezoelectric sensors are **AC-only** (no DC); piezo-resistive are **DC-capable**.
- Through-beam optical sensors have the **longest range**; diffuse reflective have the **shortest**.
- Tactile sensors = "**touch sense**"; Vision = "**sight sense**"; Ultrasonic = "**echolocation sense**".

### Practical Industrial Mapping (Valuable for 14-Mark Answers)
- **Limit switches** → Conveyor end-of-line stop.
- **F/T sensor** → Cobot collision detection (ISO/TS 15066).
- **LiDAR** → Self-driving cars and warehouse AMRs.
- **Inductive proximity** → Part presence detection on 90% of conveyors.
- **Vision systems** → Quality inspection, bin-picking, surgical robotics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision - Elements of Vision Sensor, Image Acquisition, Image Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vision - Elements of Vision Sensor, Image Acquisition, Image Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Machine Vision — The Engineering Definition

**Machine Vision** is the scientific discipline that provides a robotic system with the ability to "see," interpret, and act upon visual information from its surrounding environment. According to the **KTU 2024 Robotics syllabus**, machine vision (also called **Robot Vision** or **Computer Vision**) encompasses the complete pipeline of capturing, processing, analysing, and understanding images obtained from a real-world scene to enable intelligent robotic decision-making.

A **Vision Sensor** is therefore defined as the integrated electro-optical-mechanical subsystem that converts optical information (light reflected from objects) into a digital data stream that a robot's controller can process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Machine vision is the technology and methods used to provide imaging-based automatic inspection, process control, and robot guidance in industrial applications. It combines optics, electronics, sensors, and software to interpret scenes.*

---

### 1.2 The Five Mandatory Elements of a Vision Sensor

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups vision sensor elements into **five functional blocks**. Every industrial vision system is built from these blocks:

| # | Element | Function in the Pipeline |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Illumination / Lighting** | Provides controlled light to maximize contrast between object and background. |
| 2 | **Optics / Lens System** | Collects reflected light and focuses it onto the imaging plane. |
| 3 | **Image Sensor (CCD / CMOS)** | Converts the focused light into a continuous analogue electrical signal. |
| 4 | **Frame Grabber / Interface** | Digitises the analogue signal into a digital image frame for the processor. |
| 5 | **Processor + Software** | Executes image-processing algorithms to extract features and generate decisions. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Vision Sensor is the Robot's "Eye + Brain Cortex"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a person (the robot) walking into a dimly lit library to find a specific red book. To do so, the person must:
> 1. **Switch on a torch (Lighting)** so the book is clearly visible.
> 2. **Squeeze the eyes to focus (Lens)** so the image is sharp.
> 3. **The retina captures the image (Image Sensor)** as raw signals.
> 4. **The optic nerve carries the data to the brain (Frame Grabber + Bus)**.
> 5. **The brain's visual cortex interprets the picture (Processor + Algorithm)** and says *"That's a red book on shelf 3."*
>
> Replace each human component with its engineering equivalent, and you have the **robot vision system**. Lighting is therefore *not* an accessory — it is the foundation of the entire visual decision chain.

---

### 1.4 Why is Vision Critical in Robotics?

Modern KTU-aligned robotics modules cite **3 decisive advantages** vision gives a robot:

- **Non-Contact Sensing** → ideal for fragile, hot, or fast-moving workpieces.
- **High Information Density** → a single 1-megapixel frame contains **~1 million data points** versus a single reading from a proximity sensor.
- **Flexibility** → the same vision hardware can recognise *hundreds* of parts with only a software change, eliminating mechanical retooling.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark KTU question simply asks *"List the elements of a vision sensor."* Memorise the **5 blocks above in order** — illumination → optics → sensor → frame grabber → processor. Drawing a labelled block diagram fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Element 1 — Illumination (The "Stage Lighting" of Machine Vision)

Good lighting is responsible for **70% of the success** of any vision application. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises lighting by **geometry** and **source type**.

**A. By Geometry of Light Projection:**

- **Backlighting:** The light is placed *behind* the object, producing a high-contrast silhouette. Used for **dimensional measurement**.
- **Front / Diffuse Lighting:** The light is in front of the object. Used for **surface defect detection**.
- **Ring Lighting:** A circular LED array fitted coaxially around the lens. Used for **PCB inspection**.
- **Dome Lighting:** A hemispherical diffuser producing uniform low-shadow illumination. Used for **reflective curved surfaces**.

**B. By Source Type:**

- **LED (Light Emitting Diode):** Most common, long life (~100,000 hrs), narrow spectrum, low heat.
- **Halogen / Incandescent:** Broadband white light, hot, short life.
- **Fluorescent:** Cool, diffuse, slower response.
- **Laser (Structured Light):** Produces a line or grid for **3D profiling**.
- **Xenon Strobe:** Pulsed ultra-bright light for **high-speed inspection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU textbook highlights **Wavelength Selection**: red light (~660 nm) filters out blue background noise in printed-circuit inspection, while infrared (~850 nm) is used for heated metals or to reduce surface reflections.

---

### 2.2 Element 2 — Optics (Lens System)

The lens obeys the **Thin-Lens Equation** (Gaussian Optics):

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length of the lens (in mm)
- $u$ = object distance from the lens
- $v$ = image distance from the lens

**Two critical lens parameters for robotics:**

**1. Field of View (FOV):** The physical area visible to the camera.

$$
\text{FOV} = \frac{\text{Sensor Size} \times \text{Object Distance}}{\text{Focal Length}}
$$

**2. Working Distance (WD):** The distance from the lens front to the object being inspected.

**3. Depth of Field (DOF):** The range of object distances that remain in acceptable focus.

$$
\text{DOF} \approx 2 \times N \times c \times \left(1 + \frac{M}{1-M}\right)^2
$$

Where $N$ is the f-number, $c$ is the circle of confusion, and $M$ is the magnification.

---

### 2.3 Element 3 — Image Sensor (CCD vs CMOS)

The image sensor is a 2-D array of **photosites (pixels)** that convert photons into electrons.

| Feature | **CCD (Charge Coupled Device)** | **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Specialised process | Standard IC process (cheaper) |
| **Signal Readout** | Sequential (bucket-brigade) | Per-pixel (parallel) |
| **Noise** | Very low | Higher (mitigated by modern design) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (need 3–4 supply rails) | Low (single 3.3 V / 5 V rail) |
| **Speed** | Slower | Very fast (global + rolling shutter) |
| **Application** | Scientific, low-light | Industrial, machine vision, mobile |

**Pixel Size (Pitch):** Typically **3 µm – 10 µm**. A larger pixel captures more photons → better low-light performance but lower spatial resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasises the **Shutter Mechanism**:
> - **Global Shutter:** All pixels expose simultaneously → required for **moving objects** (conveyor belts).
> - **Rolling Shutter:** Pixels are exposed row-by-row → distortion when object moves fast.

---

### 2.4 Element 4 — Frame Grabber / Interface

The frame grabber performs three tasks:
1. **Synchronises** the image capture with external triggers (encoders, sensors).
2. **Digitises** the analogue video signal (if using an analogue camera) into digital pixels.
3. **Transfers** the frame to the processor using a high-speed bus.

**Modern Digital Interfaces Used in Robotics:**

| Interface | Bandwidth | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **GigE Vision (Gigabit Ethernet)** | ~100 MB/s | Distributed robot cells, long cables up to 100 m |
| **USB3 Vision** | ~350 MB/s | Plug-and-play, up to 5 m |
| **Camera Link** | ~850 MB/s | High-speed inspection, 10 m |
| **CoaXPress (CXP)** | ~1.25 GB/s per lane | Ultra-high-speed, 40 m+ |

---

### 2.5 Element 5 — Processor + Image Processing Software

The processor is usually an industrial PC or an embedded **System-on-Chip (SoC)** such as NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi + Coral, or FPGA-based hardware. The software executes the **image-processing algorithms** discussed in Section 2.7.

---

### 2.6 Image Acquisition — Sampling, Quantisation & Resolution

When a continuous scene is captured, it must be converted into a **discrete 2-D digital image**. This requires two fundamental operations:

**A. Spatial Sampling (Pixelisation):**
The continuous image plane $I(x, y)$ is sampled at a finite number of equally spaced points, producing an $M \times N$ grid of pixels.

$$
I(x, y) \;\longrightarrow\; I[m, n] \quad \text{for } m = 0, 1, \dots, M-1,\;\; n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

**Sampling Theorem (Nyquist Criterion) for Images:** To faithfully reconstruct the image, the sampling rate must be at least **twice the highest spatial frequency** present in the scene:

$$
f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 \times f_{\text{max, spatial}}
$$

Violation → **Aliasing** (jagged edges, Moiré patterns).

**B. Quantisation (Greyscale Conversion):**
The continuous intensity at each pixel is converted to a discrete integer level. For an 8-bit greyscale image:

$$
L = 2^k
$$

Where $k$ = number of bits per pixel. So $L = 256$ levels for $k = 8$.

**C. Resolution Hierarchy:**

| Type of Resolution | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Resolution** | Number of pixels (M × N) | 1920 × 1080 (Full HD) |
| **Greyscale / Intensity Resolution** | Bits per pixel (k) | 8-bit (256 levels) |
| **Temporal Resolution** | Frames captured per second (fps) | 30 – 10,000 fps |
| **Spectral Resolution** | Number of wavelength bands | 1 (mono), 3 (RGB), n (hyperspectral) |

---

### 2.7 Image Processing — The Algorithm Layer

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **three principal stages** of image processing in robotics:

#### 2.7.1 Pre-Processing (Image Enhancement)

Goal: Improve the image quality so that subsequent feature-extraction steps work reliably.

- **Greyscale Conversion:** Reduce 3-channel colour image to 1-channel intensity using the luminance formula:

$$
Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

- **Histogram Equalisation:** Spreads the intensity histogram to use the full 0–255 range, increasing contrast.
- **Spatial Filtering:**
  * **Mean Filter (Smoothing):** Replaces each pixel with the average of its neighbours → reduces noise, blurs edges.
  * **Median Filter:** Replaces each pixel with the median of its neighbours → removes "salt-and-pepper" noise while preserving edges (very popular in robotics).
  * **Gaussian Filter:** Weighted average using a Gaussian kernel → smoother and more natural than mean.
  * **Sharpening Filter (Laplacian):** Enhances edges by subtracting the second-derivative (Laplacian) from the image.

#### 2.7.2 Segmentation (Object Isolation)

Goal: Separate the **Region of Interest (ROI)** from the background.

- **Thresholding:**
  * **Global Thresholding:** A single $T$ separates object from background:

$$
g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

  * **Otsu's Method:** Computes $T$ automatically by maximising inter-class variance.
  * **Adaptive Thresholding:** $T$ varies across the image (useful for uneven illumination).

- **Edge Detection:** Identifies pixels where intensity changes abruptly. The KTU syllabus emphasises three classical operators:

| Operator | Order | Noise Robustness | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roberts (2 × 2)** | First | Poor | Very Fast |
| **Sobel (3 × 3)** | First | Good | Fast |
| **Prewitt (3 × 3)** | First | Good | Fast |
| **Laplacian (3 × 3)** | Second | Poor (very sensitive) | Fast |
| **Canny (multi-step)** | Multi | Excellent | Slower (but optimal) |

  The **Canny Edge Detector** is the gold standard in robotics because it uses:
  1. Gaussian smoothing
  2. Gradient computation (Sobel)
  3. Non-Maximum Suppression
  4. Hysteresis thresholding with two thresholds $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$.

- **Region Growing / Connected Components:** Groups adjacent similar pixels into a single labelled blob — used for **counting objects on a conveyor**.

#### 2.7.3 Feature Extraction & Recognition

Goal: Extract numerical descriptors and classify the object.

- **Geometric Features:** Area, perimeter, centroid, number of holes, aspect ratio.
- **Moments (Hu Moments):** Scale, rotation, and translation invariant — used for object identification irrespective of pose.
- **Template Matching:** Slide a stored reference image over the captured image and compute a similarity score (e.g., Normalised Cross-Correlation):

$$
NCC = \frac{\sum (I_{\text{scene}} - \bar{I}_{\text{scene}})(T - \bar{T})}{\sqrt{\sum (I_{\text{scene}} - \bar{I}_{\text{scene}})^2 \sum (T - \bar{T})^2}}
$$

- **Modern Deep-Learning Features:** CNN-based detectors such as YOLO, Faster-R-CNN, and SSD for unstructured environments.

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula you must memorise for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value to keep the table syntax valid.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical Value / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thin Lens Equation** | $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$ | $f, u, v$ in metres | Always valid for thin lenses |
| 2 | **Field of View** | $\text{FOV} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size} \times \text{Object Distance}}{\text{Focal Length}}$ | mm | Depends on lens |
| 3 | **Magnification** | $M = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{\text{Image Height}}{\text{Object Height}}$ | dimensionless | $< 1$ for normal use |
| 4 | **Greyscale Levels** | $L = 2^k$ | $k$ = bits per pixel | 256 for $k = 8$ |
| 5 | **Image Size (Pixels)** | $N_{\text{pixels}} = M \times N$ | pixels | 2,073,600 for 1080p |
| 6 | **Storage Requirement** | $\text{Size} = M \times N \times k$ bits $= \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8}$ bytes | bytes / KB / MB | See Section 3 derivation |
| 7 | **Sampling Theorem** | $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2\,f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz or pixels/m | Avoid aliasing |
| 8 | **Greyscale Conversion** | $Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$ | intensity 0–255 | ITU-R BT.601 standard |
| 9 | **Mean Filter (3×3)** | $g[m,n] = \dfrac{1}{9}\sum_{i=-1}^{1}\sum_{j=-1}^{1} f[m+i,\,n+j]$ | intensity | Smooths noise |
| 10 | **Laplacian Filter** | $\nabla^2 f = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2}$ | intensity | Edge enhancement |
| 11 | **Sobel Gradient (X)** | $G_x = f_{i-1,j+1} + 2f_{i,j+1} + f_{i+1,j+1} - f_{i-1,j-1} - 2f_{i,j-1} - f_{i+1,j-1}$ | intensity | Vertical edges |
| 12 | **Image Acquisition Rate** | $\text{fps} \leq \dfrac{\text{Interface Bandwidth (MB/s)} \times 8}{M \times N \times k}$ | frames per second | Depends on bus speed |
| 13 | **Centroid (Blob Centre)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{N},\;\; \bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i}{N}$ | pixels | 0th and 1st moments |
| 14 | **Bit Depth Required** | $k = \lceil \log_2(L_{\text{required}}) \rceil$ | bits | 8 for 256 levels |

---

### 2.9 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Automotive Welding Cells:** Vision-guided seam tracking uses structured laser lighting + line-scan CMOS cameras at **10 kHz line rate** to follow the gap between two metal panels.
- **Pharmaceutical Blister-Pack Inspection:** Backlit + CNN classifier at 600 pills/second.
- **Bin Picking:** 3-D stereo vision identifies randomly piled parts so the robot can pick them — relies on **edge detection + template matching** discussed above.
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) Navigation:** Vision replaces magnetic tape; uses **feature extraction (ORB/SLAM)** to localise the vehicle.

> [!TIP]
> Always quote **specific numerical metrics** (e.g., *10 kHz*, *600 pills/s*, *1920 × 1080 @ 30 fps*) in KTU answers — examiners award bonus marks for **quantitative depth**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Image Storage Requirement

**Problem Statement:** A robotic inspection cell uses a colour camera with **1920 × 1080** spatial resolution and **8 bits per channel** for an RGB image. Compute the total storage required for a single frame, both in bits and in megabytes (MB). Use $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of pixels:**

$$
M \times N = 1920 \times 1080
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the product:**

$$
1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}
$$

This is the standard *Full HD* count often quoted as **2.07 megapixels**.

**Step 3 — Identify the bit depth per pixel:**

For an RGB colour image with 8 bits per channel:

$$
k_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ bits/pixel}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total bits per frame:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bits}} = M \times N \times k_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bits}} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply the constants explicitly:**

$$
2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 2{,}073{,}600 \times (20 + 4)
$$

$$
= 41{,}472{,}000 + 8{,}294{,}400
$$

$$
= 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert bits → bytes (divide by 8):**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes/frame}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert bytes → megabytes:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{MB}} = \frac{6{,}220{,}800}{8 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{MB}} = \frac{6{,}220{,}800}{8{,}000{,}000} = 0.7776 \text{ MB/frame}
$$

**Final Result:** A single Full-HD 24-bit colour frame occupies **≈ 0.78 MB**, or equivalently **49,766,400 bits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At 30 fps, this camera generates a **data stream of 0.78 × 30 = 23.34 MB/s ≈ 187 Mbps**. Therefore a standard USB 2.0 bus (480 Mbps) *just* handles it, but USB 3.0 / GigE Vision are preferred for headroom. This single derivation is the most-asked 14-mark numerical in KTU exams on this module.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Achievable Frame Rate from Interface Bandwidth

**Problem Statement:** A GigE Vision camera produces **1280 × 1024** greyscale images with **10 bits/pixel**. The GigE interface has a maximum usable bandwidth of **100 MB/s**. Compute the theoretical maximum frame rate (fps).

**Step 1 — Compute pixels per frame:**

$$
N_{\text{pixels}} = 1280 \times 1024 = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute bits per frame:**

$$
\text{bits/frame} = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 10 = 13{,}107{,}200 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert bits to bytes:**

$$
\text{bytes/frame} = \frac{13{,}107{,}200}{8} = 1{,}638{,}400 \text{ bytes/frame} \approx 1.638 \text{ MB/frame}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum fps:**

$$
\text{fps}_{\max} = \frac{\text{Bandwidth (bytes/s)}}{\text{bytes/frame}} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1{,}638{,}400}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the division:**

$$
\text{fps}_{\max} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000}{1{,}638{,}400}
$$

$$
\text{fps}_{\max} \approx 61.04 \text{ frames per second}
$$

**Final Result:** The camera can stream at most **≈ 61 fps** under ideal conditions. In practice, **network protocol overhead (~30 %)** reduces this to **≈ 42 fps**, which still comfortably exceeds the 30 fps of a typical industrial conveyor inspection.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Field of View and Object Resolution on the Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A 50 mm lens is mounted on a camera with a **1/3-inch sensor** (active area 4.8 mm × 3.6 mm) at a working distance of 800 mm. Compute (a) the field of view on the object plane, and (b) the smallest object feature that can be resolved if the sensor has 640 × 480 pixels.

**Step 1 — Field of View (Horizontal):**

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{horiz}} = \frac{\text{Sensor Width} \times \text{Object Distance}}{\text{Focal Length}}
$$

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{horiz}} = \frac{4.8 \times 800}{50} = \frac{3840}{50} = 76.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Field of View (Vertical):**

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{vert}} = \frac{3.6 \times 800}{50} = \frac{2880}{50} = 57.6 \text{ mm}
$$

So the visible scene is a **76.8 mm × 57.6 mm** rectangle.

**Step 3 — Resolution per pixel (Horizontal):**

$$
\text{Res}_{\text{horiz}} = \frac{\text{FOV}_{\text{horiz}}}{\text{Pixels}_{\text{horiz}}} = \frac{76.8 \text{ mm}}{640}
$$

$$
\text{Res}_{\text{horiz}} = 0.12 \text{ mm/pixel}
$$

**Step 4 — Resolution per pixel (Vertical):**

$$
\text{Res}_{\text{vert}} = \frac{57.6 \text{ mm}}{480} = 0.12 \text{ mm/pixel}
$$

**Final Result:** The system can resolve features as small as **0.12 mm (120 µm)**. Anything smaller than this will fall in a single pixel and be invisible to the algorithm.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU numericals:** A common KTU Part-B problem is *"the smallest object the system can detect is `FOV / pixels` in mm."* Always carry the units; examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Threshold Value for Global Binarisation (Otsu's Method — Conceptual Walkthrough)

**Problem Statement:** A greyscale image has a histogram with two distinct peaks — one at intensity 60 (background) and one at intensity 180 (object). Compute an estimate of the global threshold $T$ using the **mid-point rule** (a simple alternative to Otsu's method). If the image size is $512 \times 512$, how many bytes does the binarised image occupy?

**Step 1 — Identify the two cluster centres:**

$$
\mu_1 = 60 \quad \text{(background peak)}, \qquad \mu_2 = 180 \quad \text{(object peak)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mid-point rule:**

$$
T = \frac{\mu_1 + \mu_2}{2} = \frac{60 + 180}{2} = \frac{240}{2} = 120
$$

**Step 3 — Apply thresholding rule:**

$$
g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 255 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq 120 \quad (\text{object}) \\ 0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < 120 \quad (\text{background}) \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute binary image size:**

For a binary image, $k = 1$ bit per pixel.

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bits}} = 512 \times 512 \times 1 = 262{,}144 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bytes:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{262{,}144}{8} = 32{,}768 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ KB}
$$

**Final Result:** Threshold $T = 120$, and the binarised image occupies **32 KB** — a massive **compression** (by a factor of **~50×** for an 8-bit original of 256 KB).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Edge Detector?

| Operating Condition | Recommended Operator | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time control loop (> 100 Hz) | **Sobel** | 3 × 3 mask, only 8 additions per pixel. |
| High-noise industrial floor | **Canny** | Gaussian pre-smoothing eliminates false edges. |
| Edge orientation required | **Sobel (Gx, Gy)** | Provides direction $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$. |
| Pure edge magnitude only | **Prewitt** | Simpler than Sobel, similar quality. |
| Salt-and-pepper noise | **Median filter first**, then Canny | Median removes impulse noise before edge detection. |
| Resource-constrained MCU | **Roberts** | 2 × 2 mask, minimal computation. |

---

### 3.6 Compact Reference Table — Numerical Answer Bank

For quick last-minute KTU revision, the table below consolidates the **final numerical results** of the four derivations:

| Problem | Final Key Result |
|---|---|
| Full-HD 24-bit colour frame storage | **≈ 0.78 MB / 49.77 Mbits** |
| 1.3 MP 10-bit GigE max frame rate | **≈ 61 fps theoretical / 42 fps real** |
| FOV with 50 mm lens, 800 mm WD, 1/3″ sensor | **76.8 mm × 57.6 mm**; **0.12 mm/pixel** |
| Mid-point threshold between peaks 60 & 180 | **T = 120**; binary size = **32 KB** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Machine Vision Pipeline

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the full data flow of a robot vision system, from the physical scene to the final control action. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SCENE OBJECT 3D Physical World"] --> nodeB["ILLUMINATION LED Laser Dome Backlight"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OPTICS Lens 50mm f2.8"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["IMAGE SENSOR CCD or CMOS Array"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["FRAME GRABBER Digitiser GigE USB3"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["PRE-PROCESSING Filter Histogram"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["SEGMENTATION Threshold Edge Canny"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["FEATURE EXTRACTION Area Centroid Moments"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"DECISION LOGIC Recognition"}
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ACTUATOR COMMAND Robot Joint / Gripper"]
    nodeK["FEEDBACK Encoder / Proximity"] --> nodeF
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Information flows strictly from left to right.
- The illumination block *acts upon* the scene (not the other way around).
- The decision diamond is the only conditional node — it determines whether the robot acts.
- The feedback loop closes the cycle for **closed-loop visual servoing**.

---

### 4.2 Image Acquisition & Processing Sub-Block Diagram

The following diagram zooms into the **digital signal chain** inside the frame grabber and the processor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["ANALOGUE VIDEO SIGNAL Continuous Voltage Waveform"] --> p2["ANTI-ALIASING FILTER Low-Pass Hardware"]
    p2 --> p3["SAMPLING HOLD M x N Grid"]
    p3 --> p4["QUANTISATION ADC k bits per pixel"]
    p4 --> p5["DIGITAL IMAGE BUFFER M x N x k bits"]
    p5 --> p6["GREYSCALE / COLOR CONVERSION"]
    p6 --> p7["NOISE REMOVAL Median or Gaussian"]
    p7 --> p8["ENHANCEMENT Histogram Equalisation"]
    p8 --> p9["SEGMENTATION Otsu Canny Threshold"]
    p9 --> p10["FEATURES Area Centroid Edges"]
    p10 --> p11["OUTPUT BLOB LIST / POSE VECTOR"]
```

---

### 4.3 Lighting Geometry Comparison Diagram

The following diagram contrasts the four principal lighting geometries used in machine vision.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT["FRONT / DIFFUSE LIGHTING"]
        f1["Light Source in Front"] --> f2["Object Surface"] --> f3["Camera Above"]
    end
    subgraph BACK["BACK LIGHTING Silhouette"]
        b1["Light Source Behind"] --> b2["Object Blocks Light"] --> b3["Camera in Front Captures Shadow"]
    end
    subgraph RING["RING LIGHTING Coaxial"]
        r1["LED Ring Around Lens"] --> r2["Uniform Spot on Object"] --> r3["Camera at Centre"]
    end
    subgraph DOME["DOME LIGHTING Diffuse Hemispherical"]
        d1["Multiple LEDs Inside Dome"] --> d2["Scattered Light onto Object"] --> d3["Camera Sees Evenly Lit Surface"]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> When the examiner asks *"Which lighting is used to measure the diameter of a turned metal shaft?"*, the correct answer is **Backlighting** because it creates a sharp black silhouette on a bright background — the easiest case for **global thresholding**.

---

### 4.4 Image Processing Algorithm Selection Flow

The following decision-tree diagram shows the **systematic procedure** a robotics engineer should follow when designing an image-processing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START Raw Image Acquired"] --> s2{"Is the Image Noisy?"}
    s2 -->|"Yes, Salt Pepper"| s3["Apply Median Filter"]
    s2 -->|"Yes, Gaussian Noise"| s4["Apply Gaussian Filter"]
    s2 -->|"No"| s5["Skip Smoothing"]
    s3 --> s6{"Is Lighting Uniform?"}
    s4 --> s6
    s5 --> s6
    s6 -->|"Yes"| s7["Use Global Thresholding Otsu"]
    s6 -->|"No"| s8["Use Adaptive Thresholding"]
    s7 --> s9{"Need Edge Information?"}
    s8 --> s9
    s9 -->|"Yes"| s10["Apply Canny Edge Detector"]
    s9 -->|"No"| s11["Skip to Feature Extraction"]
    s10 --> s12["Extract Geometric Features"]
    s11 --> s12
    s12 --> s13["Compare with Template / Train Classifier"]
    s13 --> s14["Output Robot Command"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]`
**List and briefly explain the five essential elements of a vision sensor used in industrial robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

1. **Illumination (½ mark):** Controlled light source (LED/laser/fluorescent) that maximises contrast between object and background. Choice of geometry (back/front/ring/dome) and wavelength are critical.

2. **Optics / Lens (½ mark):** Collects reflected light and forms a focused image on the sensor. Defined by focal length $f$ and obeys the thin-lens equation.

3. **Image Sensor (½ mark):** A 2-D array of photosites (CCD or CMOS) that converts optical energy into electrical charge. Determines spatial resolution and noise.

4. **Frame Grabber / Interface (½ mark):** Synchronises capture, performs analogue-to-digital conversion, and transmits digital frames to the processor via GigE / USB3 / Camera Link.

5. **Processor + Image Processing Software (1 mark):** Industrial PC or embedded SoC that runs algorithms for filtering, segmentation, feature extraction, and recognition — the actual "intelligence" of the vision system.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write *CCD* without mentioning *CMOS* (or vice-versa) and lose ½ mark. Always state **"CCD or CMOS"** to cover the syllabus breadth. Also, **"frame grabber"** is sometimes called *image acquisition card* — use the term from the textbook.

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]`
**Define image acquisition. Explain the terms *sampling*, *quantisation*, and *spatial resolution* with one relevant formula or unit for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Image acquisition (1 mark):** The process of converting a real, continuous 3-D scene into a discrete 2-D digital image by capturing the optical signal through a sensor and converting it to numerical data suitable for computer processing.

- **Sampling (1 mark):** The discretisation of the continuous image plane into a finite grid of $M \times N$ pixels. Governed by the **Nyquist criterion** $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 \times f_{\text{max, spatial}}$; failure causes aliasing.

- **Quantisation (½ mark):** The conversion of each pixel's continuous intensity into a discrete integer level. Number of levels $L = 2^k$, where $k$ = bits per pixel (e.g., **8 bits → 256 levels**).

- **Spatial Resolution (½ mark):** The density of pixels, typically expressed as $M \times N$ (e.g., **1920 × 1080**) or as line-pairs per millimetre (lp/mm). Higher values resolve finer details.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse *spatial resolution* (number of pixels) with *intensity resolution* (bits per pixel). Be precise — they are **independent** quantities.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, every Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain in detail the **elements of a vision sensor** used in robotics. Discuss the role of **CCD and CMOS sensors** with their relative merits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **different types of lighting techniques** used in machine vision with suitable applications. Why is **backlighting preferred for dimensional measurement**?

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the standard 5-block pipeline: *Illumination → Optics → Image Sensor → Frame Grabber → Processor.* (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical diagram.)

**Step 2 — Element-by-Element Explanation (3 marks):**
- *Illumination:* controlled light source; LED/laser/fluorescent; geometry choice crucial.
- *Optics:* lens of focal length $f$; thin-lens equation; field of view.
- *Image Sensor:* converts photons to electrons; CCD vs CMOS trade-off.
- *Frame Grabber:* digitiser + bus interface (GigE, USB3).
- *Processor:* runs image-processing algorithms.

**Step 3 — CCD vs CMOS Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---|---|
| Signal readout | Sequential | Per-pixel parallel |
| Noise | Low | Higher (improved) |
| Power | High | Low |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Scientific / low-light | Industrial / mobile |

> Add a concluding statement: *"For high-speed robotic pick-and-place, CMOS with global shutter is the modern industrial standard."* (½ mark)

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — List the four principal geometries (2 marks):** Back, Front/Diffuse, Ring, Dome. Add a fifth: *Structured (laser line) light* for completeness (½ mark bonus).

**Step 2 — Applications Table (3 marks):**

| Technique | Application |
|---|---|
| **Backlighting** | Dimensional measurement, edge detection of turned shafts |
| **Front / Diffuse** | Surface defect detection on painted panels |
| **Ring Lighting** | PCB component inspection |
| **Dome Lighting** | Curved, reflective objects (lenses, watch dials) |
| **Structured Light** | 3-D shape reconstruction, bin picking |

**Step 3 — Why Backlighting for Dimensional Measurement (2 marks):**
- Produces a **high-contrast silhouette** (object appears black on a bright background).
- Eliminates dependence on **surface colour and texture** — only the contour matters.
- Enables **simple global thresholding** with $T$ set midway between black and white — robust, fast, and computationally cheap.
- The **shadow boundary** corresponds exactly to the physical edge of the object, making sub-pixel edge detection highly accurate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When drawing the lighting diagram, students often *forget to label the object and camera position*. KTU evaluators explicitly deduct 1 mark for unlabelled diagrams.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the process of **image acquisition** in detail. Derive the relationship between **image size, bit depth, and storage requirement** with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With relevant equations, explain **image pre-processing techniques** such as **greyscale conversion, mean filtering, and median filtering**. Mention the role of **histogram equalisation** in image enhancement.

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Define image acquisition (1 mark):** The two-step conversion of a continuous scene into a discrete digital image via **sampling** (spatial discretisation) and **quantisation** (intensity discretisation).

**Step 2 — Sampling (1.5 marks):** Image plane sampled on $M \times N$ grid; Nyquist criterion $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$; under-sampling → aliasing.

**Step 3 — Quantisation (1.5 marks):** Each pixel assigned one of $L = 2^k$ intensity levels; $k = 8 \Rightarrow 256$ levels for standard greyscale.

**Step 4 — Storage Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Storage (bits)} = M \times N \times k
$$

$$
\text{Storage (bytes)} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8}
$$

$$
\text{Storage (MB)} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 10^6}
$$

**Numerical example (1 mark within Step 4):** For $M = 1920$, $N = 1080$, $k = 24$ (RGB):

$$
\text{Size} = \frac{1920 \times 1080 \times 24}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.78 \text{ MB per frame}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Greyscale Conversion (1.5 marks):** Combine RGB channels using the luminance formula:

$$
Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

Reduces 3 channels → 1 channel; saves 2/3 of the storage.

**Step 2 — Mean Filter (1.5 marks):** Each output pixel = arithmetic mean of a 3 × 3 neighbourhood.

$$
g[m, n] = \frac{1}{9}\sum_{i=-1}^{1}\sum_{j=-1}^{1} f[m+i, n+j]
$$

Smooths noise, **but blurs edges** — a major drawback for inspection tasks.

**Step 3 — Median Filter (2 marks):** Each output pixel = median of the 3 × 3 neighbourhood. **Excellent** at removing *salt-and-pepper* impulse noise while **preserving sharp edges** — preferred in robotics.

**Step 4 — Histogram Equalisation (2 marks):** Re-maps the intensity histogram to a uniform distribution across 0–255. The transformation is:

$$
s_k = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j) = 255 \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N}
$$

where $p_r(r_j) = n_j / N$ is the probability of intensity $r_j$. It **spreads the contrast** and reveals details hidden in dark or bright regions. **Application:** improving visibility of low-contrast weld seams before edge detection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B-b):** Students frequently confuse the **mean** and **median** filters. The cardinal rule: **mean = smoothing, blurs edges; median = removes salt-and-pepper, preserves edges.** A 1-mark loss is common for mixing up these two definitions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Vision)**

- ☐ **Five elements of a vision sensor:** **I**llumination → **O**ptics → **I**mage sensor → **F**rame grabber → **P**rocessor (mnemonic: *I-O-I-F-P*).
- ☐ **CCD vs CMOS:** CCD = low noise, scientific; CMOS = fast, low power, industrial. Default answer in robotics is **CMOS with global shutter**.
- ☐ **Lighting geometry rule of thumb:**
  - Dimensional measurement → **Backlight**
  - Surface defects → **Front / Diffuse**
  - Curved reflective → **Dome**
  - PCB / co-axial inspection → **Ring**
  - 3-D profile → **Structured (laser) light**
- ☐ **Thin-lens equation:** $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$ — always used for FOV/DOF calculations.
- ☐ **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size} \times \text{Object Distance}}{\text{Focal Length}}$. Resolution per pixel = $\text{FOV} / \text{pixel count}$.
- ☐ **Image storage equation:** $\text{Size (bytes)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8}$. For Full-HD 24-bit RGB → **0.78 MB per frame**.
- ☐ **Sampling theorem (Nyquist):** $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 f_{\text{max, spatial}}$. Violation → **aliasing**.
- ☐ **Quantisation levels:** $L = 2^k$ (e.g., $k = 8 \Rightarrow 256$ greys).
- ☐ **Greyscale conversion:** $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ (ITU-R BT.601 standard — **memorise the constants 0.299, 0.587, 0.114**).
- ☐ **Filters:** Mean (smooths, blurs edges) vs Median (removes salt-and-pepper, preserves edges). Robotics usually uses **median**.
- ☐ **Thresholding:** Global (Otsu's) for uniform light; Adaptive for uneven light.
- ☐ **Edge detectors in order of robustness:** **Roberts < Prewitt ≤ Sobel < Laplacian < Canny.** **Canny is the gold standard.**
- ☐ **Blob features:** Area, Centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$, Perimeter, Bounding Box, Hu Moments.
- ☐ **Standard numerical example to remember:** Full-HD 24-bit frame = **0.78 MB**; 1.3 MP 10-bit GigE ≈ **61 fps max**.
- ☐ **Examiner's favourite trick questions:** *(a)* "Difference between spatial and intensity resolution." *(b)* "Why is global thresholding unsuitable under uneven illumination?" *(c)* "Compare CCD and CMOS." Prepare crisp 3-sentence answers for all three.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** Always pair a *conceptual explanation* with a *labelled block diagram or a numerical example*. Answers that do both consistently score **15–25 % higher** than text-only answers, as confirmed by board-exam valuation patterns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Sensors; Actuators for Robots - Classification - Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection of Sensors; Actuators for Robots - Classification - Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Sensors and Actuators in Robotics?

In the architecture of any robotic system, **sensors** and **actuators** form the two fundamental bridges between the digital intelligence (controller) and the physical world. They are, in essence, the *eyes, ears, and muscles* of a robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Sensor:**
> A **sensor** is a transducer that detects a physical phenomenon (light, force, position, temperature, proximity, etc.) from the robot's internal state or external environment and converts it into an electrical signal suitable for measurement, display, or control input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Actuator:**
> An **actuator** is a power-conversion device that accepts a control command (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic) from the controller and produces a physical output — usually **linear force**, **rotational torque**, or **angular displacement** — to move the robot's links and end-effector.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a human arm:

- **Sensors** = nerves in your fingertips, eyes, skin (proprioception). They *sense* heat, pressure, position, distance.
- **Actuators** = your biceps and triceps (muscles). They *act* to produce force and motion when the brain commands them.

The robot controller is the **brain**. Without sensors, it is blind; without actuators, it is paralyzed. Selecting the *right* combination is what differentiates a clumsy toy from an industrial-grade robotic arm.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Sensors = "Sense" (Input), Actuators = "Act" (Output). Always remember the directional flow: Environment → Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Environment.

## 1.3 The Selection Problem — Why It Matters

When designing a robot, the student must justify *why* a particular sensor or actuator is chosen. KTU examiners award marks to students who explicitly link their selection to **performance requirements** (payload, speed, accuracy, environment, cost).

The five selection pillars (commonly tested) are:

1. **Range & Resolution** — How much must it measure, and how finely?
2. **Accuracy & Repeatability** — How close to the true value, and how consistently?
3. **Bandwidth / Response Time** — How fast can it react to a command or change?
4. **Power, Force & Torque Density** — Can the actuator deliver the required load?
5. **Environmental Compatibility** — Will it survive dust, heat, water, or explosive atmospheres?

## 1.4 High-Level Classification Snapshot

**Sensors in Robotics (KTU Module 2 scope):**

| Class | Examples | Measured Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal (Proprioceptive)** | Encoders, resolvers, tachometers, joint position sensors | Position, velocity |
| **External (Exteroceptive)** | Vision cameras, ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, force/torque | Distance, shape, contact |
| **Contact / Non-contact** | Limit switches, proximity sensors, tactile arrays | Touch, presence |

**Actuators in Robotics (KTU Module 2 scope):**

| Class | Energy Domain | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric** | Electrical → Mechanical | Precision, assembly, clean rooms |
| **Hydraulic** | Fluid pressure → Mechanical | Heavy payload, forging, mining |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air → Mechanical | Pick-and-place, food industry, simple motion |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Selection — Theoretical Foundation

A sensor is characterized by a **transfer function** that maps the physical input $x$ to the electrical output $y$:

$$y = f(x) = K \cdot x + b$$

where:
- $K$ = **sensitivity** (V/unit or A/unit)
- $b$ = **offset / bias** (output when input is zero)

### 2.1.1 Static Characteristics (the "S.T.A.B.L.E." mnemonic)

- **S**ensitivity ($K$) — Slope of the calibration curve
- **T**hreshold — Smallest detectable input change
- **A**ccuracy — Closeness to true value (in % of Full Scale)
- **B**andwidth — Frequency range over which it responds faithfully
- **L**inearity — Deviation from a straight line
- **E**rror — Hysteresis, drift, nonlinearity combined

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Characteristics

For time-varying inputs, the sensor is modeled as a first-order or second-order system. The most common is the **first-order transfer function**:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** (seconds). A small $\tau$ = fast response (essential for high-speed robot joints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **resonant frequency** of a sensor must be at least **3 to 5 times** the highest input frequency you intend to measure — otherwise, the output will be severely distorted. This is the famous "five-times rule" in instrumentation.

## 2.2 Actuator Selection — Theoretical Foundation

Actuators convert **energy** into **mechanical work**. The governing physics differs by class, but all share the same first-principle relationship:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = F \cdot v$$

where $T$ is torque (N·m), $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s), $F$ is force (N), and $v$ is linear velocity (m/s).

### 2.2.1 Electric Actuators

The dominant class. Energy conversion: **electrical → magnetic → mechanical**.

**Key Equation — DC Motor Torque:**

$$T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$$

where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (N·m/A) and $I_a$ is the armature current (A).

**Back-EMF Equation:**

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

The motor's electrical input equation (KVL of armature loop):

$$V_a = I_a \cdot R_a + E_b = I_a \cdot R_a + K_e \cdot \omega$$

**Mechanical Power Developed (Air-Gap Power):**

$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega = K_t \cdot I_a \cdot \omega$$

**Servo Motor Block (closed-loop):** Combines a DC/AC motor + encoder + controller. Used in **~80% of modern industrial robots** (e.g., 6-DOF arms in automotive assembly).

**Stepper Motor:** Moves in discrete *steps* (e.g., 1.8° per step). Open-loop position control. Used in 3D printers, CNC auxiliary axes.

### 2.2.2 Hydraulic Actuators

Energy conversion: **fluid pressure → mechanical force/torque**.

**Fundamental Force Equation (Linear Cylinder):**

$$F = P \cdot A - P_{back} \cdot A_{rod} - F_{friction}$$

For a double-acting cylinder with a single rod (asymmetric):

$$F_{ext} = P_1 \cdot A_1 - P_2 \cdot A_2$$

where $A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ is the piston (head-side) area, and $A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ is the rod-side area.

**Flow Rate → Velocity Relationship:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Hydraulic Motor Power (using volumetric and mechanical efficiencies):**

$$P_{out} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot P_{in} \cdot Q_{in} - P_{loss}$$

**Pascal's Law (the principle itself):**

$$P = \frac{F}{A} \Rightarrow F = P \cdot A$$

**Advantages of Hydraulic:**
- Very high **power-to-weight ratio** (up to 50× better than electric)
- High force/torque (industrial presses can deliver >10,000 N)
- Stiff and precise at low speeds
- Self-lubricating

**Disadvantages:**
- Leakage risk
- Requires pump, reservoir, valves, filtration
- Noisy; messy
- Slow response compared to electric

### 2.2.3 Pneumatic Actuators

Energy conversion: **compressed air pressure → mechanical motion**.

**Force Equation (similar to hydraulic but at much lower pressure):**

$$F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$$

Typical working pressure: **4 to 8 bar** (vs. hydraulic's 100–350 bar).

**Air Compressibility (critical difference from hydraulic):**

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Because air is compressible (bulk modulus ~$\mathbf{1.4 \times 10^5}$ times lower than hydraulic oil), pneumatic systems are **springy, less stiff, and less precise** — but they are **fast, clean, and cheap**.

**Festo / SMC-type pneumatic cylinders** dominate the packaging and food industry.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Golden Rule:** Memorize the units in **bold** below. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 mark per missing/wrong unit.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables & Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Force from Pressure | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P$ in Pa or N/m², $A$ in m², $F$ in **N** | Cylinder area typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ m² |
| 2 | Piston Area | $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ in **m** | Common bore: 25–100 mm |
| 3 | Pneumatic Force (with efficiency) | $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ dimensionless, ~0.85 | $P$ typically **4–8 bar** |
| 4 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = Q / A$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m², $v$ in **m/s** | $Q$ typical: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ m³/s |
| 5 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $K_t$ in **N·m/A**, $I_a$ in **A** | Industrial servo: 1–100 N·m |
| 6 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ in **V·s/rad**, $\omega$ in **rad/s** | $K_e = K_t$ in SI units |
| 7 | Motor Armature Equation | $V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$ | $V_a$ in **V**, $R_a$ in **Ω** | Steady-state vs transient |
| 8 | Mechanical Power | $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in **W** | Industrial robot joint: 100 W–15 kW |
| 9 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | $\Delta P$ in **Pa**, $Q$ in m³/s, $P$ in **W** | 1 kW = 1 bar·L/s × 100 |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (fluid in pipes) | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\mu$ in Pa·s | Turbulent if $Re > 4000$ |
| 11 | Sensor Sensitivity | $K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | $K$ in **V/unit** of input | Higher $K$ = better resolution |
| 12 | First-Order Time Response | $y(t) = K \cdot x \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $\tau$ in **seconds** | Settling time $t_s \approx 4\tau$ |
| 13 | Air Bulk Modulus | $K_{air} \approx 1.4 \times 10^5$ Pa (isothermal) | $K_{air}$ in **Pa** | Hydraulic oil: ~$1.6 \times 10^9$ Pa |
| 14 | Gear Reduction Effect | $T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$ | $N$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = gear efficiency | $N$ in robotics: 50–200 |
| 15 | Resonant Frequency (sensor) | $f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | $k$ stiffness, $m$ mass | Must be $\geq 5 \times$ input freq |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Each System Dominates

| Industry / Application | Preferred Actuator | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive body welding | **Hydraulic** | High force, stiffness |
| PCB assembly, clean rooms | **Electric servo** | Precision, no leakage |
| Food packaging | **Pneumatic** | Clean, fast, safe |
| Mars rover / space arm | **Electric (BLDC)** | Vacuum compatibility, low power |
| CNC milling | **Electric servo + spindle** | Speed & torque density |
| Forging / die casting | **Hydraulic** | Extreme force |
| Pick-and-place, low cost | **Pneumatic** | Simple, cheap, reliable |
| Humanoid robot | **Electric (BLDC + harmonic drive)** | Compact, controllable |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Delivered by a Double-Acting Pneumatic Cylinder

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore $D = 50$ mm and a rod diameter $d = 20$ mm. The supply pressure is $P_s = 6$ bar, and the back pressure on the rod side is $P_{back} = 1$ bar. Calculate the **theoretical extension force** $F_{ext}$ and **retraction force** $F_{ret}$.

**Step 1: Convert all quantities to SI units.**

- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050$ m
- $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020$ m
- $P_s = 6$ bar = $6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_{back} = 1$ bar = $1 \times 10^5$ Pa

**Step 2: Compute the piston areas.**

Head-side (piston) area $A_1$:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4}$$

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Rod-side annular area $A_2$:

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.050)^2 - (0.020)^2)}{4}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi \times (0.0025 - 0.0004)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0021}{4} = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the theoretical extension force.**

During extension, the full piston area is pressurized at $P_s$ and the rod side is vented to atmosphere ($P_{atm} \approx 0$ gauge, so we use the net pressure):

$$F_{ext} = P_s \cdot A_1 - P_{atm} \cdot A_2$$

$$F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) - 0$$

$$F_{ext} = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Compute the theoretical retraction force.**

During retraction, the rod side is pressurized and the head side is vented. We must subtract the back-pressure effect:

$$F_{ret} = P_s \cdot A_2 - P_{back} \cdot A_1$$

$$F_{ret} = (6 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.6493 \times 10^{-3}) - (1 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{ret} = 989.6 - 196.35 = 793.25 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: State the conclusion.**

The cylinder can push with **1178 N** but can only pull with **793 N** — a critical asymmetry in design. Robotic engineers use this to decide whether a single-rod cylinder is acceptable, or whether a double-rod (symmetric) cylinder is needed for balanced motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in robotics:** In a robotic arm, joint actuators are often single-rod cylinders. The asymmetry in push/pull force is one reason **electric servos with harmonic drives** dominate modern robotics — they provide symmetric, controllable torque in both directions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Speed of a DC Servo Motor under Load

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A DC servo motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1.0$ $\Omega$, torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, and applied voltage $V_a = 24$ V. A constant load torque $T_L = 0.5$ N·m is applied. Find the **steady-state armature current** $I_a$ and **angular velocity** $\omega$.

**Step 1: At steady state, motor torque equals load torque (Newton's 2nd law for rotation).**

$$T_m = T_L \Rightarrow K_t \cdot I_a = T_L$$

**Step 2: Solve for the armature current.**

$$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{0.5}{0.05} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Write the armature circuit equation.**

$$V_a = I_a R_a + E_b = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$$

**Step 4: Solve for angular velocity $\omega$.**

$$K_e \omega = V_a - I_a R_a$$

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$\omega = \frac{24 - (10)(1.0)}{0.05} = \frac{24 - 10}{0.05} = \frac{14}{0.05} = 280 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to RPM for a more practical engineering number.**

$$N = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 280 \times 9.5493 \approx 2673.8 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 7: Conclusion.** The motor settles at **10 A** armature current and **280 rad/s ≈ 2674 RPM** under the given load.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always convert to RPM in final answers. KTU evaluators like engineering-friendly units, and you earn a "presentation mark" by giving a real-world feel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydraulic Cylinder Extension Time

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm and stroke $L = 0.5$ m. Oil is supplied at flow rate $Q = 8$ L/min. Find the **time to fully extend** the cylinder.

**Step 1: Compute the piston area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert flow rate to SI.**

$$Q = 8 \text{ L/min} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.3333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute extension velocity.**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.3333 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02652 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute time to cover the stroke.**

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.5}{0.02652} = 18.85 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.** The cylinder takes **≈18.85 seconds** to extend fully.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using $Q$ in L/min and $A$ in m² without converting $Q$ to m³/s. This single mistake causes a factor of 60,000 error in the final answer. KTU examiners are strict about units.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Types

| Sensor Type | Physical Principle | Range | Resolution | Bandwidth | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | Resistive divider | 0–360° | High (μm) | Low–Medium | Joint angle (educational robots) |
| **Optical Encoder** | Light + slotted disk | Unlimited (incremental) | Very high (0.001°) | Very high | Industrial robot joints |
| **Resolver** | Electromagnetic induction | 0–360° (absolute) | Medium | Very high | Harsh environment (military, space) |
| **LVDT** | Mutual induction | ±1 mm to ±500 mm | Sub-μm | High | Linear position, precision slides |
| **Ultrasonic** | Time-of-flight echo | 0.02–10 m | cm-level | Low | Obstacle avoidance, mobile robots |
| **IR Proximity** | Reflected IR light | 0.01–0.5 m | mm-level | High | Short-range detection |
| **Strain Gauge** | Resistance change | 0–5000 μ-strain | μ-strain | Medium | Force / torque / tactile sensing |
| **Vision (CMOS/CCD)** | Photon → pixel intensity | Field-of-view | Pixel-level | Medium | Object recognition, inspection |
| **Gyroscope (MEMS)** | Coriolis force | ±2000°/s | 0.01°/s | High | IMU, balancing robots |
| **Tachometer** | Voltage proportional to rpm | 0–10,000 rpm | 1 rpm | High | Motor speed feedback |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Classes

| Property | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | Electrical mains / battery | Pump + reservoir + oil | Compressor + air reservoir |
| **Energy density (W/kg)** | 50–200 | 500–2000 | 50–100 |
| **Force/pressure** | Limited (motor torque) | Very high (up to 700 bar) | Low–Medium (4–8 bar) |
| **Stiffness** | Medium | Very high | Low (compressible air) |
| **Precision** | Very high (encoder feedback) | High | Low–Medium |
| **Speed** | High | Medium | High |
| **Cleanliness** | Excellent | Poor (oil leaks) | Good (only air) |
| **Noise** | Low | High | High (exhaust) |
| **Cost (initial)** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (filters, seals) | Medium (air drying) |
| **Safety in explosive env.** | Good | Poor (flammable oil) | Good (no spark, but pressure) |
| **Typical robot use** | 80% of all robots | Heavy industry (forging, mining) | Pick-and-place, packaging |
| **Control complexity** | Moderate (PWM, PID) | High (servo valves, pumps) | Low (on/off or proportional valve) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Robotic System with Sensors and Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Human Operator / Program] --> B[Controller CPU / PLC]
    C[Internal Sensors: Encoders, Tachometers] --> B
    D[External Sensors: Vision, Force, Proximity] --> B
    B --> E[Driver / Power Electronics]
    E --> F[Actuator: Electric or Hydraulic or Pneumatic]
    F --> G[Robot Link and Joint]
    G --> H[End Effector and Environment]
    H --> D
    G --> C
    B -.Feedback.-> I[Display and HMI]
```

**Reading Guide:** Solid arrows show forward control flow (command signal). The dashed return path represents the closed-loop feedback that makes the system *responsive* rather than *blind*.

## 4.2 Sensor Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: What must be measured] --> S1{Is it a position}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Is it linear or angular}
    S2 -- Linear --> S3[Use LVDT or Linear Encoder]
    S2 -- Angular --> S4{Is environment harsh}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Use Resolver]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Use Optical Encoder]
    S1 -- No --> S7{Is it a force or torque}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Use Strain Gauge based Load Cell]
    S1 -- No --> S9{Is it distance to object}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10{Range less than 0.5 m}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Use IR Proximity Sensor]
    S10 -- No --> S12{Range less than 10 m}
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Use Ultrasonic Sensor]
    S12 -- No --> S14[Use LiDAR or Stereo Vision]
    S1 -- No --> S15{Is it an image}
    S15 -- Yes --> S16[Use CMOS or CCD Camera]
```

**Reading Guide:** This is a simplified decision tree for KTU Module 2 scope. In practice, you would also add cost, weight, and bandwidth as constraints.

## 4.3 Actuator Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Start: Define Payload and Speed] --> T1{Is payload above 100 kg}
    T1 -- Yes --> T2{Is precision below 1 mm}
    T2 -- Yes --> T3[Use Hydraulic Actuator]
    T2 -- No --> T4[Use Hydraulic or Heavy Electric]
    T1 -- No --> T5{Is cleanroom or explosion safe needed}
    T5 -- Yes --> T6[Use Electric Servo or Stepper]
    T5 -- No --> T7{Is motion simple on off}
    T7 -- Yes --> T8[Use Pneumatic Cylinder]
    T7 -- No --> T9[Use Electric Servo with Encoder]
    T6 --> T10[Apply Harmonic or Planetary Gear]
    T9 --> T10
    T10 --> T11[Select Driver and Controller]
```

**Reading Guide:** The decision flows from the **most demanding requirement** (payload, precision, environment) down to the actuator class. The final step (T10–T11) is a *standard follow-up* for any robot arm: gear reduction + driver electronics + closed-loop controller.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — DC Servo Motor Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Position] --> SUM1{Comparator}
    FB[Encoder Feedback] --> SUM1
    SUM1 -- Error e --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID -- Control Voltage u --> PWM[PWM Driver]
    PWM -- V applied to motor --> M[DC Servo Motor]
    M -- Rotates shaft --> G[Gearbox and Robot Joint]
    G --> FB
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the textbook architecture used in *every* industrial robot joint (KUKA, ABB, FANUC). The encoder is the *sensor*; the motor is the *actuator*; the PID is the *brain* glue. Note the closed loop — the *defining feature* of a servo system.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Servo System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HR[Reference Position] --> S2{Comparator}
    HFB[LVDT or Encoder] --> S2
    S2 -- Error --> HCV[Servo Valve Controller]
    HCV --> HSV[Proportional or Servo Valve]
    HSV --> HFC[Hydraulic Cylinder or Motor]
    HFC --> HL[Robot Link and Load]
    HL --> HFB
    HP[Pump and Reservoir] --> HSV
    HFC --> HRT[Return to Tank]
```

**Reading Guide:** The pump supplies a constant high-pressure flow; the *servo valve* modulates it. This is how the Boeing 747 wing flap or a Caterpillar excavator arm is controlled.

## 4.6 Pneumatic System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PR[Air Compressor] --> PR1[Receiver Tank]
    PR1 --> FRL[Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    FRL --> DC[Directional Control Valve]
    DC -- Extend --> PC[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    DC -- Retract --> PC
    PC --> RL[Robot Joint]
    PR1 --> RL
```

**Reading Guide:** The **FRL** unit is mandatory in any pneumatic robot — *filter* removes water, *regulator* sets pressure, *lubricator* adds oil mist for cylinder life. KTU students often forget the FRL; this costs a full mark in design questions.
