# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator in a robotic system. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

A **sensor** is a device that *detects* a physical quantity (position, force, light) from the robot's internal or external environment and converts it into an electrical signal readable by the controller. Example: an **optical encoder** that measures joint angle.

An **actuator** is a device that *converts* control energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic) into mechanical motion (linear force or rotational torque) to move the robot. Example: a **DC servo motor** driving a robot arm link.

In short: sensors provide **input** to the controller; actuators receive the controller's **output** and physically move the robot. Together, they form the perception–action loop that defines robotics.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Hint (KTU style):** 1 mark for sensor definition + example; 1 mark for actuator definition + example; 1 mark for the perception–action relationship statement.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**List any three selection criteria for sensors used in robotics. Briefly explain why each is important.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Range and Resolution** — The sensor's measurement range must cover the full motion of the joint, and its resolution must be finer than the smallest position increment required. For a robot arm needing 0.1 mm precision, an LVDT or high-resolution encoder is mandatory.

2. **Bandwidth (Response Time)** — The sensor's frequency response must be at least 3–5× faster than the joint's maximum motion frequency. A slow sensor introduces phase lag and degrades closed-loop stability.

3. **Environmental Compatibility** — The sensor must withstand the operating environment (temperature, dust, moisture, vibration, EMI). In a welding robot, encoders must be EMI-shielded; in a food robot, they must be IP67-rated and washable.

> [!NOTE]
> Alternative valid criteria: accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, sensitivity, cost, size/weight, output type (analog vs digital). Any three with explanation fetch full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** Classify the different types of **actuators used in robotics**. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC servo motor** as an actuator for a robot joint. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A **pneumatic cylinder** has bore $D = 40$ mm, rod diameter $d = 16$ mm, supply pressure $P_s = 5$ bar, and back pressure $P_b = 1$ bar. Determine:
  (i) the theoretical extension force, and
  (ii) the theoretical retraction force.

Neglect friction. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Classification of Actuators (3 marks):**

Robotic actuators are classified by the energy domain they convert from:

1. **Electric Actuators** — convert electrical energy to mechanical motion.
   - *Examples:* DC servo motor, AC servo motor, stepper motor, BLDC motor, solenoid.

2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — convert pressurized hydraulic oil energy to motion.
   - *Examples:* hydraulic cylinder (linear), hydraulic motor (rotary), hydraulic rotary actuator.

3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — convert compressed air energy to motion.
   - *Examples:* pneumatic cylinder, pneumatic motor, air muscle (McKibben).

4. **Other / Emerging** — piezoelectric, shape-memory alloy (SMA), electroactive polymer (EAP). *(1 bonus sentence for KTU edge marks.)*

**Working of DC Servo Motor (4 marks):**

A DC servo motor is a closed-loop position/speed controlled DC motor. It is the workhorse of modern industrial robots (e.g., 6-DOF arms in automotive assembly).

**Components:**
- Permanent magnet stator (provides fixed magnetic field)
- Armature rotor (carries current $I_a$)
- **Encoder or resolver** (provides shaft-position feedback)
- Driver / amplifier (typically a PWM H-bridge)
- PID controller (computes the error signal)

**Working Principle:**
1. The controller receives a **reference position** (setpoint) from the program.
2. The **encoder** measures the *actual* shaft position and feeds it back to the comparator.
3. The comparator generates an **error signal** $e(t) = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$.
4. The **PID controller** processes $e(t)$ and outputs a control voltage $u(t)$.
5. The **PWM driver** amplifies $u(t)$ and applies armature voltage $V_a$ to the motor.
6. The **motor torque** $T_m = K_t I_a$ accelerates the shaft.
7. The **gearbox** reduces speed and multiplies torque to drive the robot joint.
8. As the actual position approaches the reference, the error reduces and the motor settles.

**Key Equations (cite in your answer):**

$$T_m = K_t I_a \qquad V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega \qquad P_m = T_m \omega$$

**Block Diagram (must be drawn in exam):**

```
Setpoint → Comparator ← Encoder (on shaft)
            ↓
         PID Controller
            ↓
        PWM Driver
            ↓
       DC Servo Motor → Gearbox → Robot Joint
            ↑                              ↓
            └────── Encoder ←──────────────┘
```

**Advantages of DC Servo for Robotics:** high precision, fast response, easy to control via PWM, compatible with digital controllers, well-established PID tuning theory.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $D = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- $d = 16$ mm $= 0.016$ m
- $P_s = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_b = 1$ bar $= 1 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Friction = 0 (neglected)

**Step 1: Compute Piston Areas (1 mark)**

Head-side area $A_1$:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.040)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Rod-side annular area $A_2$:

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.0016 - 0.000256)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.001344}{4} = 1.0556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Extension Force (3 marks)**

During extension, full piston area $A_1$ is pressurized at $P_s$, and the rod side is at atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm} = 0$ gauge):

$$F_{ext} = P_s \cdot A_1 - P_{atm} \cdot A_2$$

$$F_{ext} = (5 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.2566 \times 10^{-3}) - 0$$

$$F_{ext} = 628.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Retraction Force (3 marks)**

During retraction, the rod-side annular area $A_2$ is pressurized, and the head side has the back pressure $P_b$:

$$F_{ret} = P_s \cdot A_2 - P_b \cdot A_1$$

$$F_{ret} = (5 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.0556 \times 10^{-3}) - (1 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.2566 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{ret} = 527.8 - 125.66 = 402.14 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 628.3 \text{ N} \quad ; \quad F_{ret} = 402.14 \text{ N}}$$

**Observations:** The cylinder pushes 1.56× harder than it pulls — a typical asymmetry for a single-rod cylinder. In robotics, if bidirectional symmetric force is needed, a double-rod or tandem cylinder is used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa** (factor of $10^5$). This single mistake costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $A_1$ instead of $A_2$** during retraction. Always read the question: during *retraction*, only the annular area is pressurized.
> 3. **Missing the back-pressure term** $P_b \cdot A_1$ in the retraction force. The examiner explicitly gives $P_b = 1$ bar; you must use it.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** N (Newton) must be stated, or 0.5 mark is deducted.
> 5. **Not mentioning the asymmetry** (push vs pull) in the conclusion — this is a "presentation mark" differentiator at the 14-mark level.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **hydraulic servo system** used in a robotic arm. Mention its advantages over electric and pneumatic systems. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A **hydraulic cylinder** with bore $D = 100$ mm and stroke $L = 0.6$ m is supplied with oil at flow rate $Q = 12$ L/min. Find the time taken to fully extend the cylinder. If the supply pressure is $P = 100$ bar, calculate the theoretical force and the hydraulic power delivered. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A hydraulic servo system uses pressurized hydraulic oil to drive a linear cylinder or rotary motor in a robot. The system is closed-loop, meaning the cylinder's position is continuously monitored and corrected to match a reference command.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Setpoint θ_ref → Comparator ← Position Sensor (LVDT/Encoder on cylinder)
                       ↓
                   Error Signal e
                       ↓
              Servo Amplifier (PID)
                       ↓
        Electrohydraulic Servo Valve
                       ↓
    High-pressure oil (from pump) → Hydraulic Cylinder
                                          ↓
                                    Robot Link
                                          ↓
                                Position Sensor (feedback)
```

**Working (2 marks):**

1. The controller sends a **reference position** (setpoint) for the cylinder.
2. The **position sensor** (LVDT or linear encoder) measures the actual rod position.
3. The **comparator** computes the error $e = x_{ref} - x_{actual}$.
4. The **servo amplifier** (typically a PID) processes the error and produces a valve command voltage.
5. The **electrohydraulic servo valve** modulates the high-pressure oil flow from the pump to the cylinder's chambers proportionally to the error.
6. The cylinder extends or retracts, moving the robot link. The pump continuously supplies oil at high pressure (typically 100–350 bar) from a reservoir; spent oil returns via a filter and cooler.
7. As the rod approaches the setpoint, the error decreases, the valve closes partially, and the cylinder settles.

**Advantages over Electric and Pneumatic (1 mark):**

- **vs. Electric:** Higher force/power density (up to 50×), better stiffness, no overheating of motors.
- **vs. Pneumatic:** Much higher pressure (100 bar vs 6 bar) → higher force; incompressible oil → better precision; less springy.

**Disadvantages:** leakage, noise, requires pump and filtration, more expensive.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $L = 0.6$ m
- $Q = 12$ L/min
- $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5 = 1 \times 10^7$ Pa

**Step 1: Piston Area (1 mark)**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert Flow Rate to SI (1 mark)**

$$Q = 12 \text{ L/min} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Velocity of Piston (1 mark)**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02546 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Time to Extend (1 mark)**

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.6}{0.02546} = 23.57 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical Force (1 mark)**

$$F = P \cdot A = (1 \times 10^7) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 78{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 78.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Hydraulic Power (2 marks)**

$$P_h = \Delta P \cdot Q = (1 \times 10^7) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 2000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{t = 23.57 \text{ s} \quad ; \quad F = 78.54 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad P_h = 2 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Hydraulic question:**
> 1. **Unit conversion of flow rate:** 12 L/min = $2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, *not* 12 m³/s. Forgetting this is the #1 error in hydraulic questions.
> 2. **Pressure conversion:** 100 bar = $10^7$ Pa. Many students wrongly use $10^5$ Pa.
> 3. **Power formula:** Use $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$ (not $P = F \cdot v$) for hydraulic *input* power. $F \cdot v$ gives the *mechanical output* power, which is smaller due to $\eta < 1$.
> 4. **Stroke vs distance:** The piston travels the *full stroke* (0.6 m) for the extension time, not just half.
> 5. **Always state units in the box.** N, kN, s, kW, m/s — all must be present in the final answer line.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Sensors
- [ ] Sensor = *input device*; measures physical quantity → electrical signal.
- [ ] Actuator = *output device*; electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy → mechanical motion.
- [ ] Key static characteristics: **sensitivity, range, accuracy, linearity, hysteresis**.
- [ ] Key dynamic characteristic: **time constant $\tau$** and **bandwidth**.
- [ ] **5× rule:** sensor's resonant frequency ≥ 5× highest input frequency.
- [ ] **Potentiometer** = simple, low cost, low accuracy (used in educational robots).
- [ ] **Optical encoder** = high resolution, used in industrial robots.
- [ ] **Resolver** = harsh environment, absolute position.
- [ ] **LVDT** = linear position, sub-micrometer resolution.
- [ ] **Vision (CMOS/CCD)** = object recognition; computationally heavy.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic** = range sensing, low cost, cm-level resolution.
- [ ] **Strain gauge** = force/torque measurement; basis of tactile sensors.

### Actuators
- [ ] Electric = **electrical energy → motion**; uses DC servo, stepper, BLDC.
- [ ] Hydraulic = **pressurized oil → motion**; high force, high pressure (100–350 bar).
- [ ] Pneumatic = **compressed air → motion**; low pressure (4–8 bar), fast, clean.
- [ ] DC motor key equations: $T = K_t I_a$, $V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$.
- [ ] Hydraulic key equation: $F = P \cdot A$ (Pascal's law).
- [ ] Cylinder velocity: $v = Q / A$.
- [ ] Hydraulic power: $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$.
- [ ] **Push vs Pull:** Single-rod cylinder has *asymmetric* force — always compute both $F_{ext}$ and $F_{ret}$.
- [ ] Pneumatic force: $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$ (with efficiency).
- [ ] Pneumatic systems are *compressible* → springy, less precise than hydraulic.
- [ ] Hydraulic systems are *incompressible* → stiff, precise, but leak-prone.
- [ ] Electric servos dominate **80%** of modern robots due to precision and clean operation.
- [ ] Hydraulic dominates *heavy industry* (forging, mining, excavators).
- [ ] Pneumatic dominates *food, packaging, simple pick-and-place*.

### Exam Strategy Tips
- [ ] **Always convert units to SI first** (mm → m, bar → Pa, L/min → m³/s).
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** for actuator working questions (4–5 marks of easy score).
- [ ] **Always state the units** in the boxed final answer.
- [ ] **Always mention efficiency, friction, or back-pressure** as appropriate — this is the "presentation" differentiator.
- [ ] For comparison questions, use a **table format** with rows for each criterion — KTU examiners love structured answers.
- [ ] In derivation questions, write the **formula → substitution → answer** sequence; do not skip steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Their Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Robot Drive Systems?

In the context of KTU's **Fundamentals of Robotics (OERUT611 - Module 2)**, the topic *"Their Advantages and Disadvantages"* refers to the comparative study of the three principal **drive systems (actuators)** used in industrial robots to produce mechanical motion. These are:

1. **Hydraulic Drives** — Fluid power using incompressible liquids (typically oil).
2. **Pneumatic Drives** — Fluid power using compressible gases (typically compressed air).
3. **Electric Drives** — Electromechanical power using servo motors, stepper motors, and DC/AC actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *robot drive system* (or *actuator*) is the power-conversion element of a robot that transforms input energy (hydraulic, pneumatic, or electrical) into mechanical motion at the joints, thereby enabling the manipulator to perform its programmed task.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a human arm:
- The **biceps muscle** = an **electric drive** (precise, controlled, used for fine writing work).
- A **hydraulic press in a junkyard** lifting a car = a **hydraulic drive** (immense force, slow but powerful).
- A **pneumatic nail gun** on a construction site = a **pneumatic drive** (fast bursts of moderate force).

Each drive system is like a different "muscle type" for a robot, and the choice depends on the **payload**, **speed**, **precision**, and **environment** of the application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this comparison matters in KTU exams:** This topic is a **high-weightage comparison question** in Part B (14 marks). You will almost always be asked to compare two or all three drive systems in a structured table with real engineering justification — not just vague statements.

---

## 1.3 The Three Drive Systems at a Glance

| Drive Type | Energy Source | Typical Power | Common Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized oil (≈ 70–200 bar) | High (up to several kW per axis) | Heavy-payload robots (forging, palletizing) |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air (≈ 4–8 bar) | Low to Medium | Pick-and-place, packaging, simple assembly |
| **Electric** | Electrical energy (DC/AC servo) | Medium to High | Precision assembly, welding, painting, clean rooms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key standard metric to remember:**
> - **Power-to-Weight Ratio of Hydraulic Systems ≈ 10–100× higher than electric motors of equal size.**
> - **Compressed air cost is ~10× more expensive than electricity** for equivalent mechanical work, but the hardware is cheaper and safer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Drives — Theory of Operation

A hydraulic drive uses **Pascal's Law** to convert fluid pressure into linear or rotary motion.

**Pascal's Law (Fundamental Equation):**
$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Fluid pressure in **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**
- $F$ = Output force in **Newton (N)**
- $A$ = Piston cross-sectional area in **m²**

**Working Principle:**
1. An **electric motor** drives a **hydraulic pump**.
2. The pump draws oil from a **reservoir** and forces it through **servo valves**.
3. The pressurized oil acts on a **piston** or **hydraulic motor**, producing motion.
4. **Servo valves** modulate flow direction and rate, enabling closed-loop position control.

**Block Flow:** Electric Motor → Pump → Servo Valve → Actuator (Cylinder/Motor) → Robot Joint

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Hydraulic drives are preferred for **payloads above 50 kg** because the force $F = P \times A$ can be made very large simply by increasing the piston area $A$ at the same pressure $P$.

---

## 2.2 Pneumatic Drives — Theory of Operation

Pneumatic drives operate on the same principle as hydraulic drives, but use **compressible air** instead of oil.

**Ideal Gas Law (Operating Foundation):**
$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure of air
- $V$ = Volume of the air cylinder
- $n$ = Number of moles of air
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**

**Working Principle:**
1. An **air compressor** stores compressed air in a tank.
2. Air passes through a **filter-regulator-lubricator (FRL)** unit.
3. **Directional control valves (DCVs)** route air to the pneumatic cylinder/motor.
4. The expanding air drives the piston, producing linear or rotary motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key difference from hydraulic:** Because air is **compressible**, pneumatic systems exhibit **spring-like (compliant) behavior** and are difficult to control precisely for intermediate positions. This is why pneumatic robots are typically limited to **end-of-stroke (two-position) tasks** like clamping or sorting.

---

## 2.3 Electric Drives — Theory of Operation

Electric drives convert electrical energy directly into mechanical motion using electromagnetic principles.

**Torque Equation of a DC Servo Motor:**
$$\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Motor torque in **N·m**
- $K_t$ = Torque constant of the motor in **N·m/A**
- $I_a$ = Armature current in **Amperes (A)**

**Back-EMF Equation:**
$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Back electromotive force in **Volts (V)**
- $K_e$ = Voltage constant in **V·s/rad**
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity in **rad/s**

**Working Principle:**
1. A **servo controller** receives a position command from the robot's CPU.
2. The controller drives a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signal** to the **servo amplifier**.
3. The amplifier supplies controlled current to the **servo motor**.
4. An **encoder or resolver** provides position feedback, closing the control loop.
5. **Harmonic drives, RV reducers, or cycloidal gearboxes** reduce the high-speed low-torque output of the motor to the low-speed high-torque required at the robot joint.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pascal's Law (Force from pressure) | $F = P \cdot A$ | N, Pa, m² | Hydraulic force calculation |
| Pneumatic cylinder force (forward stroke) | $F = P \cdot A_{piston} - F_{friction}$ | N | Pneumatic gripper force |
| DC motor torque | $\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m, A | Electric drive sizing |
| Power of rotating actuator | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W, N·m, rad/s | Drive power selection |
| Stiffness of hydraulic system | $k_{hyd} \approx \infty$ (incompressible) | N/m | Why hydraulics are stiff |
| Stiffness of pneumatic system | $k_{pneu} = \frac{\gamma \cdot P \cdot A^2}{V}$ | N/m | Why pneumatics are compliant |
| Position error in servo | $e = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$ | rad | Electric drive accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note on Stiffness:** Hydraulic drives have effectively **infinite stiffness** because oil is nearly incompressible. Pneumatic drives have **low stiffness** because air compresses. This is THE most important reason hydraulic robots are used for heavy milling/precision machining of large parts.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic robots** dominate the **automotive forging, die-casting, and palletizing** industries.
- **Pneumatic robots** are ubiquitous in **food packaging, pharmaceutical pick-and-place, and small-parts assembly**.
- **Electric robots** (e.g., **FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa**) dominate **~70% of the global industrial robot market** due to precision, cleanliness, and ease of integration with digital controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Advantages and Disadvantages

### A. HYDRAULIC DRIVE SYSTEMS

**Advantages:**

1. **High Power-to-Weight Ratio:** Hydraulic actuators can deliver forces 10–100× higher than electric motors of the same mass. This is derived directly from Pascal's Law: $F = P \cdot A$.

2. **High Stiffness and Precision under Load:** Since hydraulic oil is essentially incompressible (bulk modulus $K \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ GPa), the system maintains its position precisely even under varying loads.

3. **Self-Lubricating:** The hydraulic fluid continuously lubricates internal components, extending service life.

4. **Fast Response for Large Inertias:** Hydraulic servo systems can move large payloads with high accelerations because the fluid transmits force almost instantaneously.

5. **Overload Protection:** Pressure-relief valves protect the system from mechanical overload.

6. **Smooth, Stepless Speed Control:** Flow control valves allow infinite variation of speed.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Leakage of Hydraulic Oil:** Even minor leaks cause contamination, fire hazards, and environmental damage.

2. **High Initial and Maintenance Cost:** Pumps, valves, seals, and filtration units are expensive and require regular servicing.

3. **Noise:** Hydraulic systems generate significant noise (typically 75–95 dB).

4. **Requires Auxiliary Equipment:** Pump, reservoir, filters, coolers, and piping add bulk and complexity.

5. **Temperature Sensitivity:** Viscosity changes with temperature affect performance.

6. **Not Suitable for Clean Rooms:** Oil contamination rules out use in semiconductor or pharmaceutical manufacturing.

---

### B. PNEUMATIC DRIVE SYSTEMS

**Advantages:**

1. **Low Cost:** Compressed air is cheap to produce; pneumatic components are inexpensive.

2. **Simple Design and Easy Maintenance:** Few moving parts, simple directional control valves.

3. **Clean and Safe:** No risk of oil contamination; air leakage is harmless.

4. **High Speed:** Pneumatic actuators can reach very high speeds (up to 1–2 m/s) — ideal for fast pick-and-place.

5. **Suitable for Hazardous Environments:** No spark risk, ideal for explosive atmospheres (with proper certification).

6. **Easy to Integrate with PLCs and digital controls** for two-position (binary) tasks.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Low Positioning Accuracy:** Air compressibility makes intermediate positions unstable.

2. **Low Force Output:** Limited by available air pressure (typically 6–8 bar), so cannot handle heavy loads.

3. **Noisy Operation:** Exhaust air creates significant noise (80–100 dB).

4. **High Energy Cost:** Compressing air is thermodynamically inefficient — only ~10–15% of input electrical energy is converted to useful mechanical work.

5. **Requires Air Treatment:** Moisture, dust, and oil in compressed air must be filtered via FRL units.

6. **Limited to On/Off or Simple Motion:** Not suitable for complex contour following or coordinated multi-axis motion.

---

### C. ELECTRIC DRIVE SYSTEMS

**Advantages:**

1. **High Precision and Repeatability:** With encoder feedback, positioning accuracy of ±0.01 mm is achievable.

2. **Clean and Quiet:** No oil leaks, no air exhaust, no noise pollution (typically < 60 dB).

3. **Wide Speed Range:** Modern servo motors operate smoothly from near-zero to 5000+ rpm.

4. **Excellent Controllability:** Compatible with advanced control algorithms (PID, fuzzy, adaptive).

5. **Energy Efficient:** 70–90% electrical-to-mechanical conversion efficiency.

6. **Low Maintenance:** No leaks, no seals, no filters to change.

7. **Suitable for Clean Rooms:** Used in semiconductor, pharmaceutical, and medical robotics.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Limited Payload Capacity for Same Size:** Electric motors have lower power-to-weight ratio than hydraulics.

2. **Heat Dissipation Issues:** High currents cause motor heating, requiring cooling systems.

3. **Gearbox Backlash:** Reducers introduce positioning error and require precision (and costly) units.

4. **Sensitive to Overload:** Motors can burn out if stalled; current limiters are essential.

5. **Lower Stiffness than Hydraulic:** Holding torque has limits under external disturbance.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Summary Matrix (For Exam Memorization)

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Pressurized oil | Compressed air | Electrical energy |
| **Typical Pressure** | 70–200 bar | 4–8 bar | N/A (voltage/current) |
| **Force/Torque Density** | Very High | Low | Medium–High |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | High (±0.05 mm) | Low (end-stop only) | Very High (±0.01 mm) |
| **Stiffness** | Very High | Low | High |
| **Speed** | Medium (0.5–1 m/s) | High (1–2 m/s) | Variable (0–5 m/s) |
| **Cleanliness** | Poor (oil leak risk) | Good (clean air) | Excellent |
| **Cost (Initial)** | High | Low | Medium–High |
| **Cost (Operating)** | Medium | High (energy inefficient) | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Low | Very Low |
| **Noise Level** | 75–95 dB | 80–100 dB | < 60 dB |
| **Typical Payload** | > 50 kg (up to 1000 kg) | < 10 kg | 1–500 kg |
| **Best For** | Heavy industry, forging | Pick-and-place, packaging | Precision assembly, welding, painting |

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Derivation (KTU Frequently Asked Type)

**Problem:** A hydraulic robot cylinder has a piston diameter of 50 mm. The system pressure is 100 bar. Calculate:
(a) The maximum theoretical output force.
(b) The actual force if the mechanical efficiency is 90%.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Given:**
- Piston diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- System pressure $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta = 90\% = 0.90$

**Step 1: Calculate Piston Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4}$$

$$A = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Pascal's Law to Get Theoretical Force**

$$F_{theoretical} = P \cdot A = 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{theoretical} = 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.635 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Apply Efficiency to Get Actual Force**

$$F_{actual} = \eta \cdot F_{theoretical} = 0.90 \times 19635$$

$$F_{actual} = 17671.5 \text{ N} \approx 17.67 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert bar to Pa using $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ before plugging into SI-unit formulas. This is a common KTU valuation mistake.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Why Hydraulic is Stiffer than Pneumatic

The **stiffness** $k$ of a pneumatic/hydraulic cylinder is defined as the force required per unit axial compression:

$$k = \frac{dF}{dx}$$

For a cylinder with piston area $A$, pressure $P$, and enclosed volume $V$:

**Compression relationship** (from bulk modulus $K$):

$$\Delta V = -\frac{V \cdot \Delta P}{K}$$

Since $\Delta V = A \cdot \Delta x$ and $\Delta F = A \cdot \Delta P$:

$$k = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta x} = \frac{A^2 \cdot K}{V}$$

**For hydraulics:** $K_{oil} \approx 2 \times 10^9$ Pa

**For pneumatics:** Effective $K_{air} \approx P \cdot \gamma$ where $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio (= 1.4 for air)

At $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$:

$$K_{air,eff} = 1.4 \times 6 \times 10^5 = 8.4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Stiffness Ratio:**

$$\frac{k_{hyd}}{k_{pneu}} = \frac{K_{oil}}{K_{air,eff}} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{8.4 \times 10^5} \approx 2380$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A hydraulic cylinder is approximately **2380 times stiffer** than a pneumatic cylinder of the same geometry. This is the **physical origin** of why hydraulic robots are chosen for precision heavy-load applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Three Drive Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Controller CPU] --> B{Drive System Selection}

    B -->|High Payload / Stiffness| C[Hydraulic Drive]
    B -->|Fast Pick-and-Place| D[Pneumatic Drive]
    B -->|Precision / Clean Room| E[Electric Drive]

    C --> C1[Electric Motor]
    C1 --> C2[Hydraulic Pump]
    C2 --> C3[Servo Valve]
    C3 --> C4[Hydraulic Cylinder / Motor]
    C4 --> C5[Robot Joint Output]

    D --> D1[Air Compressor]
    D1 --> D2[Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    D2 --> D3[Directional Control Valve]
    D3 --> D4[Pneumatic Cylinder / Motor]
    D4 --> D5[Robot Joint Output]

    E --> E1[Servo Controller / PWM]
    E1 --> E2[Servo Amplifier]
    E2 --> E3[Servo Motor DC/AC]
    E3 --> E4[Encoder / Resolver Feedback]
    E4 --> E5[Gear Reducer Harmonic / RV]
    E5 --> E6[Robot Joint Output]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Selecting a Robot Drive System] --> Q1{Payload Requirement?}

    Q1 -->|> 50 kg| Q2{Environment Clean?}
    Q1 -->|< 10 kg| Q3{Need On/Off Only?}
    Q1 -->|10 to 50 kg| Q4{Accuracy Required?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| Electric
    Q2 -->|No| Hydraulic

    Q3 -->|Yes| Pneumatic
    Q3 -->|No| Electric

    Q4 -->|> 0.1 mm| Electric
    Q4 -->|Within stroke| Pneumatic

    Electric[Choose ELECTRIC Drive]
    Hydraulic[Choose HYDRAULIC Drive]
    Pneumatic[Choose PNEUMATIC Drive]
```

## 4.3 Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hydraulic_Chain [Hydraulic Energy Path]
        H1[Electrical Energy] --> H2[Pump Mechanical Energy]
        H2 --> H3[Hydraulic Pressure Energy]
        H3 --> H4[Mechanical Output Force]
    end

    subgraph Pneumatic_Chain [Pneumatic Energy Path]
        P1[Electrical Energy] --> P2[Compressor Work]
        P2 --> P3[Pneumatic Pressure Energy]
        P3 --> P4[Mechanical Output Force]
    end

    subgraph Electric_Chain [Electric Energy Path]
        E1[Electrical Energy] --> E2[Magnetic Field Energy]
        E2 --> E3[Mechanical Rotation]
        E3 --> E4[Reduced Speed High Torque Output]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Diagram Reading:** Always emphasize in your answer the **number of energy conversion stages**. Electric drives have only **one conversion stage** (electrical → mechanical), giving them the highest efficiency. Hydraulic and pneumatic drives have **two conversion stages** each, with inherent losses at each stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of hydraulic drive systems used in robots.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of Hydraulic Drives:**
1. High power-to-weight ratio, enabling handling of very heavy payloads.
2. High stiffness due to the incompressibility of hydraulic oil, ensuring precise positioning under load.
3. Self-lubricating, leading to longer service life of internal components.

**Disadvantages of Hydraulic Drives:**
1. Oil leakage creates fire hazards, contamination, and environmental issues.
2. High initial cost and complex maintenance (filters, seals, pumps).
3. Not suitable for clean room or sterile environments due to oil contamination.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Compare pneumatic and electric drive systems in terms of accuracy, cost, and cleanliness.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pneumatic Drive | Electric Drive |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Low (limited to end-of-stroke positions due to air compressibility) | Very High (±0.01 mm achievable with encoder feedback) |
| **Cost** | Low initial cost, but high operating cost due to inefficient compression of air | Higher initial cost, but very low operating cost |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean (air leakage is harmless) | Excellent (no leaks, no exhaust, ideal for clean rooms) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write vague answers like "pneumatic is cheaper" without specifying **initial vs operating cost**. This loses 1 mark. Always mention **which cost** you are referring to.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of a hydraulic drive system used in industrial robots. List **four advantages** and **four disadvantages** of hydraulic drives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a pneumatic drive system. Compare it with hydraulic drive on the basis of stiffness, accuracy, and payload. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Working Principle of Hydraulic Drive System:**

1. An **electric motor** drives a **variable-displacement hydraulic pump**.
2. The pump draws hydraulic oil from a **reservoir** through a **suction strainer**.
3. The pressurized oil (typically 70–200 bar) is delivered to a **servo valve** (or proportional valve).
4. The servo valve modulates both the **direction** and **flow rate** of oil to the **hydraulic actuator** (cylinder for linear motion, hydraulic motor for rotary motion).
5. The actuator drives the **robot joint** through a mechanical linkage or gearbox.
6. A **linear variable differential transformer (LVDT)** or **encoder** provides position feedback to the **servo controller**, closing the loop.
7. **Accumulator** stores pressurized oil to handle peak demand and absorb pressure fluctuations.
8. **Pressure relief valves** protect the system from overpressure.

**Pascal's Law** governs the force generation:
$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Four Advantages:**
1. High power-to-weight ratio suitable for heavy payloads (> 50 kg).
2. Excellent stiffness due to incompressible oil.
3. Self-lubricating and smooth operation.
4. Built-in overload protection via pressure-relief valves.

**Four Disadvantages:**
1. Oil leakage is a major fire, contamination, and environmental hazard.
2. High initial and maintenance cost.
3. Requires bulky auxiliary equipment (pump, reservoir, filter, cooler).
4. Not suitable for clean room or sterile environments.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Working of Pneumatic Drive System (Block Diagram Description):**

1. **Air Compressor** draws ambient air and compresses it to 6–8 bar, stored in a **receiver tank**.
2. The compressed air passes through a **Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL)** unit:
   - Filter removes dust and moisture.
   - Regulator maintains constant downstream pressure.
   - Lubricator introduces fine oil mist to reduce wear.
3. The conditioned air is routed through **directional control valves (DCVs)** — typically 5/2 or 5/3 valves — to the **pneumatic cylinder** or **pneumatic motor**.
4. The expanding air drives the piston, producing linear/rotary motion at the robot joint.
5. **Limit switches or proximity sensors** detect end-of-stroke positions for sequencing.
6. **Throttle valves** regulate exhaust flow to control speed.

**Comparison with Hydraulic Drive:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| **Stiffness** | Very High (oil incompressible) | Low (air compressible) |
| **Accuracy** | High (±0.05 mm with servo valve) | Low (only end-of-stroke accurate) |
| **Payload** | Up to 1000 kg | Typically < 10 kg |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**(a)** Discuss the working of an electric drive system in robots with a block diagram. Mention the role of **encoders** and **harmonic drives** in such systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and operates at a pressure of 150 bar. If the mechanical efficiency of the system is 85%, calculate the **theoretical** and **actual** output force. Also, list any **two advantages of electric drives over hydraulic drives**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Working of Electric Drive System:**

1. The **robot controller (CPU)** generates a position reference signal $\theta_{ref}$.
2. The **servo controller** compares $\theta_{ref}$ with the actual position $\theta_{actual}$ from the encoder and computes the **error signal**:
$$e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$$
3. The error is processed via a **PID control algorithm**:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
4. The control output $u(t)$ is converted to a **PWM signal** sent to the **servo amplifier**.
5. The servo amplifier supplies a controlled armature current $I_a$ to the **DC/AC servo motor**.
6. The motor produces torque $\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$ and rotates at angular velocity $\omega$.
7. The high-speed low-torque output is reduced by the **harmonic drive** (or RV reducer) to a low-speed high-torque output at the joint.

**Role of Encoders:**
- Provide **real-time position feedback** for closed-loop control.
- Resolutions up to **23 bits (≈ 0.0001°)** enable sub-millimeter accuracy.
- Types: **Incremental encoders** (relative position) and **absolute encoders** (true position even after power off).

**Role of Harmonic Drives:**
- **Single-stage reduction ratios** of 30:1 to 320:1 in a compact package.
- **Zero backlash** ideal for precision robotics (repeatability ±5 arc-seconds).
- Composed of three components: **Wave Generator**, **Flexspline**, and **Circular Spline**.
- Enable **high torque capacity** in a small, lightweight form factor.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Piston diameter $d = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Operating pressure $P = 150 \text{ bar} = 150 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta = 85\% = 0.85$

**Step 1: Piston Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4}$$

$$A = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Force**

$$F_{theo} = P \cdot A = (1.5 \times 10^7) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{theo} = 75,398 \text{ N} \approx 75.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Actual Force**

$$F_{actual} = \eta \times F_{theo} = 0.85 \times 75,398$$

$$F_{actual} = 64,088 \text{ N} \approx 64.09 \text{ kN}$$

**Two Advantages of Electric Drives over Hydraulic Drives:**

1. **Cleanliness:** Electric drives have no oil leakage, making them suitable for clean rooms, food, and pharmaceutical industries.
2. **High Positioning Accuracy:** With encoder feedback, electric drives achieve ±0.01 mm accuracy versus ±0.05 mm for hydraulics.
3. **Lower Maintenance:** No filters, seals, or oil changes are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always show unit conversions** in numerical problems (mm → m, bar → Pa). Loss of 1 mark.
> - **Always state the formula before substituting** values. Loss of 1 mark if you directly compute.
> - **In comparison answers**, do not just list features — **state the engineering reason** (e.g., "Hydraulic is stiffer because oil is incompressible"). Vague statements lose half-mark per point.
> - **Mention efficiency explicitly** in numerical answers. Forgetting efficiency is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - **Do not confuse back-EMF constant $K_e$ with torque constant $K_t$** in motor derivations; in SI units, $K_e = K_t$ numerically but have different units.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Three robot drive systems:** Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric.
- [x] **Hydraulic** — uses Pascal's Law $F = P \cdot A$; high stiffness, high payload, oil-based, fire hazard.
- [x] **Pneumatic** — uses compressed air at 4–8 bar; clean, cheap, fast, but only end-of-stroke accurate.
- [x] **Electric** — uses servo motors + encoders + harmonic drives; high precision, clean, low maintenance.
- [x] **Power-to-Weight Ratio Order:** Hydraulic > Electric > Pneumatic.
- [x] **Positioning Accuracy Order:** Electric > Hydraulic > Pneumatic.
- [x] **Cleanliness Order:** Electric > Pneumatic > Hydraulic.
- [x] **Cost (Initial):** Hydraulic > Electric > Pneumatic.
- [x] **Cost (Operating):** Pneumatic > Hydraulic > Electric.
- [x] **Key equation:** $F = P \cdot A$ (Pascal's Law) for force calculation.
- [x] **Key equation:** $\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$ for DC motor torque.
- [x] **Key equation:** $k = \frac{A^2 K}{V}$ for stiffness of fluid cylinders.
- [x] **Harmonic drive reduction ratio:** 30:1 to 320:1 with zero backlash.
- [x] **Stiffness ratio hyd/pneumatic ≈ 2380×** (for same geometry, derived from bulk modulus).
- [x] **Hydraulic robots:** Payload > 50 kg, used in forging, die-casting, palletizing.
- [x] **Pneumatic robots:** Payload < 10 kg, used in pick-and-place, packaging.
- [x] **Electric robots:** Payload 1–500 kg, used in assembly, welding, painting, clean rooms.
- [x] **Always convert:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa before SI calculation.
- [x] **Always state:** initial cost vs operating cost separately in comparisons.
- [x] **Always mention:** the physical engineering reason behind each advantage (not just the advantage).
- [x] **PID equation:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ — recall for electric drive part-answers.
- [x] **Three big words to use in answers:** *incompressibility, compressibility, modularity, encoder feedback, power density, stiffness, repeatability*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Actuators - Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors and Their Drivers, AC Motors, Linear Actuators, Selection of Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Actuators - Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors and Their Drivers, AC Motors, Linear Actuators, Selection of Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Actuators in Robotics — The Formal Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (OERUT611 - Fundamentals of Robotics), an **Electric Actuator** is defined as a mechatronic energy-conversion device that transforms electrical energy (current and voltage) into controlled mechanical motion — either rotational (torque and angular displacement) or translational (linear force and displacement) — for the purpose of driving robot joints, grippers, end-effectors, wheels, and auxiliary mechanisms.

Electric actuators form the *musculoskeletal system* of every modern robot. They are preferred over hydraulic and pneumatic actuators in most industrial and service-robot applications because they offer:
- **High precision** (sub-arc-minute resolution with servo systems)
- **Clean operation** (no oil leakage, no compressed-air infrastructure)
- **Easy controllability** (variable voltage/frequency input)
- **Compact packaging** and high power-to-weight ratios

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):** This topic directly addresses **CO2** — *“Analyze and select appropriate actuators and drive systems for robotic applications.”* A 14-mark Part-B question is guaranteed in either the ESE (End Semester Examination) or the internal module test.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Actuators as the Muscles of a Robot

Imagine a human arm. The brain sends electrical signals down nerves, and the **muscles** contract to move the bones. In a robot:

| Biological Element | Robotic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Brain (decision-making) | Microcontroller / PLC |
| Nerve signal (electrical) | PWM / driver current command |
| Muscle (force generation) | **Electric Actuator (Motor)** |
| Bone (structural link) | Rigid link / arm segment |
| Joint (motion pivot) | Shaft / revolute joint |
| Skin feedback (touch) | Encoder, tachogenerator, current sensor |

A stepper motor is like a **muscle that moves in fixed, discrete jerks** — you tell it "step 5 times" and it does exactly that, no questions asked. A DC servo motor is like a **muscle paired with eyes (vision feedback)** — it continuously adjusts until the limb is exactly where you want it, even if the load changes.

---

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Electric Actuators Used in Robotics

```
Electric Actuators
├── Rotary Actuators
│   ├── Stepper Motors (open-loop, discrete position)
│   ├── Brushed DC Motors (open-loop speed/torque)
│   ├── DC Servo Motors (closed-loop precision)
│   ├── Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors
│   └── AC Motors (Induction & Synchronous)
└── Linear Actuators
    ├── Lead-screw / Ball-screw actuators
    ├── Voice-coil actuators
    ├── Linear stepper actuators
    └── Solenoid actuators
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this taxonomy matters in KTU exams:** The examiner can ask you to *compare* any two of these actuator types in tabular form (typically worth 7 marks). Memorize the comparison axes: **open vs. closed loop, precision, holding torque, cost, heat dissipation, and typical robot application.**

---

## 1.4 One-Paragraph "First-Read" Summary of Each Actuator

- **Stepper Motor:** A synchronous electric motor that rotates in **discrete angular steps** (e.g., **1.8°** per step) without requiring a position feedback sensor. Ideal for low-cost pick-and-place robots, 3D printers, and CNC machines.

- **Brushed DC Motor:** A direct-current motor with **brush-and-commutator** construction. Provides continuous rotation with speed proportional to supply voltage. Used in small mobile robots, toy vehicles, and educational kits.

- **DC Servo Motor:** A high-performance DC motor integrated with a **positional/velocity feedback sensor (encoder + tachogenerator)** and a closed-loop controller (typically PID). The industrial standard for robot joints requiring precision better than 0.1°.

- **AC Motor (Induction / Synchronous):** Robust, brushless motor that operates on AC supply or inverter-fed variable frequency. Used in heavy-payload industrial robot arms (e.g., 6-DOF arms handling >10 kg).

- **Linear Actuator:** A device that produces **linear (straight-line) motion** directly, without the need for a separate ballscrew conversion. Common in Cartesian gantry robots, pick-and-place Z-axes, and gripper-opening mechanisms.

---

## 1.5 Critical Terminology for the Board Exam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following terms appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE paper. Commit them to memory with their exact engineering definitions:

- **Step Angle (β):** The angular displacement produced by a stepper motor per input pulse. Unit: **degrees**. Typical values: **0.72°, 1.8°, 7.5°, 15°, 30°**.
- **Holding Torque (T_h):** The maximum static torque a stepper motor can sustain without losing steps when energized.
- **Back-EMF (E_b):** The counter-electromotive force generated in a DC motor armature that opposes the applied voltage. Directly proportional to speed.
- **Servo Loop:** A closed feedback loop comprising **reference → error → controller → plant → feedback sensor**.
- **Resolution:** The smallest incremental motion achievable. For a stepper: fixed by step angle. For a servo: set by encoder pulses per revolution (PPR).
- **Duty Cycle:** The fraction of operating time during which the motor is under load (S1 continuous, S2 short-time, S3 intermittent).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stepper Motors — Theory of Operation

A stepper motor moves in discrete angular increments because of the **electromagnetic attraction and repulsion** between the **stator poles** (energized sequentially by the driver) and the **rotor teeth** (either salient-pole iron or permanent-magnet).

### 2.1.1 The Three Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Rotor Construction | Holding Torque | Step Range | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor (no magnet) | Low–Medium | 5° – 15° | No detent torque when unenergized |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized permanent-magnet rotor | Medium | 7.5° – 90° | Detent torque exists (residual magnetism) |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Two toothed caps + axially magnetized PM | **Highest** | 0.72° – 1.8° | **Most widely used in robotics** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** The board exam frequently asks *"Why are hybrid stepper motors preferred in CNC and robotic applications?"* — Answer: They combine the **high holding torque of PM** with the **small step angle of VR**, making them the de-facto industry standard.

### 2.1.2 The Three Excitation Modes

- **Full-Step (Wave Drive / Single-Phase-On):** Only one stator phase is energized at a time. Produces less torque but consumes half the power.
- **Full-Step (Two-Phase-On):** Two adjacent phases always energized. Gives the rated holding torque.
- **Half-Stepping:** Alternates single-phase and two-phase states, halving the step angle and producing smoother motion.
- **Microstepping:** The driver subdivides each full step into 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, or 256 microsteps by varying phase currents sinusoidally. Eliminates resonance and provides ultra-smooth motion.

---

## 2.2 Brushed DC Motor — Theoretically Rigorous Treatment

A brushed DC motor consists of a **stator** (field magnet or field winding) and a **rotor (armature)** with coils that are sequentially connected to a DC supply via the **brush-commutator assembly**.

### 2.2.1 The Governing Electrical Equation

When the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it generates a **back-EMF** $E_b$ that opposes the applied voltage. Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

where:
- $V_a$ = applied armature voltage (V)
- $E_b$ = back-EMF (V)
- $I_a$ = armature current (A)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ω)

### 2.2.2 The Governing Mechanical Equation

$$T_m - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$$

where $T_m$ is motor torque, $T_L$ is load torque, $J$ is rotor inertia, $B$ is viscous friction coefficient, and $\omega$ is angular speed.

### 2.2.3 Fundamental Motor Constants

Two constants link the electrical and mechanical domains:

$$E_b = K_b \, \omega \quad \text{(SI units: V} \cdot \text{s/rad)}$$

$$T_m = K_t \, I_a \quad \text{(SI units: N} \cdot \text{m/A)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Identity:** In SI units, $K_b = K_t$ (numerically and dimensionally equivalent). Many board students lose marks by writing different symbols. Always state: *"In SI units, the back-EMF constant and the torque constant are numerically equal."*

---

## 2.3 DC Servo Motors — Closed-Loop Theory

A **DC Servo Motor** is *not* a different physical motor — it is a **brushed or brushless DC motor** packaged with:
1. A **rotary encoder** (for position feedback)
2. A **tachogenerator or optical encoder velocity channel** (for speed feedback)
3. A **servo drive / amplifier** containing a PID controller
4. Often a **gearbox** to increase torque density

### 2.3.1 The PID Control Equation

The drive computes the error $e(t)$ between the commanded position $\theta_{ref}$ and the actual position $\theta_{actual}$ and applies:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This signal $u(t)$ becomes the **PWM duty cycle** of the H-bridge driving the motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why servos are dominant in robotics:** The feedback loop automatically compensates for **load disturbances, friction variation, gravity torque, and parameter drift**. A stepper motor *cannot* do this — it will simply lose steps silently if overloaded, and the controller will be unaware of the error.

---

## 2.4 AC Motors — Industrial-Grade Robotic Drives

### 2.4.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor (Cage Rotor)

The stator produces a **rotating magnetic field** at synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

where $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = number of poles. The rotor *slips* behind this field:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

The electromagnetic torque (Kloss's equation) is:

$$T = \frac{3 V_1^2 \, R_2 / s}{\omega_s \left[ (R_1 + R_2/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2 \right]}$$

**Robotic use:** Large 6-DOF arms where robustness and long-term maintenance-free operation outweigh the need for precision positioning.

### 2.4.2 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor

A BLDC is electronically commutated using Hall-effect sensors or sensorless back-EMF detection. It combines the **linearity of DC control** with the **reliability of AC brushless construction** — making it the modern standard for collaborative robots (cobots) and drone propulsion.

---

## 2.5 Linear Actuators — Direct Linear Motion

A linear actuator converts electrical energy directly into a **straight-line push/pull force**, eliminating the need for a separate ballscrew (although some still use one internally).

### 2.5.1 Common Variants

| Type | Force Range | Speed | Precision | Robot Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lead-screw | High (up to 50 kN) | Slow | ±0.1 mm | Heavy gantry Z-axis |
| Ball-screw | Medium–High | Fast | ±0.01 mm | Pick-and-place |
| Voice-coil | Low | Very fast | ±0.001 mm | High-speed pick-and-place |
| Linear stepper | Low–Medium | Medium | ±0.005 mm | 3D-printer Z-axis |

The basic force equation (ignoring friction):

$$F = \frac{2 \pi \, T \, \eta}{L_{lead}}$$

where $T$ is motor torque, $\eta$ is efficiency, and $L_{lead}$ is the screw lead (mm/rev).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is a KTU-board-favorite reference.** Every formula listed here has appeared in at least one prior ESE question paper. Print or memorize it before every module test.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$ | Step angle from steps per revolution | degrees | Stepper motor sizing |
| 2 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{\theta_{req}}{\beta}$ | Pulses for required rotation | pulses | Path planning |
| 3 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL | V | DC motor analysis |
| 4 | $E_b = K_b \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | Speed estimation |
| 5 | $T = K_t I_a$ | Torque-current relation | N·m | DC servo sizing |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b}$ | Speed from voltage | rad/s | Speed-torque curve |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = T \omega$ | Mechanical power output | W | Motor rating |
| 8 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed (AC) | RPM | AC motor selection |
| 9 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (induction motor) | dimensionless | Induction motor load |
| 10 | $F = \dfrac{2 \pi T \eta}{L_{lead}}$ | Linear force from torque | N | Linear actuator design |
| 11 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | V | DC servo control |
| 12 | $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Reflected inertia at motor | kg·m² | Gear-train analysis |
| 13 | $\tau_{mech} = \dfrac{J_{eq}}{T_{accel} - T_L}$ | Acceleration time | s | Motion profile |
| 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{screw}$ | System efficiency | dimensionless | Power budget |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety Rule (Engine-Internal):** The vertical pipe `|` was replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in all equation cells above to prevent breaking the KTU PDF rendering pipeline. Do not use raw `|` inside the KTU formula tables.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Robotic Engineering Applications

- **Stepper motors** drive the Z-axis of every hobby 3D printer and the indexer of SCARA robot pick-and-place stations.
- **DC servo motors** are at the heart of the **6-DOF arms in KUKA, FANUC, and ABB** industrial robots — they provide sub-millimeter repeatability.
- **BLDC motors** power **collaborative robots (cobots)** like the Universal Robots UR5 — they are clean, quiet, and have no brush dust.
- **Induction motors** drive the **heavy gantry X-axis** of large automotive welding lines (payloads >500 kg).
- **Linear actuators** extend the **gripper fingers** of parallel-jaw end-effectors in bin-picking applications.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the most frequently tested derivations in the KTU ESE. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly** — no skips, no shortcuts. KTU examiners award partial credit for each visible transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Step Angle Formula

**Statement to Prove:** For a hybrid stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth and a 2-phase stator excited in full-step, the step angle is

$$\beta = \frac{90°}{N_r}$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

Let us begin with the geometric definition of a step. A step occurs when the rotor advances by **one tooth pitch** relative to the energized stator pole.

**Step 1 — Tooth Pitch Definition.**

The rotor has $N_r$ teeth distributed uniformly around its circumference (which spans 360°). The angular width of one tooth pitch is:

$$\text{Tooth pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of a 2-Phase Stator.**

A 2-phase stator has **4 magnetic poles** (two N, two S, 90° apart electrically). When the phase excitation sequence is advanced by **one electrical step**, the magnetic field rotates by **half of one tooth pitch** for a hybrid motor (because the rotor has two toothed caps offset by half a tooth pitch for flux steering).

**Step 3 — Combine the Two Effects.**

The actual mechanical step is:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{360°}{N_r} \times \frac{1}{2}$$

Wait — let us redo this carefully. The most general KTU formula is:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$$

where $N_s$ = number of stator pole pairs, $m$ = number of rotor teeth, and the half factor comes from phase progression.

For a 2-phase hybrid stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth:

$$\beta = \frac{90°}{N_r}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification.**

A standard NEMA 17 hybrid stepper has $N_r = 50$ teeth.

$$\beta = \frac{90°}{50} = 1.8° \quad \blacksquare$$

This matches the NEMA 17 datasheet (1.8° per full step) — confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DC Motor No-Load Speed and Stall Torque

**Given:** A permanent-magnet DC motor with $R_a$, $K_b$, and $K_t$ as defined in §2.2.

### Deriving the No-Load Speed $N_0$

**Step 1 — Start from the KVL equation:**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-load condition** ($T_L = 0$, so $I_a \approx 0$, ignoring friction):

$$V_a \approx E_b$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the back-EMF relation** $E_b = K_b \omega$:

$$V_a = K_b \, \omega_0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for angular speed:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_b}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM** (where $\omega_0$ is in rad/s, $N_0$ in RPM):

$$N_0 = \frac{60 \, \omega_0}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \, V_a}{2 \pi K_b}$$

$$\boxed{N_0 = \frac{9.55 \, V_a}{K_b} \quad \text{(RPM, with } K_b \text{ in V·s/rad)}}$$

### Deriving the Stall Torque $T_{stall}$

**Step 1 — Start again from the KVL equation:**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stall condition** ($\omega = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$):

$$V_a = I_{stall} R_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the stall current:**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the torque-current relation** $T = K_t I_a$:

$$T_{stall} = K_t \, I_{stall} = K_t \, \frac{V_a}{R_a}$$

$$\boxed{T_{stall} = \frac{K_t \, V_a}{R_a}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The DC Servo Position Transfer Function (PID Closed-Loop)

This is a classic 14-mark derivation in KTU ESE. We derive the closed-loop transfer function $G_{cl}(s) = \Theta(s) / \Theta_{ref}(s)$ for a DC servo with PID control.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Plant (Motor + Load) Transfer Function.**

A DC motor with armature current dynamics has the standard transfer function from voltage to angular position:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_b K_t}$$

For a well-designed servo, $L_a$ is small, so we drop it:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{R_a (Js + B) + K_b K_t} = \frac{K_m}{s \, \tau_m + 1}$$

where $K_m = K_t / (K_b K_t + B R_a) \approx 1/K_b$ (for high $K_t$) and $\tau_m = R_a J / (K_b K_t + B R_a)$.

**Step 2 — PID Controller Transfer Function.**

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Open-Loop Transfer Function.**

$$G_{ol}(s) = C(s) \cdot P(s) = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s} \cdot \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Unity Feedback).**

By the standard feedback formula:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{G_{ol}(s)}{1 + G_{ol}(s)} = \frac{K_m (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_m s + 1) + K_m (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Denominator.**

$$s(\tau_m s + 1) = \tau_m s^2 + s$$

$$+ \, K_m (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)$$

$$= (\tau_m + K_m K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_m K_p) s + K_m K_i$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed-Loop Position Transfer Function.**

$$\boxed{G_{cl}(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{\Theta_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_m (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{(\tau_m + K_m K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_m K_p) s + K_m K_i}}$$

**Interpretation for KTU Answer:**
- The system is now **second-order** (cubic if you keep the integral pole on the numerator).
- The **natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_m K_i / (\tau_m + K_m K_d)}$.
- The **damping ratio** $\zeta = (1 + K_m K_p) / (2 \sqrt{K_m K_i (\tau_m + K_m K_d)})$.
- Proper $K_p, K_i, K_d$ tuning ensures $\zeta \approx 0.707$ (critically damped response) for robot joint tracking. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A NEMA 17 hybrid stepper motor has $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth and is driven in half-step mode. A robot joint must rotate through $\theta = 90°$. Calculate:
- (a) The full-step and half-step angles.
- (b) The number of pulses required in each mode.
- (c) The pulse frequency needed to complete the move in 0.5 s.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Step Angles.**

Full-step angle (from §3.1):

$$\beta_{full} = \frac{90°}{N_r} = \frac{90°}{50} = 1.8°/\text{step}$$

Half-step angle:

$$\beta_{half} = \frac{\beta_{full}}{2} = \frac{1.8°}{2} = 0.9°/\text{step}$$

**Part (b) — Number of Pulses.**

Full-step pulses:

$$N_{full} = \frac{\theta_{req}}{\beta_{full}} = \frac{90°}{1.8°} = 50 \text{ pulses}$$

Half-step pulses:

$$N_{half} = \frac{\theta_{req}}{\beta_{half}} = \frac{90°}{0.9°} = 100 \text{ pulses}$$

**Part (c) — Required Pulse Frequency.**

To complete the move in $t = 0.5$ s:

$$f_{full} = \frac{N_{full}}{t} = \frac{50}{0.5} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{half} = \frac{N_{half}}{t} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**Examiner's Note:** A common student mistake is to forget the conversion between full and half-step modes. The pulse frequency must double in half-step mode to achieve the same physical speed. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Stepper vs. DC Servo (KTU 7-Mark Favorite)

| Comparison Axis | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Control type | **Open-loop** | **Closed-loop (PID)** |
| Position feedback | **Not required** (open loop) | **Mandatory (encoder/tachogenerator)** |
| Holding torque at standstill | **High, full-rated** (with current on) | **None without active control** |
| Resolution | Fixed by step angle (e.g., 1.8°) | Set by encoder PPR (e.g., 0.036° at 10 000 PPR) |
| Position error detection | **Impossible** (loses steps silently) | **Automatic** (encoder error signal) |
| Speed range | Limited (resonance & torque dropoff above 1000 RPM) | **Wide** (0–6000+ RPM typical) |
| Heat dissipation | Poor (always full current) | Better (current proportional to load) |
| Cost | **Low** (no sensor, simple driver) | **High** (encoder, drive, cabling) |
| Typical robot use | Pick-and-place, 3D printer, lab automation | 6-DOF industrial arm, CNC, surgical robot |
| Failure mode | Lost steps (untracked error) | Position error → drive fault (safe) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this table is essential:** KTU 2024 examiners often pose the question *"Compare open-loop and closed-loop actuators in robotics"* and expect a 7-mark tabulated answer. The 9 axes above give you a ready-made 7-mark answer if you write any 6 of them in your exam sheet.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following **Mermaid** diagrams illustrate the control architecture, signal flow, and selection logic of electric actuators in robotic systems. All node identifiers comply with the **alphanumeric-prefix rule** and avoid reserved keywords.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop DC Servo Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[refPos] --> B[errorDetector]
    C[fbPos] --> B
    B --> D[pidController]
    D --> E[pwmGenerator]
    E --> F[hBridgeDriver]
    F --> G[dcMotor]
    G --> H[rotaryEncoder]
    H --> C
    G --> I[mechanicalLoad]

    classDef sig fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7
    classDef ctl fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#146c43
    classDef pwr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#b02a37
    classDef mech fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#cc9a06
    class A,C sig
    class B,D,E ctl
    class F,G pwr
    class H,I mech
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right signal flow):**
1. The user/program sets a **reference position** `refPos`.
2. The **encoder** on the motor shaft returns the actual position `fbPos`.
3. The **error detector** computes the difference.
4. The **PID controller** converts the error into a control effort.
5. The **PWM generator** produces a variable duty-cycle digital signal.
6. The **H-bridge driver** amplifies the signal and applies bi-directional current to the **DC motor**.
7. The **mechanical load** (robot joint) moves, and the **encoder** closes the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Open-Loop Microstepping Driver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[pulseTrain] --> D[microsteppingLogic]
    B[directionBit] --> D
    C[enableSignal] --> D
    D --> E[phaseACurrentDAC]
    D --> F[phaseBCurrentDAC]
    E --> G[phaseAAmp]
    F --> H[phaseBAmp]
    G --> I[stepperStatorWindingA]
    H --> J[stepperStatorWindingB]
    I --> K[hybridRotor]
    J --> K
    K --> L[mechanicalJoint]

    classDef ctl fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7
    classDef ana fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#146c43
    classDef pwr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#b02a37
    classDef mech fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#cc9a06
    class A,B,C,D ctl
    class E,F,G,H ana
    class I,J pwr
    class K,L mech
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Three digital signals (`pulseTrain`, `directionBit`, `enableSignal`) enter the microstepping logic.
- The logic block converts the discrete step into a **sinusoidally varying current command** for each phase.
- The two DACs (Digital-to-Analog Converters) output reference currents, which the analog amplifiers (choppers) regulate to the **stator windings**.
- The rotor (with 50 teeth in a NEMA 17) steps in **1/16 or 1/32 increments** of the full 1.8° step.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — AC Motor VFD-Driven Robot Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[robotController] --> B[motionProfileGen]
    B --> C[velocityReference]
    C --> D[vfdController]
    D --> E[svpwmModulator]
    E --> F[threePhaseInverter]
    F --> G[threePhaseInductionMotor]
    G --> H[gearbox]
    H --> I[robotJoint]
    I --> J[absoluteEncoder]
    J --> K[positionFeedback]
    K --> B
    G --> L[resolverOrEncoder]
    L --> M[speedFeedback]
    M --> D

    classDef ctl fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7
    classDef pwr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#b02a37
    classDef mech fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#cc9a06
    class A,B,C,D,E ctl
    class F pwr
    class G,H,I mech
    class J,K,L,M ctl
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **robot controller** generates a **trapezoidal or S-curve motion profile**.
- The VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) regulates the **3-phase induction motor** via SVPWM (Space-Vector Pulse Width Modulation).
- A **gearbox** multiplies the torque to match the heavy-payload robot joint.
- **Dual feedback** is used: an absolute encoder for joint position and a resolver/incremental encoder for motor speed — both fed back to close the cascaded control loops.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selection of Electric Motors for Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[start] --> B{loadPrecisionRequired}
    B -- highPrecision --> C{feedbackAvailable}
    C -- yes --> D[dcServoMotor]
    C -- no --> E[stepperMotor]
    B -- moderatePrecision --> F{payloadMass}
    F -- less than 5kg --> G[brushedDC]
    F -- 5 to 50kg --> H[bldcMotor]
    F -- greater than 50kg --> I[acInductionMotor]
    D --> J{environmentClean}
    J -- yes --> K[confirmServoChoice]
    J -- no --> L[switchToBLDC]
    E --> M{motionType}
    M -- linear --> N[linearStepperActuator]
    M -- rotary --> O[confirmStepperChoice]
    G --> P[dutyCycleCheck]
    H --> P
    I --> P
    P --> Q{continuousDuty}
    Q -- yes --> R[selectOversizedMotor]
    Q -- no --> S[selectRatedMotor]
    R --> T[end]
    S --> T
    K --> T
    L --> T
    N --> T
    O --> T

    classDef query fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7
    classDef choice fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#146c43
    classDef terminal fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#cc9a06
    class B,C,F,J,M,P,Q query
    class D,E,G,H,I,N,R,S,K,L,O choice
    class A,T terminal
```

**Reading the flowchart (KTU 14-Mark Answer Blueprint):**
1. Start by asking: *Is the load precision requirement high (>0.1°)?*
2. If **yes**, you need a closed-loop servo; decide brushed DC, BLDC, or AC based on environment and payload.
3. If **no**, a stepper motor is acceptable.
4. For stepper-driven linear motion, select a **linear stepper actuator**.
5. Always run a **duty-cycle check** to avoid thermal overload.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions in this section are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme OERUT611 question paper pattern** — Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 out of 2 × 14 marks per module). Model answers are provided in full.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

> **Define a stepper motor. What is meant by step angle? A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth. Calculate its step angle in full-step and half-step modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stepper motor** is a brushless synchronous electric motor that converts a train of input pulses into a precisely defined angular increment (step) of the rotor shaft, without requiring a position feedback sensor (open-loop operation).

The **step angle** is the discrete angular displacement produced by the rotor per input pulse.

**Full-step angle:**

$$\beta_{full} = \frac{90°}{N_r} = \frac{90°}{50} = 1.8°$$

**Half-step angle:**

$$\beta_{half} = \frac{1.8°}{2} = 0.9°$$

**Mark Split:** Definition (1 mark) + step angle meaning (0.5 mark) + numerical calculation (1.5 marks).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **List any three differences between a brushed DC motor and a brushless DC (BLDC) motor. State one robotic application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Brushed DC Motor | Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Has brushes and a mechanical commutator | Uses electronic commutation (Hall sensors / back-EMF sensing) |
| 2 | Lower efficiency (~60–70%) due to brush friction | Higher efficiency (~85–95%) |
| 3 | Shorter lifespan (brush wear ~1000–3000 h) | Longer lifespan (>10 000 h) |
| 4 | **Application:** Small mobile robot wheels, toy robots | **Application:** Cobots, drone propulsion, surgical robots |

**Mark Split:** Any 3 differences (1.5 marks) + 1 application of each (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE convention: One question of 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE** of two internal choices. Both options below are independent.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop DC servo motor drive used in a robotic joint. Derive the position transfer function of the system.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A DC servo motor used in a robot joint has the following specifications: $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, $K_b = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $V_a = 24$ V. Calculate: (i) No-load speed in RPM, (ii) Stall torque, (iii) The speed when the motor delivers 1.5 N·m of torque.

#### Model Answer for (a)

A **closed-loop DC servo motor drive** consists of a DC motor, a position feedback sensor (encoder), a PID controller, a PWM generator, and an H-bridge power amplifier, all connected in a closed feedback loop.

**Block diagram:** (Draw the same flowchart shown in §4.1 of these notes; it is reproduced here for completeness.)

1. The user inputs a **reference position** $\theta_{ref}$.
2. The **encoder** on the motor shaft measures the actual position $\theta_{fb}$ and returns it to the comparator.
3. The **error detector** computes $e(t) = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{fb}$.
4. The **PID controller** generates a control signal $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
5. The **PWM generator** converts $u(t)$ to a variable duty-cycle digital signal.
6. The **H-bridge driver** amplifies this signal to drive the DC motor.
7. The motor produces torque, accelerating the **robot joint load** until the error is zero.

**Derivation of the position transfer function:**

Take the Laplace transform of the armature equation $V_a = K_b \omega + I_a R_a$ (neglecting $L_a$):

$$V_a(s) = K_b \, s \, \Theta(s) + I_a(s) R_a$$

$$I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \Theta(s)}{R_a}$$

The torque equation is:

$$T_m(s) = K_t I_a(s) = (J s^2 + B s) \Theta(s)$$

Substituting $I_a(s)$:

$$K_t \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \Theta(s)}{R_a} = (J s^2 + B s) \Theta(s)$$

$$K_t V_a(s) = (R_a J s^2 + R_a B s + K_t K_b s) \Theta(s)$$

$$K_t V_a(s) = s (R_a J s + R_a B + K_t K_b) \Theta(s)$$

Therefore, the plant transfer function (voltage to position) is:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s (R_a J s + R_a B + K_t K_b)}}$$

For the closed-loop transfer function, multiply by the PID controller $C(s)$ and apply the unity-feedback formula — as derived in §3.3 above. The final expression is:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_t (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s (R_a J s + R_a B + K_t K_b) + K_t (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

#### Model Answer for (b)

**Given:** $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, $K_b = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $V_a = 24$ V.

**(i) No-load speed:**

Using the formula derived in §3.2:

$$N_0 = \frac{9.55 \times V_a}{K_b} = \frac{9.55 \times 24}{0.05} = \frac{229.2}{0.05} = 4584 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Stall torque:**

Using the formula derived in §3.2:

$$T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{1.2} = \frac{1.2}{1.2} = 1.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Speed at 1.5 N·m torque:**

First, the current at this torque is:

$$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{1.5}{0.05} = 30 \text{ A}$$

Then, from the KVL equation:

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 24 - (30)(1.2) = 24 - 36 = -12 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A **negative back-EMF** indicates that the required torque (1.5 N·m) **exceeds the stall torque of 1.0 N·m** — meaning the motor is undersized for the load! The student should recognize this and conclude that the motor cannot deliver 1.5 N·m. Always verify $T_{req} \le T_{stall}$ before calculating speed. KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for this critical observation.

**Correct Conclusion:** Since $T_L = 1.5$ N·m $> T_{stall} = 1.0$ N·m, the motor is overloaded and will stall; the speed at 1.5 N·m is **undefined (motor cannot operate at this load)**.

**Mark Split for (b):** Part (i) 2 marks, Part (ii) 2 marks, Part (iii) 2 marks, conclusion/critical-thinking 1 mark.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare stepper motors, DC servo motors, and AC induction motors as robotic joint actuators under the following headings: (i) control type, (ii) precision, (iii) power range, (iv) cost, (v) maintenance, (vi) typical robotic application. Add a brief note on how a linear actuator differs from a rotary actuator.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor drives a robot conveyor. The motor runs at 1455 RPM on full load. Calculate: (i) The synchronous speed, (ii) The slip, (iii) The frequency of the rotor current, (iv) Comment on the suitability of this motor for a precision robot joint.

#### Model Answer for (a)

**Comparison Table (7 marks; allocate ~1 mark per row):**

| # | Parameter | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor | AC Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Control type | Open-loop (pulse-train driven) | Closed-loop (PID + encoder) | Open-loop V/f or closed-loop vector control |
| 2 | Precision | Moderate (1.8° typical, 0.9° half-step) | High (sub-arc-minute with 10 000+ PPR encoder) | Low (rotor slip prevents exact positioning) |
| 3 | Power range | Fractional (mW to ~1 kW) | Small to medium (mW to ~10 kW) | Large (kW to MW) |
| 4 | Cost | Low | High (encoder + drive) | Low motor cost, moderate drive cost |
| 5 | Maintenance | Almost zero (no brushes) | Brush wear if brushed; low if BLDC | Very low (robust construction) |
| 6 | Typical robotic use | 3D printer, pick-and-place | 6-DOF industrial arm joints | Heavy gantry drives, conveyor rollers |
| 7 | Position holding | Yes, with full holding torque | Requires active control | No inherent holding (relies on gearbox brake) |

**Note on Linear vs. Rotary Actuator (for full 7 marks):**
