> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Students must be able to **distinguish between SCARA and PUMA configurations** based on joint types, degrees of freedom, workspace geometry, and application domains. This is a recurring 7-to-14 mark question in End Semester Examinations.

---

## 1.2 SCARA Robot — The High-Speed Assembly Specialist

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
**SCARA** stands for **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** (sometimes called **Selective Compliance Articulated Robot Arm**). It was developed in **1978** at the **Yamanashi University, Japan**, by Professor Hiroshi Makino. The defining kinematic feature is its **selective compliance**: the arm is **compliant (flexible) in the horizontal X–Y plane** but **rigid along the vertical Z-axis**.

The standard SCARA configuration is a **four-axis (4-DOF)** manipulator with the joint sequence **R₁ – R₂ – P₃ – R₄** (two parallel revolute joints for the arm, one prismatic joint for vertical lift, and one revolute joint for tool rotation about the vertical axis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine a **gantry crane in a factory** that can swing left–right and forward–backward (that's the compliant horizontal motion), but its hook can only move straight up and straight down with no wobble (that's the rigid vertical motion), and the hook itself can spin to tighten a screw. That is exactly how a SCARA behaves. It is the industrial equivalent of a highly disciplined "pick-and-place" worker who is excellent at a single task on a flat tabletop.

### Key Features of SCARA (in 6 points)
1. **Selective Compliance:** Compliant in horizontal plane (X–Y), rigid vertically (Z).
2. **High Speed & High Repeatability:** Typically achieves cycle times under 1 second; repeatability of **±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm**.
3. **High Payload-to-Weight Ratio:** Compact and stiff vertical column supports moderate payloads (1–10 kg).
4. **Cylindrical Workspace:** The reachable volume resembles a short cylinder/disk.
5. **Floor or Ceiling Mounting:** Unique among industrial robots due to its symmetric structure.
6. **Joint Configuration:** All four joint axes are **parallel to the vertical (Z) axis**, which is what causes the selective compliance.

### Primary Applications
- Printed Circuit Board (PCB) assembly
- Pick-and-place operations
- Screwdriving and insertion tasks
- Palletizing small parts
- Adhesive dispensing
- Small parts assembly in electronics

---

## 1.3 PUMA Robot — The Anthropomorphic Workhorse

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
**PUMA** stands for **Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly** (originally an acronym coined by **Unimation Inc., USA, in 1978**, with development influenced by General Motors). It is a **six-axis (6-DOF)**, all-**revolute (R)** manipulator designed to mimic the **kinematic structure of the human arm**: shoulder, upper arm, elbow, forearm, wrist (with three rotational joints), and end-effector.

The standard PUMA joint sequence is **R₁ (waist/waist rotation) – R₂ (shoulder) – R₃ (elbow) – R₄ (wrist roll) – R₅ (wrist pitch) – R₆ (wrist yaw)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Think of the PUMA as a **mechanical version of a person's arm and shoulder**. Your shoulder can rotate left-right (waist), tilt up-down (shoulder), bend at the elbow (elbow joint), and your wrist can roll, pitch, and yaw in three independent directions before your fingers (the end-effector) grasp something. PUMA replicates all of this. It is the "Swiss Army knife" of industrial robots — slower than SCARA at simple tasks but vastly more dexterous in 3D space.

### Key Features of PUMA (in 6 points)
1. **Anthropomorphic (Jointed-Arm) Geometry:** Mimics the human arm.
2. **6 Degrees of Freedom (6R):** Provides full spatial dexterity, can reach **any pose (position + orientation) within its workspace**.
3. **All-Revolute Joints:** High mechanical simplicity, no linear actuators.
4. **Spherical Workspace:** The reachable volume is a portion of a sphere (offset spherical shell).
5. **Electric Servo Drive:** All joints are driven by DC servo motors with encoders — clean, quiet, precise.
6. **Moderate Reach & Payload:** Typically 0.5–2 kg payload, 0.6–1.0 m reach.

### Primary Applications
- Arc welding and spot welding
- Spray painting
- Material handling and machine tending
- Complex 3D assembly
- Deburring and polishing
- Research and educational platforms (classic PUMA 560 is widely studied)

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparative Snapshot (Definition-Level)

| Parameter | SCARA | PUMA |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm | Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly |
| Origin (Year) | Yamanashi University, Japan (1978) | Unimation Inc., USA (1978) |
| DOF | 4 (typically) | 6 |
| Joint Sequence | R – R – P – R | R – R – R – R – R – R |
| Workspace Shape | Cylindrical (flat disk-like) | Spherical (partial sphere) |
| Compliance | Compliant in X–Y, Rigid in Z | Compliant in all directions |
| Mounting | Floor or Ceiling | Floor only |
| Typical Payload | 1 – 10 kg | 0.5 – 2 kg |
| Repeatability | ±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm | ±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm |
| Best Suited For | High-speed planar assembly | Complex 3D dexterous tasks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The KTU Backbone Concept

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is defined as the **number of independent parameters (joint variables) that uniquely specify the configuration of the manipulator in space**. For a serial-chain manipulator operating in 3D space, the maximum useful DOF is **6** (3 for position + 3 for orientation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A robot needs **at least 6 DOF to reach any arbitrary position AND orientation in 3D space**. A robot with fewer than 6 DOF is called a **deficient-DOF robot** and is restricted to specific tasks. SCARA is exactly such a deficient-DOF (4-DOF) robot.

### Grübler's Formula (KTU Mandatory Formula)
For a planar mechanism:
$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

For a spatial mechanism:
$$F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where:
- **F** = Degrees of Freedom
- **n** = Total number of links (including ground)
- **j₁** = Number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints: revolute R, prismatic P)
- **j₂** = Number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam, gear)
- **j₃, j₄, j₅** = 3-, 4-, 5-DOF pairs respectively

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** The KTU examiner often asks students to **derive the DOF of SCARA or PUMA using Grübler's formula**. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in any ESE.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of SCARA (RRPR)

The SCARA has **4 moving links** plus the ground. Joints are: R, R, P, R. All four joint axes are **vertical (parallel to Z)**.

Applying the spatial Grübler formula:
- n = 5 (4 moving links + ground)
- j₁ = 4 (all four joints are 1-DOF)
- j₂ = 0
- (Each link and ground also each have 3-DOF in space, but with the standard simplification for a serial chain)

For a **simple open-chain serial manipulator**, the standard formula simplifies to:
$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$
where f_i is the DOF of each joint.

So for SCARA:
$$F_{SCARA} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ DOF}$$

### Workspace Geometry of SCARA
Because the two arm revolute joints rotate about vertical axes, the end-effector position (x, y) in the horizontal plane is given by the standard 2R planar arm equations (with link lengths L₁, L₂):
$$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$z = d_3 \quad (\text{controlled by prismatic joint})$$

The reachable workspace is a **portion of a thick cylindrical disk** of radius (L₁ + L₂) and height equal to the prismatic joint's stroke.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Analysis of PUMA (RRRRRR — 6R)

The PUMA has **6 moving links** plus the ground. All 6 joints are revolute.

For an open-chain 6R:
$$F_{PUMA} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6 \text{ DOF}$$

### Workspace Geometry of PUMA
The PUMA's first three joints (waist, shoulder, elbow) determine the **position** of the wrist center. These three joints give the manipulator a **spherical-coordinate-like position capability**:
- θ₁: rotation about the base (azimuth)
- θ₂: shoulder pitch (elevation from horizontal)
- θ₃: elbow pitch (changes distance from shoulder to wrist)

The last three joints (R₄, R₅, R₆) form a **spherical wrist**, providing arbitrary **orientation** of the end-effector. This **kinematic decoupling** (position by first 3 joints, orientation by last 3 joints) is the classic **Pieper's criterion** for analytic inverse kinematics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** PUMA satisfies **Pieper's criterion** because the last three revolute joint axes intersect at a single common point (the **wrist center**). This is why PUMA's inverse kinematics has a **closed-form analytic solution**, which is the gold standard in KTU numericals.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grübler (Spatial) | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | General DOF of spatial mechanisms |
| 2 | Grübler (Planar) | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | DOF of planar linkages |
| 3 | Open-chain DOF | $F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Serial manipulators like SCARA, PUMA |
| 4 | SCARA 2R forward position | $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | Planar end-effector position |
| 5 | SCARA 2R forward position | $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | Planar end-effector position |
| 6 | SCARA vertical position | $z = d_3$ | Prismatic joint stroke |
| 7 | 2R reach condition | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | Used to find θ₂ via law of cosines |
| 8 | Law of cosines (θ₂) | $\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1 L_2}$ | SCARA elbow joint |
| 9 | Spherical wrist condition | $d_4 = d_5 = d_6 = 0$ (DH convention) | Pieper's criterion satisfied by PUMA |
| 10 | Workspace radius (SCARA) | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ | Maximum horizontal reach |
| 11 | Workspace radius (SCARA) | $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | Minimum (inner dead-zone) |
| 12 | Pose count requirement | Min DOF for full pose = 6 | Explains why SCARA cannot do arbitrary 3D assembly |

> [!TIP]
> The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **absolute value**. In KTU answer scripts, write it explicitly as "mod" or "absolute value of" to avoid notation confusion.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SCARA vs PUMA choice in industry is a classic **trade-off study**:
- **Choose SCARA** when the task is **planar, repetitive, and high-speed** (e.g., inserting 1000 chips per hour on a PCB).
- **Choose PUMA** when the task requires **arbitrary 3D orientation** (e.g., arc-welding a car chassis from multiple angles).

In modern automotive assembly lines, you will often find a **hybrid cell**: SCARAs doing PCB assembly on one side, PUMAs doing body welding on the other. Understanding both architectures is therefore not just academic — it directly informs **cell layout design, cycle-time estimation, and ROI calculations** in real factory automation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive DOF Derivation of SCARA Using Grübler's Formula

### Setup
Consider a 4-DOF SCARA with the following links:
- Link 0 = Ground (fixed base)
- Link 1 = First arm (rotates about vertical axis z₀)
- Link 2 = Second arm (rotates about vertical axis z₁)
- Link 3 = Vertical slider (translates along z₂)
- Link 4 = Tool flange (rotates about vertical axis z₃)

Joint types in order: R – R – P – R

### Step 1: Count the total number of links
The number of links **n** includes the ground link. So:
$$n = 4 \text{ (moving links)} + 1 \text{ (ground)} = 5$$

### Step 2: Count the number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints)
Each revolute (R) joint contributes 1 DOF, and each prismatic (P) joint contributes 1 DOF. Total joints in SCARA = 4.
$$j_1 = 4, \quad j_2 = 0, \quad j_3 = 0, \quad j_4 = 0, \quad j_5 = 0$$

### Step 3: Apply the spatial Grübler formula
$$F = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Substitute the values:
$$F = 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0$$

Compute step by step:
$$F = 6(4) - 20$$
$$F = 24 - 20$$
$$F = 4$$

### Step 4: Conclusion
$$\boxed{F_{SCARA} = 4 \text{ DOF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** Since all four joints of SCARA have axes **parallel to the vertical Z-axis**, the end-effector cannot rotate about the horizontal X or Y axes. It can therefore **change position in X, Y, Z and rotation about Z (4 independent motions)** — matching our 4 DOF result. This is a self-consistency check students should always perform.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive DOF Derivation of PUMA Using Grübler's Formula

### Setup
The PUMA 560 has 6 revolute joints, with links numbered 0 to 6 (Link 0 = ground). Joint sequence: R₁ – R₂ – R₃ – R₄ – R₅ – R₆.

### Step 1: Count links
$$n = 6 \text{ (moving links)} + 1 \text{ (ground)} = 7$$

### Step 2: Count joints
All six joints are revolute (1-DOF each).
$$j_1 = 6, \quad j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$$

### Step 3: Apply Grübler
$$F = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1$$
$$F = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6)$$
$$F = 6(6) - 30$$
$$F = 36 - 30$$
$$F = 6$$

### Step 4: Conclusion
$$\boxed{F_{PUMA} = 6 \text{ DOF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic Check (Why 6 is the maximum useful DOF):** A rigid body in 3D space has exactly **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A 6-DOF manipulator can therefore place its end-effector at **any arbitrary position and orientation** within its workspace — this is called **dexterous workspace** and is the design goal of the PUMA.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SCARA vs PUMA Side-by-Side Analysis

This matrix is the **definitive KTU board-exam summary table**. Memorize it.

| Analytical Criterion | SCARA | PUMA |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm | Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly |
| **Year & Origin** | 1978, Yamanashi Univ., Japan | 1978, Unimation Inc., USA |
| **DOF** | 4 | 6 |
| **Joint Sequence (DH)** | R – R – P – R | R – R – R – R – R – R |
| **Grübler DOF (F)** | $F = 6(4) - 5(4) = 4$ | $F = 6(6) - 5(6) = 6$ |
| **Total Links (n)** | 5 (incl. ground) | 7 (incl. ground) |
| **Lower Pairs (j₁)** | 4 | 6 |
| **Workspace Shape** | Cylindrical (thick disk) | Spherical (portion of sphere) |
| **Workspace Volume (typical)** | $\pi (L_1+L_2)^2 \cdot d_3$ | Complex spherical shell region |
| **Max Reach (Position)** | $L_1 + L_2$ (horizontal) | $\sqrt{d_2^2 + (d_4+a_4)^2 + \dots}$ (function of DH params) |
| **Vertical Motion** | Prismatic joint (rigid) | Revolute joints (compliant) |
| **End-Effector Orientation** | Only rotation about Z | Full 3D orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) |
| **Pieper's Criterion** | Not applicable (only 4 DOF) | Satisfied (spherical wrist) |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | Closed-form, simple | Closed-form, moderate complexity |
| **Singularities** | At $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$ (arm fully extended/folded) | At multiple configurations (elbow, wrist, shoulder) |
| **Drive Type** | Electric servo (often with pneumatic vertical lift) | DC servo motors at all joints |
| **Mounting** | Floor **OR** ceiling | Floor only |
| **Cycle Time (typical)** | 0.3 – 1.0 s | 1.0 – 3.0 s |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| **Payload (typical)** | 1 – 10 kg | 0.5 – 2 kg |
| **Best Application** | PCB assembly, pick-and-place | Arc welding, painting, complex assembly |
| **Cost (relative)** | Lower | Higher |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of SCARA Elbow Angle (θ₂) — The Most Common Numerical

A typical KTU numerical problem provides the target planar position (x, y) and asks for joint angles. The derivation uses the **Law of Cosines** on the 2R planar arm.

### Given
- Link lengths L₁ and L₂
- Target position (x, y)

### Step 1: Compute radial distance
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

### Step 2: Apply Law of Cosines to triangle formed by L₁, L₂, r
The angle θ₂ (elbow joint, measured from the L₁ extension) satisfies:
$$r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2)$$

Note: This convention uses interior triangle angle. The standard SCARA convention gives:
$$r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$$

### Step 3: Solve for cos θ₂
$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$$

### Step 4: Compute θ₂
$$\theta_2 = \arccos\left(\frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Compute θ₁ (shoulder joint)
Using atan2 for quadrant safety:
$$\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$$

### Step 6: z-coordinate from prismatic joint
$$d_3 = z$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This exact 3-step procedure is encoded in the **inverse kinematics solver** of every commercial SCARA controller. It runs at **1 kHz** in real-time to track moving conveyor belts in factory assembly lines.

---

## 3.5 Sanity Check — Why SCARA is "Selectively Compliant"

A **compliant mechanism** is one that deforms elastically under load. SCARA's arm links are long and slender in the horizontal plane, allowing them to flex slightly in X and Y when a part is force-fitted. However, the **vertical column is short and stout** and supported by the prismatic joint's linear bearing — it cannot bend vertically.

| Direction | Compliant? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| X (horizontal) | **Yes** | Long slender links flex |
| Y (horizontal) | **Yes** | Long slender links flex |
| Z (vertical) | **No** | Short rigid column, prismatic guide |

This **anisotropic stiffness** is precisely what makes SCARA ideal for **peg-in-hole insertion**: the arm "gives" laterally to absorb misalignment, but pushes straight down without buckling.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define SCARA. List any four of its key features.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
SCARA stands for **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm**. It is a 4-DOF manipulator with joint sequence R–R–P–R developed at Yamanashi University, Japan in 1978.

**Four Key Features:**
1. **Selective compliance** — compliant in the horizontal X–Y plane, rigid along the vertical Z-axis.
2. **High speed and high repeatability** (typically ±0.02 to ±0.05 mm).
3. **Cylindrical workspace** shaped like a thick disk, ideal for planar assembly.
4. **Floor or ceiling mounting** flexibility due to its symmetric vertical structure.
5. (Bonus) High payload-to-weight ratio with compact footprint.

---

### Q2. State the full form of PUMA and mention two of its applications.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
PUMA stands for **Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly**. It is a 6-DOF, all-revolute anthropomorphic manipulator developed by Unimation Inc., USA in 1978.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Arc welding** in automotive body assembly (multi-orientation path required).
2. **Spray painting** of complex curved surfaces (requires arbitrary end-effector orientation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) — DOF Analysis & Kinematics Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice, Set A]**

#### (a) Derive the Degrees of Freedom of a 4-DOF SCARA robot using Grübler's formula. Explain why SCARA is called "selectively compliant." (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify components of SCARA:**
- Number of moving links = 4
- Number of joints = 4 (R, R, P, R)
- Ground link included

**Step 2 — Apply Grübler's spatial formula:**
$$F = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

With n = 5, j₁ = 4, all higher pairs = 0:
$$F = 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) = 24 - 20 = 4$$

$$\boxed{F_{SCARA} = 4 \text{ DOF}}$$

**Step 3 — Explanation of "Selective Compliance":**
- The two arm revolute joints (R₁, R₂) rotate about the **vertical Z-axis** and have **long, slender horizontal links**. Under side loads, these links **flex elastically in the X and Y directions** → this gives **compliance in the horizontal plane**.
- The vertical motion is provided by a **prismatic joint with a rigid linear guide**, supported by a **short, stout column**. This structure **cannot bend vertically** → this gives **rigidity in the Z direction**.
- Hence, the arm is **selectively compliant** (compliant only in chosen directions) — an ideal property for **peg-in-hole assembly** where lateral misalignment must be absorbed but vertical stiffness must be maintained.

---

#### (b) A SCARA robot has two links of length L₁ = 400 mm and L₂ = 300 mm. The end-effector is required to reach a point P(350, 250, 50) mm. Determine the joint angles θ₁, θ₂ and the prismatic displacement d₃. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify z-coordinate:**
The prismatic joint controls vertical motion. Therefore:
$$d_3 = z = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute radial distance in the X–Y plane:**
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{350^2 + 250^2} = \sqrt{122500 + 62500} = \sqrt{185000}$$
$$r \approx 430.12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Law of Cosines to find θ₂:**
$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$$
$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{185000 - 160000 - 90000}{2 \times 400 \times 300} = \frac{-65000}{240000}$$
$$\cos\theta_2 = -0.2708$$
$$\theta_2 = \arccos(-0.2708) \approx 105.72°$$

**Step 4 — Compute shoulder angle θ₁ using atan2:**
$$\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$$

Compute:
- $\operatorname{atan2}(250, 350) = 35.54°$
- $L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 300 \times \sin(105.72°) = 300 \times 0.9627 = 288.81$ mm
- $L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 300 \times (-0.2708) = -81.24$ mm
- $L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 400 - 81.24 = 318.76$ mm
- $\operatorname{atan2}(288.81, 318.76) = 42.18°$

Therefore:
$$\theta_1 = 35.54° - 42.18° = -6.64°$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\theta_1 \approx -6.64°, \quad \theta_2 \approx 105.72°, \quad d_3 = 50 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification of Reach:** $L_1 + L_2 = 700$ mm > 430.12 mm ✓ (Point is within workspace)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — PUMA Architecture & Comparative Analysis Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice, Set B]**

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the architecture of a PUMA robot. Derive its degrees of freedom using Grübler's formula. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture of PUMA 560:**
The PUMA has **6 revolute joints** in the sequence R₁ – R₂ – R₃ – R₄ – R₅ – R₆, organized as:
- **R₁** (Waist): Rotates the entire arm about a vertical axis (azimuth).
- **R₂** (Shoulder): Pitches the upper arm up-down about a horizontal axis.
- **R₃** (Elbow): Bends the forearm relative to the upper arm about a parallel horizontal axis.
- **R₄, R₅, R₆** (Wrist Roll, Wrist Pitch, Wrist Yaw): A spherical wrist whose three axes **intersect at a single wrist center**.

**Refer to the Mermaid schematic in SECTION 4.3 for the block diagram.**

**Step 2 — Apply Grübler's formula:**
- Number of moving links = 6, plus ground = 7 → n = 7
- All 6 joints are revolute → j₁ = 6, all others = 0

$$F = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) = 36 - 30 = 6$$

$$\boxed{F_{PUMA} = 6 \text{ DOF}}$$

**Step 3 — Significance:** 6 DOF equals the DOF of a rigid body in 3D space. Therefore, the PUMA can achieve **any arbitrary position and orientation** (any pose) within its workspace, making it a **dexterous manipulator**.

---

#### (b) Compare SCARA and PUMA robots based on: (i) DOF and joint configuration, (ii) workspace shape, (iii) applications, and (iv) compliance characteristics. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (Tabular):**

| Criterion | SCARA | PUMA |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **DOF & Joints** | 4 DOF, RRPR sequence | 6 DOF, RRRRRR sequence |
| (ii) **Workspace** | Cylindrical (thick disk), radius = L₁ + L₂ | Spherical shell, function of link twist angles |
| (iii) **Applications** | PCB assembly, pick-and-place, screwdriving | Arc welding, painting, machine tending |
| (iv) **Compliance** | Selectively compliant (X–Y flex, Z rigid) | Isotropically compliant (all directions) |

**Additional Inferences:**

1. **Mounting:** SCARA can be **floor or ceiling mounted** (due to symmetric vertical column); PUMA is **floor-mounted only** (anthropomorphic design assumes base-on-ground).

2. **Inverse Kinematics:** Both have **closed-form analytical solutions**; PUMA's solution is more complex due to the spherical wrist requiring Euler angle decomposition.

3. **Pieper's Criterion:** PUMA **satisfies** the criterion (concurrent wrist axes), enabling decoupling of position and orientation kinematics. SCARA does not require this since it is deficient-DOF (cannot reach arbitrary orientation).

4. **Cost & Complexity:** SCARA is **simpler and cheaper** (fewer joints, simpler controller). PUMA is **more expensive** (6 motors, 6 encoders, complex software).

5. **Selection Rule:** Use **SCARA** for **planar, high-speed, repetitive** tasks. Use **PUMA** for **3D, dexterous, orientation-critical** tasks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the ground link** in the link count (n) when applying Grübler's formula. This causes an off-by-one error. **Always count: n = (moving links) + 1.**
> 2. **Confusing the "selective compliance" direction.** Students often write "SCARA is rigid in X–Y and compliant in Z" — this is **backwards**. SCARA is **compliant in X–Y (lateral) and rigid in Z (vertical).**
> 3. **In numerical problems, students forget to convert degrees to radians** (or vice versa) when feeding into sin/cos functions. Use calculator in **degree mode** unless the problem specifies radians.
> 4. **In θ₁ calculation, using only `arctan` instead of `atan2`** loses quadrant information. Always use **atan2(y, x)** for robust results.
> 5. **Confusing SCARA's prismatic joint as a "lifting" joint only.** It is more correctly described as providing the **vertical (Z) degree of freedom** that is decoupled from X–Y motion.
> 6. **For PUMA, students often claim it has 7 DOF** because they miscount joints. The classic PUMA 560 has exactly **6 DOF** (verified by Grübler).
> 7. **Skipping the workspace verification** (checking whether target point is inside $(L_1 - L_2) \leq r \leq (L_1 + L_2)$) before computing joint angles. Always **check reachability first**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SCARA & PUMA (Module 3):**

### Core Identity Cards
- **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** → 4 DOF → RRPR → Yamanashi 1978.
- **PUMA = Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly** → 6 DOF → RRRRRR → Unimation 1978.

### Must-Memorize DOF Derivations
- SCARA: $F = 6(5-1) - 5(4) = 4$
- PUMA: $F = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 6$

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- Grübler spatial: $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- Open-chain simplification: $F = \sum f_i$
- 2R elbow angle: $\cos\theta_2 = \frac{r^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1 L_2}$
- SCARA z-axis: $d_3 = z$

### Must-Memorize Concepts
- **Selective compliance** = horizontal compliance + vertical rigidity.
- **Workspace of SCARA** = cylindrical disk.
- **Workspace of PUMA** = spherical shell.
- **Pieper's criterion** = PUMA satisfies it (spherical wrist).
- **3 + 3 decoupling** of PUMA: first 3 joints = position, last 3 joints = orientation.
- **SCARA mounting** = floor or ceiling; **PUMA mounting** = floor only.

### Must-Memorize Comparative Differences
- DOF (4 vs 6), Joint sequence (RRPR vs RRRRRR), Workspace (Cylinder vs Sphere), Application (PCB vs Welding), Compliance (Selective vs Isotropic), Mounting (Flexible vs Floor-only), Cost (Lower vs Higher).

### Quick-Recall Mnemonics
- **"SCARA is for Screwdrivers, SMD, and Sockets"** — planar electronics tasks.
- **"PUMA is for Paint, Polish, and Position-critical 3D"** — dexterous industrial tasks.

### Examiner's Golden Rule
> "Always include the **ground link** in your Grübler count, always **verify reachability** before solving, and always use **atan2** for inverse kinematics — these three habits alone will save 3–4 marks per ESE attempt."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Robots Based on Motion Control Methods and Drive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Classification of Robots Based on Motion Control Methods and Drive Technologies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OERUT611 – Fundamentals of Robotics (Module 3)**, robots are classified along two orthogonal engineering axes: 1. **Motion Control Methods** – *How* the robot is commanded, monitored, and corrected during motion (open-loop vs. closed-loop, servo vs. non-servo, point-to-point vs. continuous path). 2. **Drive Technologies** – *What physical actuator* converts electrical/fluid energy into mechanical motion at the joints (Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Module 3):** Classification based on **motion control** and **drive systems** forms the analytical foundation for selecting a robot for a given industrial task (assembly, welding, painting, material handling). It directly maps to **CO2**: *Analyze the classification and specifications of industrial robots.* ### 1.2 The Two-Axis Classification (Taxonomic Overview) | Axis | Sub-Classifications | Core Distinguishing Question | |---|---|---| | **Motion Control** | Servo (Closed-loop) / Non-Servo (Open-loop) | Is the actual position fed back to the controller? | | **Path Control** | Point-to-Point (PTP) / Continuous Path (CP) / Controlled Path | How many points are recorded between start and end? | | **Drive Technology** | Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric | What is the prime mover at the joint? | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Taxi vs. The Self-Driving Car" Imagine two ways of getting from **Kalamassery to Palakkad**: - **Open-Loop (Non-Servo) Robot = A Blindfolded Passenger in a Taxi.** The driver (controller) tells the wheel to turn exactly 1,250 times to reach the destination. The wheel turns exactly 1,250 times — but if the road is uphill, or the tyre is flat, **the car ends up somewhere else entirely**. There is **no feedback** to correct the error. This is how a **pneumatic pick-and-place robot** works: it fires the cylinder for a fixed stroke length. - **Closed-Loop (Servo) Robot = A Self-Driving Car with GPS.** Every 10 milliseconds, the GPS tells the car *"you are 200 m short of the target."* The steering and accelerator **automatically correct** based on this feedback. If a tyre goes flat, the car compensates. This is how an **articulated welding robot with DC servo motors** works: an encoder reports the actual joint angle, and the controller adjusts the voltage to drive the motor until the error = **0**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** A *servo* robot is not defined by the type of motor — it is defined by the **presence of a feedback loop**. An electric robot can be non-servo, and a hydraulic robot can be servo. ### 1.4 Definitions of Key Metrics - **Resolution:** The smallest incremental motion the robot can produce (typically **0.01 mm to 0.1 mm** for industrial robots). - **Repeatability:** The closeness of agreement between repeated position measurements under identical conditions. Industrial benchmark: **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** for high-precision servo robots. - **Accuracy:** The deviation between the *commanded* position and the *actual* achieved position. Always **worse than repeatability**. - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joint motions. Industrial robots typically have **4 to 6 DOF**. > [!TIP] > **For KTU Answers:** Always define both **Accuracy** and **Repeatability** explicitly in any comparison between servo and non-servo systems — this is a guaranteed 2-mark differentiator in Part A questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification Based on Motion Control Methods #### 2.1.1 Non-Servo (Open-Loop / Limited Sequence) Robots **Operational Logic:** 1. The controller sends a *binary* ON/OFF command to an actuator (solenoid valve, solenoid). 2. The actuator (pneumatic cylinder / DC motor with limit switches) executes a **fixed stroke** or **fixed rotation**. 3. **No feedback** sensor reports the actual position. 4. Motion is stopped by **mechanical stops** or **limit switches** at the end of travel. **Engineering Characteristics:** - **Architecture:** Bang-Bang control (digital, two-state). - **Feedback:** Absent. Open-loop. - **Sensors:** Only **end-of-travel limit switches**. - **Path Type:** Pure **Point-to-Point (PTP)** — stops between points; cannot trace a smooth path. - **Speed:** Very high acceleration/deceleration (up to **1 g**), but jerky. - **Payload:** Light to medium (typically up to **~50 kg**). - **Cost:** Lowest in the industry. - **Example:** Pneumatic pick-and-place arms in electronic assembly (e.g., Yamaha YK-X series SCARA variants in simple mode). #### 2.1.2 Servo (Closed-Loop) Robots **Operational Logic:** 1. The controller computes a **trajectory** (position vs. time) for each joint. 2. The current *commanded* position $\theta_{cmd}(t)$ is compared with the *actual* position $\theta_{act}(t)$ from an **encoder** or **resolver**. 3. The **error signal** $e(t) = \theta_{cmd}(t) - \theta_{act}(t)$ is fed to a **PID controller**. 4. The controller outputs a continuous voltage/current to the motor that **minimizes** $e(t)$ to zero. **Engineering Characteristics:** - **Architecture:** Continuous analog or digital closed-loop control (typically PID). - **Feedback:** Encoders (optical/magnetic), resolvers, tachogenerators. - **Path Type:** Supports **Point-to-Point (PTP)**, **Continuous Path (CP)**, and **Controlled Path (CP)**. - **Speed:** Smooth, programmable velocity profiles (trapezoidal, S-curve, polynomial). - **Payload:** Light to very heavy (**up to 1000+ kg** for heavy-payload hydraulic servo robots like KUKA KR 1000). - **Cost:** 3× to 10× a non-servo equivalent. - **Example:** 6-axis articulated welding robots (e.g., ABB IRB, FANUC M-20iA). > [!NOTE] > **Path Control Sub-Classification (Critical for KTU):** > - **PTP (Point-to-Point):** Robot moves from A to B; path *between* is not controlled. Used for **spot welding, palletizing, pick-and-place**. > - **CP (Continuous Path):** Entire path is recorded (e.g., by teach-pendant drag-teach). Used for **arc welding, painting, polishing, sealing**. > - **Controlled Path:** A hybrid — the robot moves in straight lines or circles between a few key points, with controlled velocity. Used for **assembly and water-jet cutting**. ### 2.2 Classification Based on Drive Technology #### 2.2.1 Hydraulic Drives - **Working Fluid:** Mineral oil (typically at **70–200 bar**, i.e., **7–20 MPa**). - **Actuators:** Hydraulic cylinders (linear) and hydraulic motors (rotary, vane or piston type). - **Power Source:** Electric motor driving a hydraulic pump; control via **servo valves** (proportional or bang-bang). - **Strengths:** Highest **power-to-weight ratio**; high payload capacity (up to **1000 kg+**); inherent compliance (good for grinding); self-lubricating; no overheating under stall. - **Weaknesses:** Leakage (oil contamination); requires pump, reservoir, filter, cooler; **low speed accuracy** at very low velocities; high maintenance; environmental concerns. - **Typical Industrial Use:** Heavy-payload manipulators, die-casting, forging, large-payload spot welding in automotive body shops. #### 2.2.2 Pneumatic Drives - **Working Fluid:** Compressed air (typically at **4–8 bar**, i.e., **0.4–0.8 MPa**). - **Actuators:** Pneumatic cylinders (mostly linear), pneumatic motors (rotary, vane type). - **Power Source:** Air compressor + filter + regulator + lubricator (FRL unit). - **Strengths:** **Cheapest** drive technology; clean (no leakage contamination); very high speeds; simple control; readily available in any factory. - **Weaknesses:** **Incompressibility low** — air is compressible, so positioning is *inherently inaccurate* (open-loop, non-servo); low payload; requires compressor infrastructure; noisy. - **Typical Industrial Use:** Light-payload pick-and-place, packaging, small parts assembly, simple material transfer. #### 2.2.3 Electric Drives - **Working Fluid:** Electrical energy directly. - **Actuators:** **DC servo motors, AC servo motors, brushless DC (BLDC), stepper motors, linear motors**. - **Power Source:** Mains AC → rectifier → DC bus → PWM amplifier → motor. - **Strengths:** **Highest precision and repeatability** (sub-0.01 mm possible); clean (no fluids); no compressor or pump needed; quiet; excellent speed control over a wide range; easily computer-interfaced. - **Weaknesses:** Lower power-to-weight than hydraulic; heat dissipation at high torque; **risk of overheating under stall** (unlike hydraulic). - **Typical Industrial Use:** **Dominant in modern industry** (>70% of new installations) — precision assembly, arc welding, painting, electronics, cleanrooms, medical robotics. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Reference) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Equations for Robot Drive Sizing** | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Joint Torque Required** | $\tau = I \alpha + \tau_{g} + \tau_{f}$ | N·m | Sum of inertia torque, gravity torque, friction torque | | 2 | **Effective Inertia (reflected to motor shaft)** | $I_{eff} = I_{motor} + I_{load}\left(\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}\right)^{2}$ | kg·m² | Reflected inertia through gear ratio $N_1/N_2$ | | 3 | **Power Required at Joint** | $P = \tau \cdot \omega = \tau \cdot \frac{2\pi n}{60}$ | W | For motor sizing | | 4 | **Hydraulic Cylinder Force** | $F = P_{oil} \cdot A_{piston} = P_{oil} \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | N | Linear hydraulic actuator thrust | | 5 | **Pneumatic Cylinder Force** | $F = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$ | N | Where $\eta \approx 0.85$ is mechanical efficiency | | 6 | **Servo Position Error (Steady State)** | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_{p}K}$ | rad or mm | For a unit-step input (P-controller alone) | | 7 | **PID Control Law** | $u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int e(t)\, dt + K_{d} \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | V or A | Industry-standard robot joint controller | | 8 | **Stepper Motor Resolution** | $\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}}{N_{steps/rev} \cdot m_{micro}}$ | degrees/step | For $m_{micro}$ = microstepping factor | | 9 | **Robot Repeatability (Statistical)** | $R = \pm k \cdot \sigma$ | mm | Typically $k = 2$ or $3$ | | 10 | **Degrees of Freedom (Workspace)** | $n_{DOF} = n_{links} - n_{constraints}$ | — | Kutzbach criterion (planar/spatial) | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Strategy:** For KTU Part A (3-mark) questions, memorize equations **1, 4, 7** — these appear in nearly every robot drive question. For Part B, be ready to use **equations 1, 2, 3** together for a 14-mark motor-sizing problem. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This classification directly drives **robot selection** in an industrial cell: - **Choose Pneumatic** when payload < **5 kg**, cycle time is short, accuracy is not critical, and cost dominates. → *Pharmaceutical blister packaging line.* - **Choose Hydraulic** when payload > **200 kg**, and there is a risk of stall. → *Press-to-press transfer in a 10,000-ton forging press.* - **Choose Electric Servo** when **precision, smoothness, multi-axis coordination, and reprogrammability** are required. → *6-axis arc welding of a car chassis.* ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Inertia at the Motor Shaft (Gear-Train Reflected Inertia) This is a **core KTU 14-mark derivation** — it appears in problems involving motor sizing for a robot joint. **Problem Statement:** A robot joint has a motor with rotor inertia $I_m$ and a gear train with ratio $N = N_1/N_2$ (motor gear : load gear). The load inertia is $I_L$. Find the effective inertia as seen by the motor. **Step 1 — Define the gear ratio.** Let the motor shaft rotate at angular velocity $\omega_m$ and the load shaft rotate at $\omega_L$. By the law of gearing: $$ N = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_L} = \frac{\theta_m}{\theta_L} $$ *Explanation:* The motor gear has $N_1$ teeth, the load gear has $N_2$ teeth. The ratio $N$ represents the speed reduction (and torque amplification) factor. **Step 2 — Write the kinetic energy of the rotating system.** The total kinetic energy of the system must be conserved between the motor and load sides: $$ KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} I_m \omega_m^{2} + \frac{1}{2} I_L \omega_L^{2} $$ *Explanation:* The motor and the load are two rotating bodies; each has its own KE based on its own moment of inertia and angular speed. **Step 3 — Substitute the gear relation to express everything at the motor shaft.** Since $\omega_L = \dfrac{\omega_m}{N}$: $$ KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} I_m \omega_m^{2} + \frac{1}{2} I_L \left(\frac{\omega_m}{N}\right)^{2} $$ *Explanation:* We express the load speed in terms of the motor speed so we can combine the inertias. **Step 4 — Factor out $\frac{1}{2}\omega_m^{2}$.** $$ KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \omega_m^{2} \left[ I_m + \frac{I_L}{N^{2}} \right] $$ *Explanation:* This is the key transformation — the load inertia is *reflected* to the motor shaft, divided by $N^{2}$. **Step 5 — Define the effective inertia.** By analogy with a single rotating mass, $KE = \frac{1}{2} I_{eff} \omega_m^{2}$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,I_{eff} = I_m + \frac{I_L}{N^{2}}\,} $$ *Explanation:* The motor designer uses $I_{eff}$ to compute the torque needed to accelerate the joint, because all inertias have been "moved" to a common reference (the motor shaft). > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** A high gear ratio $N$ dramatically reduces the *reflected* load inertia. This is why harmonic-drive reducers (with $N \approx 100:1$ to $160:1$) are ubiquitous in robot joints — they make the motor "feel" a tiny load, enabling fast acceleration. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error of a Proportional-Only Servo Controller This addresses the question: *"When a constant joint position is commanded, does a P-controller ever reach zero error?"* **Step 1 — Define the open-loop transfer function.** For a DC motor + load with proportional gain $K_p$ and plant gain $K$: $$ G(s) = \frac{K_{p} K}{(Js + b)(Ls + R) + K^{2}} $$ *Explanation:* $J$ is the effective inertia, $b$ is damping, $L$ and $R$ are motor inductance and resistance, $K$ is the motor constant. **Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for a unit step input $\theta_{cmd}(s) = 1/s$.** $$ e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} $$ *Explanation:* The error in the s-domain is the difference between the input and the closed-loop output. For a unity-feedback system, $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$, and $C(s)/R(s) = G/(1+G)$, so $E(s)/R(s) = 1/(1+G)$. **Step 3 — Evaluate the limit.** $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K / (\text{denominator at } s=0)} $$ For a typical motor, the denominator at $s=0$ is finite (call it $D_0$). So: $$ \boxed{\,e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K / D_0} \neq 0\,} $$ *Explanation:* A *pure* P-controller always has a **non-zero steady-state error** for a step input — this is a fundamental limitation. It is why industrial robot controllers use **PI or PID** (the integral term drives the error to exactly zero). --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Hydraulic Cylinder Force & Sizing Example (Typical KTU Numerical) **Problem:** A hydraulic robot joint uses a cylinder of piston diameter $D = 80$ mm. The oil pressure is set to $P_{oil} = 100$ bar. The mechanical efficiency is $\eta = 0.9$. The load is $F_{load} = 40{,}000$ N. Determine if the cylinder can lift the load and compute the resulting acceleration if the moving mass is $m = 200$ kg. **Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units (Pa).** $$ P_{oil} = 100 \text{ bar} \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa/bar} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 10^{7} \text{ Pa} $$ *Explanation:* 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa (exact by definition for many engineering applications). **Step 2 — Compute piston area.** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} $$ *Explanation:* Standard formula for the cross-sectional area of a circular piston. **Step 3 — Compute the theoretical thrust force.** $$ F_{ideal} = P_{oil} \cdot A = 10^{7} \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} $$ *Explanation:* Force equals pressure times area — Pascal's law applied to a hydraulic cylinder. **Step 4 — Apply mechanical efficiency.** $$ F_{useful} = F_{ideal} \cdot \eta = 50{,}265 \times 0.9 = 45{,}239 \text{ N} $$ *Explanation:* Efficiency accounts for seal friction and friction in the cylinder bearings. **Step 5 — Compare with load.** Since $F_{useful} = 45{,}239$ N $> F_{load} = 40{,}000$ N, the cylinder **can** lift the load. **Step 6 — Compute net force available for acceleration.** $$ F_{net} = F_{useful} - F_{load} = 45{,}239 - 40{,}000 = 5{,}239 \text{ N} $$ **Step 7 — Compute acceleration using Newton's Second Law.** $$ a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{5{,}239}{200} = 26.2 \text{ m/s}^{2} $$ *Explanation:* This is roughly **2.67 g** — a very high acceleration, typical of hydraulic robots. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation:** The hydraulic robot can lift the 40 kN load with a safety margin of $\frac{45{,}239 - 40{,}000}{40{,}000} = 13.1\%$ and accelerate it at over 2.5 g. This validates the choice of an 80-mm bore cylinder at 100 bar for a heavy-payload robot. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Servo vs. Non-Servo / Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic vs. Electric) | Engineering Parameter | Non-Servo (Open-Loop) | Servo (Closed-Loop) | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Feedback** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (encoder) | ✅ or ❌ (depends) | ❌ Usually no | ✅ Yes | | **Position Resolution** | Low (limit-switch based) | High (0.01–0.1 mm) | Medium (0.1–1 mm) | Poor (>1 mm) | Highest (<0.01 mm) | | **Repeatability** | ±1 to ±5 mm | ±0.02 to ±0.1 mm | ±0.5 to ±2 mm | ±2 to ±10 mm | ±0.02 to ±0.05 mm | | **Max Payload (industrial)** | <50 kg | up to 1000+ kg | up to 1500 kg | <20 kg | up to 800 kg | | **Max Speed (joint)** | Medium | High | High | Very High | High | | **Power-to-Weight Ratio** | Low | Medium | **Highest** | Low | Medium | | **Cost** | **Lowest** | High | High (pump infrastructure) | **Lowest** | Medium | | **Maintenance** | Low | Medium | High (leaks, filters) | Low | **Lowest** | | **Cleanliness** | Medium (electric) | High | **Low** (oil) | **High** (air) | **High** | | **Path Capability** | PTP only | PTP, CP, Controlled | PTP, CP | PTP only | PTP, CP, Controlled | | **Typical Control** | Bang-bang | PID | Servo-valve PID | Solenoid ON/OFF | PWM PID | | **Example Application** | Pick-and-place | Arc welding | Heavy forging | Packaging | Precision assembly | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification Decision Tree (Two-Axis Taxonomy) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Industrial Robot] --> B{Classification Axis} B --> C[Motion Control Method] B --> D[Drive Technology] C --> E{Is feedback used?} E -->|NO| F[Non-Servo
Open-Loop
Limit Switches] E -->|YES| G[Servo
Closed-Loop
Encoder + PID] F --> F1[Point-to-Point PTP only] G --> G1[PTP + CP + Controlled Path] D --> H{Working Medium?} H -->|Compressed Air| I[Pneumatic Drive] H -->|Hydraulic Oil| J[Hydraulic Drive] H -->|Electricity| K[Electric Drive] I --> I1[Cylinder + Solenoid Valve] J --> J1[Cylinder + Servo Valve] K --> K1[DC/AC Servo + PWM Amp] F1 --> X[Pick-and-Place Packaging] G1 --> Y[Arc Welding / Painting / CNC Tending] I1 --> X J1 --> Z[Heavy Forging / Die Casting] K1 --> Y ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Servo (Closed-Loop) Robot Joint Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Trajectory Planner
theta_cmd of t] --> B[Comparator
e = theta_cmd - theta_act] B --> C[PID Controller
Kp, Ki, Kd] C --> D[PWM Power Amplifier] D --> E[Servo Motor
DC or AC] E --> F[Harmonic Drive
Gearbox N:1] F --> G[Robot Joint
Link + Load] G --> H[Encoder / Resolver
Position Sensor] H --> B style A fill:#ffe4b5 style C fill:#b0e0e6 style E fill:#98fb98 style H fill:#ffb6c1 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Non-Servo (Open-Loop) Pneumatic Pick-and-Place ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PLC or Microcontroller] --> B[Relay / Solenoid Driver] B --> C[5/2 Solenoid Valve] C -->|Compressed Air 6 bar| D[Pneumatic Cylinder] D --> E[End-of-Stroke
Limit Switch 1] D --> F[End-of-Stroke
Limit Switch 2] E --> A F --> A style A fill:#ffe4b5 style C fill:#b0e0e6 style D fill:#98fb98 style E fill:#d3d3d3 style F fill:#d3d3d3 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Robot Drive System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Central Controller
Industrial PC or PLC] --> B[Joint Controller Card
DSP or FPGA] B --> C[Power Electronics
PWM Inverter / Servo Valve Driver] C --> D[Prime Mover
Electric Motor / Hydraulic Pump / Air Compressor] D --> E[Transmission
Gears / Harmonic Drive] E --> F[Robot Link
Mechanical Output] F --> G[End Effector
Gripper or Tool] F --> H[Feedback Sensor
Encoder / Resolver / LVDT] H --> B P[Power Supply
Mains 415V AC 3-phase] --> C Q[Hydraulic Reservoir
or Air Receiver] --> D style A fill:#ffe4b5 style B fill:#b0e0e6 style C fill:#ffb6c1 style D fill:#98fb98 style H fill:#dda0dd ``` ### 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Path Control Methods (Visual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Path Control Classification] --> B[Point-to-Point PTP] A --> C[Continuous Path CP] A --> D[Controlled Path] B --> B1[Spot Welding] B --> B2[Palletizing] B --> B3[Pick and Place] C --> C1[Arc Welding] C --> C2[Painting] C --> C3[Polishing / Buffing] C --> C4[Sealant Application] D --> D1[Precision Assembly] D --> D2[Water Jet Cutting] D --> D3[Gluing] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to Read These Diagrams for KTU Exams:** In 14-mark questions, you can reproduce the *Servo Joint Control Block Diagram* (4.2) and label each block with the relevant equation (PID law, motor torque equation, encoder resolution). This single diagram is worth 3–4 marks if drawn correctly. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Differentiate between servo and non-servo robots with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Servo Robot | Non-Servo Robot | |---|---|---| | **Control Type** | Closed-loop | Open-loop | | **Feedback** | Continuous (encoder/resolver) | Limited (limit switches only) | | **Path** | PTP, CP, Controlled | PTP only | | **Accuracy** | High (0.01–0.1 mm) | Low (1–5 mm) | | **Cost** | High | Low | | **Example** | 6-axis arc-welding robot (e.g., ABB IRB 1410) | Pneumatic pick-and-place (e.g., Yamaha YK500) | *(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for the comparison table + 1 mark for examples.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** **Compare hydraulic, pneumatic, and electric drive systems for robots.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Hydraulic:** Highest power-to-weight ratio; suitable for heavy-payload (500–1000+ kg) applications like forging and die-casting. Drawbacks: oil leakage, requires pump and filtration. *(1 mark)* - **Pneumatic:** Cheapest and cleanest; uses compressed air at 4–8 bar. Limited to light payload (<20 kg) and open-loop applications like packaging. *(1 mark)* - **Electric:** Highest precision and repeatability; dominant in modern installations (>70%); uses DC/AC servo or stepper motors with PWM amplifiers. Used in precision assembly, arc welding, cleanroom robotics. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - In servo vs. non-servo questions, students often **confuse "servo" with "servo motor"** — remember, *servo* refers to the *control architecture*, not the motor. A stepper-motor robot can be non-servo. > - In drive-comparison questions, students forget to mention **working pressure** and **power-to-weight ratio** — these are the two quantitative differentiators that examiners expect. > - In numerical problems, **always convert bar → Pa** before computing forces. Losing a unit conversion is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > - For path-control questions, students often claim *"CP is better than PTP"* — this is wrong. They are **task-dependent**, not superior/inferior. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Modified Past Pattern]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a closed-loop servo-controlled robot joint. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic robot joint uses a double-acting cylinder with a piston diameter of **100 mm** and a rod diameter of **50 mm**. The supply pressure is **120 bar** and the return pressure is **5 bar**. The load is **70 kN** during the extension stroke and **30 kN** during retraction. The mechanical efficiency is **0.88**. Determine the effective thrust in both directions and verify if the cylinder can move the load. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks:** *Block Diagram (drawn in answer sheet — 3 marks):* ``` [Trajectory Planner] -> [Comparator] -> [PID Controller] -> [PWM Amplifier] | [Encoder] <-------------------- [Robot Joint] <----- [Motor] <--------+ |__________________________| ``` *Explanation of each block (4 marks):* 1. **Trajectory Planner:** Generates the desired joint angle $\theta_{cmd}(t)$ as a function of time using a polynomial (cubic or quintic) for smooth motion. *(1 mark)* 2. **Comparator (Summing Junction):** Computes the position error $e(t) = \theta_{cmd}(t) - \theta_{act}(t)$. *(1 mark)* 3. **PID Controller:** Processes the error and outputs a control signal $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$. The proportional term reacts to current error, the integral term eliminates steady-state error, the derivative term damps overshoot. *(1 mark)* 4. **PWM Power Amplifier:** Converts the low-power control signal into a high-power PWM waveform that drives the motor with the required voltage and current. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Servo Motor + Gearbox:** Converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical motion, geared down to the joint speed via a harmonic drive. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Encoder / Resolver:** Mounted on the motor shaft (or joint), reports the actual position $\theta_{act}$ back to the comparator, closing the loop. *(0.5 mark)* --- **Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks:** *Given:* - Piston diameter $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m - Rod diameter $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m - Supply pressure $P_s = 120$ bar $= 120 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 1.2 \times 10^{7}$ Pa - Return (back) pressure $P_b = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa - Mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.88$ - Extension load $F_{ext,load} = 70$ kN $= 70{,}000$ N - Retraction load $F_{ret,load} = 30$ kN $= 30{,}000$ N **Step 1 — Compute piston area (full bore).** $$ A_p = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} $$ *Explanation:* Standard area formula. **Step 2 — Compute annular area (rod side).** $$ A_r = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1^{2} - 0.05^{2})}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.0025)}{4} = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} $$ *Explanation:* During retraction, oil acts on the annular area (piston area minus the rod area). **Step 3 — Effective force during extension.** The net pressure pushing the piston is $(P_s - P_b)$. With efficiency: $$ F_{ext,useful} = (P_s - P_b) \cdot A_p \cdot \eta $$ $$ F_{ext,useful} = (1.2 \times 10^{7} - 5 \times 10^{5}) \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.88 $$ $$ F_{ext,useful} = (1.15 \times 10^{7}) \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.88 = 79{,}478 \text{ N} \approx 79.5 \text{ kN} $$ *Explanation:* The efficiency factor multiplies the ideal force, accounting for seal friction and bearing drag. **Step 4 — Compare with extension load.** Since $F_{ext,useful} = 79.5$ kN $> F_{ext,load} = 70$ kN, the cylinder **can extend the load**. ✅ *Margin:* $\frac{79.5 - 70}{70} \times 100\% = 13.6\%$ — healthy margin. **Step 5 — Effective force during retraction.** $$ F_{ret,useful} = (P_s - P_b) \cdot A_r \cdot \eta = 1.15 \times 10^{7} \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.88 = 59{,}609 \text{ N} \approx 59.6 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 6 — Compare with retraction load.** Since $F_{ret,useful} = 59.6$ kN $> F_{ret,load} = 30$ kN, the cylinder **can retract the load** with a large margin (≈98.7%). ✅ **Conclusion (1 mark):** The double-acting hydraulic cylinder is well-sized for both extension and retraction of the given loads. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** Classify robots based on (i) motion control methods and (ii) drive technologies. Tabulate the differences and provide two industrial applications for each type. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6-axis articulated robot uses a DC servo motor at its shoulder joint. The effective inertia reflected at the motor shaft is $I_{eff} = 0.02$ kg·m². The motor must accelerate the joint from 0 to **1.5 rad/s** in **0.3 s**, while also overcoming a gravity torque of **15 N·m** and a friction torque of **5 N·m**. Compute: (i) the acceleration torque, (ii) the total torque required, and (iii) the peak power during the acceleration phase. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 marks:** **Classification (3 marks for the table):** | Basis | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | |---|---|---|---| | **Motion Control** | Non-Servo (Open-loop) | Servo (Closed-loop) | — | | **Path Control** | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Continuous Path (CP) | Controlled Path | | **Drive Technology** | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric | **Applications (4 marks — 1 per drive + 1 per motion control):** - **Non-Servo:** Pick-and-place in electronic assembly; packaging-line sorting. *(1 mark)* - **Servo:** Arc welding of car chassis; precision CNC tending. *(1 mark)* - **Hydraulic:** Forging-press loading; 1000+ kg die casting. *(1 mark)* - **Pneumatic:** Bottle-capping in beverage industry; small-parts transfer. *(1 mark)* --- **Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 marks:** *Given:* - Effective inertia $I_{eff} = 0.02$ kg·m² - Final angular velocity $\omega_f = 1.5$ rad/s - Initial angular velocity $\omega_i = 0$ - Time $t = 0.3$ s - Gravity torque $\tau_g = 15$ N·m - Friction torque $\tau_f = 5$ N·m **Step 1 — Compute angular acceleration.** $$ \alpha = \frac{\omega_f - \omega_i}{t} = \frac{1.5 - 0}{0.3} = 5 \text{ rad/s}^{2} $$ *Explanation:* This is the rate at which the joint must change its angular velocity. **Step 2 — Compute acceleration (inertia) torque.** $$ \tau_{acc} = I_{eff} \cdot \alpha = 0.02 \times 5 = 0.1 \text{ N·m} $$ *Explanation:* Newton's rotational law: torque = inertia × angular acceleration. **Step 3 — Compute total torque required.** $$ \tau_{total} = \tau_{acc} + \tau_g + \tau_f = 0.1 + 15 + 5 = 20.1 \text{ N·m} $$ *Explanation:* The motor must overcome the inertia torque, the gravity torque (from the weight of the arm), and the friction torque. All three add vectorially for the worst case. **Step 4 — Compute peak power during acceleration.** Peak power occurs at the end of the acceleration phase (at $\omega = 1.5$ rad/s): $$ P_{peak} = \tau_{total} \cdot \omega_f = 20.1 \times 1.5 = 30.15 \text{ W} $$ *Explanation:* Power = torque × angular speed. At the start ($\omega = 0$) the power is zero; it grows linearly to its peak at the end of the acceleration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Note:** Notice that the inertia torque is only **0.1 N·m**, while the gravity + friction torques total **20 N·m** — i.e., the *static* loads dominate. This is typical for a robot shoulder joint, where the arm's own weight is the largest design constraint. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Two orthogonal axes** of robot classification: **Motion Control** and **Drive Technology**. - [ ] **Servo ≠ Servo motor.** Servo refers to **closed-loop control architecture**; a stepper motor is *not* a servo system. - [ ] **Three drive types:** Hydraulic (heavy, oil, 70–200 bar), Pneumatic (light, air, 4–8 bar), Electric (precise, dominant, PWM-driven). - [ ] **Three path types:** PTP (spot weld), CP (arc weld/paint), Controlled Path (assembly/cut). - [ ] **Electric drives dominate modern industry** (>70% of new installations). - [ ] **Hydraulic** = highest payload (1000+ kg); **Pneumatic** = cheapest; **Electric** = most precise. - [ ] **Key equation (Torque sizing):** $\tau = I_{eff} \alpha + \tau_{gravity} + \tau_{friction}$ - [ ] **Key equation (Reflected inertia):** $I_{eff} = I_{motor} + \dfrac{I_{load}}{N^{2}}$ where $N$ is the gear ratio. - [ ] **Key equation (Hydraulic force):** $F = P_{oil} \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$ - [ ] **Key equation (PID control):** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ - [ ] **Non-servo robots** are open-loop → limited to PTP → use limit switches → cheapest. - [ ] **Servo robots** are closed-loop → support PTP, CP, Controlled Path → use encoders → highest accuracy. - [ ] **For KTU answers, always** include the block diagram + at least one numerical example per 7-mark sub-question. - [ ] **Memorize the unit conversions:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; $1$ in = $25.4$ mm; $1$ hp = $746$ W. - [ ] **Pneumatic cylinders** are inherently non-servo (compressibility prevents accurate position control) — use them only for end-point tasks. - [ ] **Robot repeatability ≠ accuracy.** Repeatability is *much better* than accuracy; in industry we usually quote repeatability. - [ ] **A harmonic-drive reducer** (N ≈ 100:1) is the standard transmission for a servo robot joint because it drastically reduces the reflected inertia. - [ ] **Block diagrams are mandatory** for any 7-mark control or drive question in KTU 2024 Scheme — they alone can earn 3–4 marks. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare and contrast," always present the answer as a **table** (not prose). KTU examiners scan tables first and award marks faster for clearly visible distinctions.

topics.title: 3R Concurrent Wrist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 3R Concurrent Wrist

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 3R Concurrent Wrist — Module 3 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **3R Concurrent Wrist** (also called a **Spherical Wrist**) is a wrist sub-assembly of a robotic manipulator composed of **three revolute (R) joints** whose rotational axes **intersect at a single common point**, known as the **wrist center** or **concurrent point**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is formally referred to as a *kinematically decoupled spherical wrist*, and it is the standard end-effector orientation module used on industrial robots (e.g., PUMA 560, ABB IRB series, FANUC LR-Mate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A wrist is classified as *spherical* if and only if all three joint axes intersect at one point. This geometric property is the foundation of the **Decoupling Theorem** (also called the *Pieper Criterion*), which states that the inverse kinematics problem of a 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist can be separated into two independent sub-problems:
> 1. **Position Inverse Kinematics** — solved using the first 3 (positioning) joints.
> 2. **Orientation Inverse Kinematics** — solved using the last 3 (wrist) joints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a pen with your hand:
- Your **shoulder and elbow** position the pen tip at a point on the table (this is the *positioning* part).
- Your **wrist** (which can roll, pitch, and yaw) orients the pen to point in the desired direction (this is the *orientation* part).

The 3R concurrent wrist is the mechanical "wrist" of a robot arm. The *concurrent* nature means that the three little motors that roll, pitch, and yaw the gripper all sit at the **same point in space**, so spinning them does not move the tool center point (TCP). It only changes the *direction* the tool is pointing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world anchor:** A human wrist is **not** a perfect 3R concurrent wrist because the carpal bones are offset, but a robot's spherical wrist is geometrically cleaner — like a tiny **universal-joint with an extra roll axis**, all meeting at one ball-joint center.

### 1.3 Physical Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Standard wrist joint range | $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ | $\pm 180^\circ$ (or $\pm 360^\circ$ depending on vendor) |
| Euler angle convention used | ZYZ or ZYX (Roll-Pitch-Yaw) | Industry standard for 6-DOF arms |
| Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | $n$ | **3** (pure orientation) |
| Tool Center Point (TCP) displacement during wrist rotation | $\Delta p$ | **0** (zero, by design) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why "Concurrent"? — The Decoupling Property

The concurrent design is not aesthetic — it is mathematically powerful. The **3 × 3 rotation matrix of the wrist** becomes a function of **only the three wrist joint angles**, with no translational component. This means:

- The first 3 joints determine **where** the wrist center is in space.
- The last 3 joints determine **how the end-effector is oriented**.

The general form of the wrist rotation matrix (using ZYZ Euler angles, the most common KTU convention) is:

$$
R_{0}^{6} = R_{0}^{3} \cdot R_{3}^{6}
$$

where:
- $R_{0}^{3}$ depends only on $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ (positioning joints).
- $R_{3}^{6}$ depends only on $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ (wrist joints).

Because the axes intersect, the position of the end-effector is:

$$
p_{0}^{6} = p_{0}^{3}
$$

i.e., the TCP position equals the wrist-center position. This is the **decoupling theorem**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The decoupling drastically simplifies real-time control. Industrial robot controllers (e.g., ABB Rapid, KUKA KRL, FANUC TP) solve position and orientation as two independent blocks, enabling faster cycle times — a key reason every modern 6-axis industrial robot uses a 3R concurrent wrist.

## 2.2 Euler Angle Parameterizations (KTU High-Yield)

The wrist rotation $R_3^6$ is most commonly expressed using **Euler angles**. The two parameterizations you must master for KTU exams are:

### (a) ZYZ Euler Angles (Fixed / Classical Euler Convention)

The wrist rotates about the moving $z$-axis, then the new $y$-axis, then the new $z$-axis:

$$
R_{ZYZ}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_z(\phi)\, R_y(\theta)\, R_z(\psi)
$$

where:
- $\phi$ (precession) = $\theta_4$ (first wrist joint)
- $\theta$ (nutation) = $\theta_5$ (second wrist joint)
- $\psi$ (spin) = $\theta_6$ (third wrist joint)

### (b) ZYX Euler Angles (Roll-Pitch-Yaw)

The wrist rotates about fixed-frame $z$ (yaw), then $y$ (pitch), then $x$ (roll):

$$
R_{ZYX}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = R_z(\alpha)\, R_y(\beta)\, R_x(\gamma)
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the structure of $R_{ZYZ}$ and $R_{ZYX}$ — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-3 question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decoupling theorem (position) | $p_0^6 = p_0^3$ | Position is independent of $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ |
| 2 | Decoupling theorem (orientation) | $R_0^6 = R_0^3 \cdot R_3^6$ | Orientation split into arm + wrist parts |
| 3 | Wrist rotation, ZYZ | $R_3^6 = R_z(\theta_4) R_y(\theta_5) R_z(\theta_6)$ | Standard for PUMA-class robots |
| 4 | Wrist rotation, ZYX | $R_3^6 = R_z(\alpha) R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma)$ | Standard for aerospace / mobile robots |
| 5 | Inverse: find $R_3^6$ | $R_3^6 = (R_0^3)^{-1} R_0^6 = (R_0^3)^{T} R_0^6$ | $R^{-1} = R^{T}$ for rotation matrices |
| 6 | Nutation angle (ZYZ) | $\theta_5 = \cos^{-1}(r_{33})$ | $r_{33}$ is the (3,3) element of $R_3^6$ |
| 7 | Singularity condition | $\theta_5 = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$ | $\sin \theta_5 = 0$ — gimbal lock |
| 8 | Wrist-center offset | $p_{wc} = p_{end} - d_6 \cdot \hat{z}_{tool}$ | $d_6$ is the tool length along $z_6$ |
| 9 | Number of IK solutions | Up to **8** (2 from arm × 4 from wrist) | For non-singular configurations |
| 10 | Wrist DOF | $n = 3$ | Pure orientation, zero translation |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The 3R concurrent wrist is the *de facto* standard in:
- **Automotive welding robots** (e.g., PUMA 760, KUKA KR 1000)
- **Surgical robots** (da Vinci EndoWrist — a cable-driven 3R concurrent wrist scaled to 8 mm)
- **Space manipulators** (Canadarm2 has a 3R wrist with 6 sensors for teleoperation)
- **CNC tool changers** for 5-axis machining (rotation matrices derived above drive the post-processor)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wrist Rotation Matrix in ZYZ Form

The three elementary rotations of the wrist about the moving frame are:

**Step 1 — Rotation about $z_3$ by angle $\theta_4$:**

$$
R_z(\theta_4) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_4 & -\sin\theta_4 & 0 \\
\sin\theta_4 & \cos\theta_4 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotation about $y_4$ by angle $\theta_5$:**

$$
R_y(\theta_5) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_5 & 0 & \sin\theta_5 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-\sin\theta_5 & 0 & \cos\theta_5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotation about $z_5$ by angle $\theta_6$:**

$$
R_z(\theta_6) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_6 & -\sin\theta_6 & 0 \\
\sin\theta_6 & \cos\theta_6 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply them sequentially to obtain $R_3^6 = R_z(\theta_4) R_y(\theta_5) R_z(\theta_6)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^6 = 
&\begin{bmatrix}
c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\
s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\
-s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the shorthand $c_i = \cos\theta_i$ and $s_i = \sin\theta_i$ has been used. This is the **canonical ZYZ wrist matrix** you must know by heart for the KTU exam.

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Wrist Kinematics (Given Desired Orientation)

You are given the desired end-effector orientation $R_0^6$ (a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix) and the arm orientation $R_0^3$ (computed from the already-solved $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$). Find $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$.

**Step 1 — Compute the required wrist rotation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^6 &= (R_0^3)^{-1} \cdot R_0^6 \\
R_3^6 &= (R_0^3)^{T} \cdot R_0^6 \quad \text{(since $R^{-1} = R^{T}$ for rotation matrices)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let the resulting matrix be:

$$
R_3^6 = 
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\
r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\
r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_5$ (the nutation angle):**

Equate the (3,3) element of $R_3^6$ to $c_5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{33} &= \cos\theta_5 \\
\therefore \theta_5 &= \cos^{-1}(r_{33}) = \text{atan2}\!\left(\pm\sqrt{r_{13}^{2} + r_{23}^{2}},\, r_{33}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Two solutions exist for $\theta_5$ (positive and negative nutation), giving the **2-fold wrist ambiguity**.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_4$:**

Divide the (1,3) element by the (2,3) element (this isolates $\tan\theta_4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{13} &= \cos\theta_4 \sin\theta_5 \\
r_{23} &= \sin\theta_4 \sin\theta_5 \\
\frac{r_{23}}{r_{13}} &= \frac{\sin\theta_4}{\cos\theta_4} = \tan\theta_4 \\
\therefore \theta_4 &= \text{atan2}(r_{23},\, r_{13})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_6$:**

Divide the (3,1) element by the (3,2) element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{31} &= -\sin\theta_5 \cos\theta_6 \\
r_{32} &= \sin\theta_5 \sin\theta_6 \\
\frac{r_{32}}{r_{31}} &= -\frac{\sin\theta_6}{\cos\theta_6} = -\tan\theta_6 \\
\therefore \theta_6 &= \text{atan2}(-r_{32},\, r_{31})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Singularity Analysis

**Condition:** A 3R concurrent wrist loses one DOF (degenerates to 2 DOF) when:

$$
\sin\theta_5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_5 = 0^\circ \text{ or } 180^\circ
$$

**Physical meaning:** When the second wrist joint aligns the first and third wrist axes, rotations about $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ produce the same end-effector orientation — a *gimbal-lock* situation. The manipulator Jacobian loses rank, and the robot cannot produce angular velocity about the axis of alignment.

**Engineering countermeasure:** Most industrial controllers re-route $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ through a *continuation* algorithm (e.g., ABB's singularity handling mode "SingArea=Wrist") that freezes the sum $\theta_4 + \theta_6$ and redistributes motion equally.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping of KTU Question Patterns

| KTU Question Pattern | Section to Apply | Marks Typical |
|---|---|---|
| "Define 3R concurrent wrist and state decoupling theorem" | §1.1, §2.1 | 3 – 4 |
| "Derive $R_3^6$ for ZYZ Euler angles" | §3.1 | 7 |
| "Solve inverse kinematics of spherical wrist given $R$" | §3.2 | 7 – 14 |
| "Explain wrist singularity and how it is handled" | §3.3 | 4 – 7 |
| "Compare ZYZ and ZYX Euler angle conventions" | §2.2 | 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decoupled Inverse Kinematics

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the flow of a 6-DOF inverse-kinematics solver when the robot has a 3R concurrent wrist — exactly what you would see inside an industrial robot controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User: Desired Pose px, py, pz, R0_6] --> B{Wrist is Concurrent?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Wrist Center pw = pend - d6 * z_tool]
    B -- No --> Z[Return: Non-decouplable, full 6D numerical IK]
    C --> D[Inverse Position: Solve theta1, theta2, theta3]
    D --> E[Compute R0_3 from theta1, theta2, theta3]
    E --> F[Compute R3_6 = R0_3_transpose * R0_6]
    F --> G[Extract theta5 from r33 = cos theta5]
    G --> H{theta5 singular?}
    H -- No --> I[Extract theta4 from atan2 r23, r13]
    H -- Yes --> J[Activate Singularity Handler]
    I --> K[Extract theta6 from atan2 -r32, r31]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output: theta1 to theta6 joint commands]
    L --> M[Servo Loop: 1 kHz typical]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Wrist Singularity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Current theta5] --> S2{Is sin theta5 approx 0?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Normal IK branch]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Gimbal Lock Detected]
    S4 --> S5[Freeze theta4 + theta6 sum]
    S5 --> S6[Distribute motion 50/50]
    S6 --> S7[Apply joint rate limiting]
    S3 --> S8[Standard control loop]
    S7 --> S8
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic (Textual Representation)

Since the Mermaid engine cannot draw 3D mechanical joints natively, here is a labelled textual schematic of a 3R concurrent wrist (PUMA-style):

```
            Tool / End-Effector
                  |
            [Joint 6]  <-- Roll axis  (z6)
                  |
            [Joint 5]  <-- Bend axis  (y5)
                  |
            [Joint 4]  <-- Spin axis  (z4)
                  |
        * * * * * * * * * *   <-- Concurrent Point
        |                       (All three axes intersect here)
        |
    [Link 3 / Forearm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All three axes pass through the point marked `* * * * *`. This is the geometric definition of a **concurrent** wrist. If you tried to draw a 3D version, you would see the three axes meeting like three lines crossing the centre of a sphere.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a 3R concurrent wrist. State the decoupling theorem and explain its significance in robot kinematics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **3R concurrent wrist** is a wrist mechanism consisting of three revolute joints whose axes intersect at a single common point, called the wrist center. Because of this geometric property, the position of the end-effector is determined entirely by the first three (positioning) joints, and its orientation is determined by the last three (wrist) joints. This is the **Decoupling Theorem** (Pieper, 1968).

**Significance (1 mark each):**
- It allows the 6-DOF inverse kinematics problem to be split into two independent sub-problems of 3 DOF each.
- This reduces the computational cost for real-time control.
- It is the standard architecture for nearly all industrial 6-axis robots (PUMA, ABB, KUKA, FANUC).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "decoupling means the joints are independent." That is **wrong**. The joints are *not* independent — they are *coupled kinematically*, but the *kinematics equation itself* separates into a position part and an orientation part. Use the word **"sub-problem decomposition"** to score full marks.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is wrist singularity? Under what condition does it occur in a 3R concurrent wrist?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **wrist singularity** (also called *gimbal lock*) is a configuration in which the spherical wrist loses one degree of freedom, and the manipulator cannot produce angular velocity about a specific axis of the end-effector.

**Condition:** It occurs when the second wrist joint angle satisfies $\theta_5 = 0^\circ$ or $\theta_5 = 180^\circ$, i.e., when $\sin\theta_5 = 0$. Physically, this aligns the first and third wrist axes, making $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ produce identical orientations of the end-effector.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the geometry of a 3R concurrent wrist. List the conditions for a wrist to be classified as spherical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the wrist rotation matrix $R_3^6$ using **ZYZ Euler angles** for a 3R concurrent wrist. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Geometry of a 3R concurrent wrist (7 marks):**

- A 3R concurrent wrist consists of three revolute joints, conventionally numbered **Joint 4, Joint 5, and Joint 6** in a 6-DOF serial manipulator. **(1 mark)**
- The axes of these three joints **intersect at a single point**, called the **wrist center**. **(2 marks)**
- *Conditions for a spherical wrist* (state any 3): **(3 marks)**
  1. All three joint axes must intersect at one common point.
  2. Each joint must be revolute (R-type).
  3. The axes must be linearly independent (no two axes collinear).
  4. The kinematic chain from base to tool must have exactly 6 DOF, with the wrist forming the last 3 DOF.
  5. The end-effector translation must be a function of only the first three joints.
- *Diagram:* Show a 3-link wrist with axes $z_4$, $z_5$, $z_6$ all passing through a single marked point. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Derivation of $R_3^6$ in ZYZ form (7 marks):**

The wrist performs three sequential rotations about the moving frame:
- Rotation by $\theta_4$ about $z_3$ (axis 1).
- Rotation by $\theta_5$ about $y_4$ (axis 2).
- Rotation by $\theta_6$ about $z_5$ (axis 3).

**Step 1 (1 mark):**

$$
R_z(\theta_4) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
c_4 & -s_4 & 0 \\
s_4 & c_4 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):**

$$
R_y(\theta_5) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
c_5 & 0 & s_5 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-s_5 & 0 & c_5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):**

$$
R_z(\theta_6) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
c_6 & -s_6 & 0 \\
s_6 & c_6 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Multiply:**

$$
R_3^6 = R_z(\theta_4)\, R_y(\theta_5)\, R_z(\theta_6) =
