## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Define **joint space trajectory planning**. Mention any two advantages of planning trajectories in joint space rather than Cartesian space.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Joint space trajectory planning is the generation of time-dependent functions $\theta_i(t)$ for each joint $i$ of a robot, such that the manipulator moves from an initial joint configuration $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ to a final configuration $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$, while satisfying prescribed position, velocity, and acceleration constraints at the boundaries.

**Advantages:**
1. **No kinematic singularities** within the planned motion because the path automatically stays within the reachable joint limits.
2. **Computational simplicity** — the joint commands can be computed by direct evaluation of scalar polynomial expressions, requiring only a few multiplications per control cycle.
3. (Bonus) **Direct actuator compatibility** — the outputs are joint angles, which feed the servo loops directly without inverse kinematics recomputation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
State the **four boundary conditions** used in deriving the coefficients of a cubic polynomial trajectory, and write down the resulting expressions for $a_0$, $a_1$, $a_2$, and $a_3$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Boundary conditions:
- $\theta(0) = \theta_0$
- $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$
- $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$

Coefficient expressions:

$$a_0 = \theta_0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,2}}, \quad a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,3}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the four coefficients of a cubic polynomial trajectory for a single robot joint moving from initial position $\theta_0$ to final position $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$ with zero initial and final velocities. Show all the boundary condition equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-link robot with a revolute joint needs to move from $\theta_0 = 30°$ to $\theta_f = 75°$ in $t_f = 4$ seconds using a cubic polynomial trajectory. Compute:
- (i) The four polynomial coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The joint position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ s. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) The peak velocity and the time at which it occurs. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The cubic polynomial is:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Differentiating once:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

**Boundary conditions:**

- $\theta(0) = \theta_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_0 = \theta_0$
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_1 = 0$
- $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 t_f^{\,2} + a_3 t_f^{\,3} = \theta_f - \theta_0$
- $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^{\,2} = 0$

Solving the last two equations simultaneously:

From the velocity-at-$t_f$ equation:

$$a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} a_3 t_f$$

Substituting into the position-at-$t_f$ equation:

$$\left(-\frac{3}{2} a_3 t_f\right) t_f^{\,2} + a_3 t_f^{\,3} = \theta_f - \theta_0$$

$$-\frac{1}{2} a_3 t_f^{\,3} = \theta_f - \theta_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,3}}$$

Back-substituting:

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,2}}$$

**Final results:**

$$\boxed{a_0 = \theta_0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,2}}, \quad a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^{\,3}}}$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark each for the four coefficients, 3 marks for the derivation steps)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\theta_0 = 30°$, $\theta_f = 75°$, $t_f = 4$ s.

Step 1: Compute the displacement and convert.

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 75° - 30° = 45°$$

Converting to radians (for SI consistency in derivative units):

$$\Delta\theta = 45° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad} \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

Step 2: Compute coefficients.

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 30° \quad (\text{or } 0.5236 \text{ rad})$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 \times 45°}{4^2} = \frac{135}{16} = 8.4375 \text{ °/s}^2$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2 \times 45°}{4^3} = \frac{-90}{64} = -1.40625 \text{ °/s}^3$$

Step 3: Compute position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ s.

Position:

$$\theta(2) = 30° + 0 + 8.4375 \times 2^2 + (-1.40625) \times 2^3$$
$$= 30° + 33.75° - 11.25° = 52.5°$$

Velocity:

$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 0 + 2 \times 8.4375 \times 2 + 3 \times (-1.40625) \times 2^2$$
$$= 33.75 - 16.875 = 16.875 \text{ °/s}$$

Acceleration:

$$\ddot{\theta}(2) = 2 \times 8.4375 + 6 \times (-1.40625) \times 2$$
$$= 16.875 - 16.875 = 0 \text{ °/s}^2$$

**Verification:** Acceleration is zero at $t = t_f/2 = 2$ s, which is the expected inflection point of the cubic — **this is a sanity check that earns partial credit in KTU valuation.**

Step 4: Peak velocity.

Setting $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$:

$$2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; t = -\frac{a_2}{3 a_3} = -\frac{8.4375}{3 \times (-1.40625)} = 2 \text{ s}$$

Peak velocity magnitude:

$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 16.875 \text{ °/s} = 0.2945 \text{ rad/s}$$

Alternative closed-form check:

$$\dot{\theta}_{\text{peak}} = \frac{3 \vert \Delta\theta \vert}{2 t_f} = \frac{3 \times 45°}{2 \times 4} = 16.875 \text{ °/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $a_0$ | $30°$ (or $0.5236$ rad) |
| $a_1$ | $0$ |
| $a_2$ | $8.4375$ °/s² |
| $a_3$ | $-1.40625$ °/s³ |
| $\theta(2)$ | $52.5°$ |
| $\dot{\theta}(2)$ | $16.875$ °/s |
| $\ddot{\theta}(2)$ | $0$ °/s² |
| Peak velocity | $16.875$ °/s at $t = 2$ s |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat description of the trajectory profile, derive the expressions for the cruise velocity $\omega$ and the blend acceleration $\ddot{\theta}_c$ for a **linear trajectory with parabolic blends**, given start angle $\theta_0$, end angle $\theta_f$, total time $t_f$, and blend time $t_b$. State the assumption used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot joint is required to move from $\theta_0 = 10°$ to $\theta_f = 50°$ in $t_f = 3$ seconds using a **linear trajectory with parabolic blends**. The blend time is $t_b = 1$ s. Determine:
- (i) The cruise velocity $\omega$ and the blend acceleration $\ddot{\theta}_c$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The joint position and velocity at $t = 1.5$ s. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) The joint position and velocity at $t = 2.5$ s. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** The trajectory is **symmetric** — both blend regions have the same duration $t_b$, and the cruise velocity is constant between the blends.

**Trajectory profile (three regions):**

- **Blend-In ($0 \le t \le t_b$):** Parabolic, constant acceleration $\ddot{\theta}_c$.
- **Linear Cruise ($t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$):** Constant velocity $\omega$, zero acceleration.
- **Blend-Out ($t_f - t_b \le t \le t_f$):** Parabolic, constant deceleration $-\ddot{\theta}_c$.

**Blend-In equations** (integrating with $\theta(0)=\theta_0$, $\dot{\theta}(0)=0$):

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = \ddot{\theta}_c \, t$$

$$\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\theta}_c \, t^2$$

At the end of the blend, $t = t_b$, the velocity must equal the cruise velocity $\omega$:

$$\omega = \ddot{\theta}_c \, t_b \quad (1)$$

**Linear cruise equation** (for $t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$):

$$\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\theta}_c \, t_b^{\,2} + \omega (t - t_b)$$

**Blend-Out equations** (by symmetry, integrating from $t_f$ backward):

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = \ddot{\theta}_c \, (t_f - t)$$

$$\theta(t) = \theta_f - \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\theta}_c \, (t_f - t)^2$$

**Global constraint:** Total displacement equals the sum of contributions from the three regions:

$$\theta_f - \theta_0 = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \omega \, t_b}_{\text{blend-in}} + \underbrace{\omega (t_f - 2 t_b)}_{\text{cruise}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \omega \, t_b}_{\text{blend-out}} = \omega (t_f - t_b)$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\boxed{\,\omega = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f - t_b}\,}$$

From Equation (1):

$$\ddot{\theta}_c = \frac{\omega}{t_b} = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b (t_f - t_b)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\ddot{\theta}_c = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b (t_f - t_b)}\,}$$

**Required condition for existence of a linear region:** $t_b \le t_f/2$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\theta_0 = 10°$, $\theta_f = 50°$, $t_f = 3$ s, $t_b = 1$ s.

Step 1: Verify existence condition. $t_b = 1$ s $\le t_f/2 = 1.5$ s ✓

Step 2: Compute displacement.

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 40°$$

Step 3: Compute cruise velocity and blend acceleration.

$$\omega = \frac{40°}{3 - 1} = 20 \text{ °/s}$$

$$\ddot{\theta}_c = \frac{40°}{1 \times (3 - 1)} = 20 \text{ °/s}^2$$

**Part (i) — Complete.**

**Part (ii) — Position and velocity at $t = 1.5$ s:**

Since $t_b = 1$ s and $t_f - t_b = 2$ s, the point $t = 1.5$ s lies in the **linear cruise region** ($1 \le t \le 2$).

$$\theta(1.5) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\theta}_c \, t_b^{\,2} + \omega (1.5 - t_b)$$

$$= 10° + \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 1^2 + 20 \times (1.5 - 1)$$

$$= 10° + 10° + 10° = 30°$$

$$\dot{\theta}(1.5) = \omega = 20 \text{ °/s}$$

**Part (iii) — Position and velocity at $t = 2.5$ s:**

The point $t = 2.5$ s lies in the **blend-out region** ($2 \le t \le 3$).

Using the blend-out equation:

$$\theta(2.5) = \theta_f - \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\theta}_c \, (t_f - 2.5)^2 = 50° - \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times (0.5)^2$$

$$= 50° - 2.5° = 47.5°$$

$$\dot{\theta}(2.5) = \ddot{\theta}_c \, (t_f - 2.5) = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ °/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Cruise velocity $\omega$ | $20$ °/s |
| Blend acceleration $\ddot{\theta}_c$ | $20$ °/s² |
| $\theta(1.5 \text{ s})$ | $30°$ |
| $\dot{\theta}(1.5 \text{ s})$ | $20$ °/s |
| $\theta(2.5 \text{ s})$ | $47.5°$ |
| $\dot{\theta}(2.5 \text{ s})$ | $10$ °/s |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error on $a_3$:** Many students write $a_3 = +2(\theta_f-\theta_0)/t_f^3$. The correct sign is **negative** because the cubic must bend *down* (decelerate) toward $t_f$. **Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $t_b \le t_f/2$:** If $t_b$ exceeds $t_f/2$, the linear region vanishes and the formula gives a negative cruise time. Always check this. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Using degrees inconsistently:** Mixing degrees and radians in velocity/acceleration units is the **#1 silent error** in KTU scripts. Pick one (preferably degrees for these problems, since the inputs are in degrees) and stay consistent. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 4. **Not identifying the trajectory region before substituting:** At $t=1.5$ s with $t_b=1$ s, students often blindly use the cubic formula. Always **first check which region** the requested time falls into. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 5. **Skipping the boundary-condition statement:** Even in numerical problems, KTU examiners award 1 mark for explicitly writing down the four boundary conditions before solving. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joint Space Trajectory Planning**

- [ ] **Two schemes** in KTU Module 4: **Cubic Polynomial** and **Linear with Parabolic Blends**.
- [ ] **Cubic polynomial** has 4 coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ matching the 4 boundary conditions.
- [ ] Standard boundary conditions for cubic: $\theta_0$, $\theta_f$, and **zero** velocity at both ends.
- [ ] $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3(\theta_f-\theta_0)/t_f^2$, $a_3 = -2(\theta_f-\theta_0)/t_f^3$.
- [ ] Cubic acceleration is **linear** in $t$, **discontinuous** at endpoints (jerk is infinite).
- [ ] Cubic peak velocity is $3|\Delta\theta|/(2t_f)$ and occurs at $t = t_f/2$.
- [ ] **Linear with parabolic blends** has 3 regions: blend-in (parabolic), cruise (linear), blend-out (parabolic).
- [ ] Cruise velocity $\omega = (\theta_f - \theta_0)/(t_f - t_b)$.
- [ ] Blend acceleration $\ddot{\theta}_c = (\theta_f - \theta_0)/[t_b(t_f - t_b)]$.
- [ ] **Existence condition:** $t_b \le t_f/2$.
- [ ] In **blend-in** region: $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{\theta}_c t^2$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = \ddot{\theta}_c t$.
- [ ] In **linear cruise** region: $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{\theta}_c t_b^2 + \omega(t - t_b)$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = \omega$.
- [ ] In **blend-out** region: $\theta(t) = \theta_f - \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{\theta}_c (t_f - t)^2$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = \ddot{\theta}_c (t_f - t)$.
- [ ] Always **identify the region** before plugging time into a formula.
- [ ] Always **state boundary conditions** before solving — earns a free mark.
- [ ] Always **verify units** (degrees vs radians) — silent errors are heavily penalized.
- [ ] Linear+parabolic gives **bounded, finite acceleration**; pure linear has infinite acceleration at endpoints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cartesian Space Planning, Point to Point vs Continuous Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Space Planning, Point to Point vs Continuous Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cartesian Space Planning — Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, **Cartesian Space Planning** (also known as **Task-Space Planning** or **World-Space Planning**) is formally defined as:

> *"The process of generating a trajectory for a robotic manipulator by specifying, interpolating, and tracking motion through a sequence of poses (position + orientation) defined in the global, rectangular Cartesian coordinate frame of the work environment, and then converting these Cartesian waypoints into the corresponding joint-variable trajectories via Inverse Kinematics."*

In Cartesian space, every point on the trajectory is described by the **position vector** $\mathbf{p} = [p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$ and the **orientation matrix** $\mathbf{R}$ (or equivalent orientation representation such as Euler angles / quaternions) of the end-effector with respect to the base frame $\{0\}$.

The standard sequence of operations in Cartesian planning is:

1. Define a set of Cartesian waypoints (Cartesian milestones).
2. Interpolate between successive waypoints (Linear, Circular, or Spline interpolation).
3. At every interpolated pose, solve the **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** problem to obtain joint angles $\mathbf{q} = [\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n]^T$.
4. Drive the joint servos using the resulting joint trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Emphasis:** The defining feature of Cartesian planning is that the **path geometry is controlled in task space** — meaning the robot tool tip follows a mathematically defined curve (straight line, circle, spline) in 3D world coordinates, *regardless* of how the joints move.

## 1.2 Point-to-Point (PTP) vs Continuous Path (CP) Planning — Definitions

| Planning Type | Formal KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Point-to-Point (PTP)** | A motion control strategy where the robot is required to move from an initial pose to a final pose; the trajectory *between* the two poses is **unspecified** and only the via points matter. |
| **Continuous Path (CP)** | A motion control strategy where the robot is required to follow a **specified geometric path** (line, arc, or spline) continuously between an initial and a final pose, with controlled velocity along the entire path. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** PTP control cares only about **endpoint accuracy**, whereas CP control demands **path accuracy AND path velocity regulation** throughout the motion segment.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Stick

Think of the difference in human terms:

- **PTP (Point-to-Point) is like a Drone flying from your house to a friend's house.** The drone only cares about reaching the destination accurately. It can take *any* zigzag, curved, or looping path between the two points — what matters is the **start and end coordinates**. This is like a robotic arm in a pick-and-place operation: pick the bolt, place it — we don't care how the elbow swung in between.

- **Cartesian Space Planning (especially with Linear Interpolation)** is like a **GPS turn-by-turn navigation that locks onto the exact road segments.** You tell it "go from A to B along this straight street, then turn left on this other street." The GPS commits to a *specific straight-line geometry* in world coordinates, even if the car underneath has to steer, accelerate, and turn its wheels to honor that constraint.

- **Continuous Path Planning** is like a **calligrapher's hand drawing the letter 'S'.** Every ink-drop along the curve is monitored. The pen *cannot* skip the middle of the S and land at the end — it must trace the entire curve. Industrial analogs are **welding seams, laser cutting contours, and spray-painting edges**, where the workpiece geometry dictates the path.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** *"PTP = WHERE I start and WHERE I stop. CP = WHAT is the curve between them."*

## 1.4 Canonical Use-Case Mapping

| Robot Task | Planning Type Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Palletizing boxes | PTP | Endpoint accuracy is enough; path irrelevant. |
| Spot welding car chassis | PTP | Only the weld points need accurate positioning. |
| Arc welding a pipeline seam | CP (Continuous) | The torch must trace the joint line. |
| Laser cutting a profile | CP (Continuous) | The laser must follow the part outline. |
| Spray painting a car door | CP (Continuous) | Uniform coating requires constant path velocity. |
| Assembling PCB components | PTP / Joint-space | Pick-and-place at high speed. |
| Glue dispensing on a windshield | CP (Continuous) | Bead geometry is path-dependent. |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In KTU board examinations, the following numerical references are considered standard robotics constants:

- **Degrees of freedom of a standard 6R industrial robot:** $n = 6$.
- **TCP (Tool Center Point):** The point on the end-effector whose Cartesian position is being commanded.
- **Sampling period for trajectory generation:** $\Delta t \in [1\,\text{ms},\ 10\,\text{ms}]$ (typical).
- **Path-velocity bounds:** $|v(t)| \le v_{max}$ and $|\dot{v}(t)| \le a_{max}$ — these are the **path constraints** enforced in CP planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Competing Reference Frames

A fundamental KTU exam question is: *"Why plan in joint space sometimes and Cartesian space other times?"* The answer lies in this comparison:

| Property | Joint-Space Planning | Cartesian-Space Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Trajectory variable** | $\mathbf{q}(t) = [\theta_1(t),\dots,\theta_n(t)]^T$ | $\mathbf{x}(t) = [p_x(t), p_y(t), p_z(t), \phi(t), \theta(t), \psi(t)]^T$ |
| **Path shape in world** | Uncontrolled, generally curved | Controlled (linear, circular, spline) |
| **Kinematic singularities** | No singularities to worry about | Singularities can cause infinite joint velocities |
| **IK solver required?** | No | Yes — at every interpolation step |
| **Computational load** | Low | High (real-time IK at every millisecond) |
| **Joint limits enforcement** | Direct (clamp $\theta_i$) | Indirect (clamp after IK) |
| **Drift / unreachable points** | Cannot occur | Can occur (IK may fail for some Cartesian points) |
| **Typical applications** | PTP pick-and-place | Welding, painting, machining, CP tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** "Joint space is the *execution* space; Cartesian space is the *intent* space. The planner translates intent into execution."

## 2.2 Anatomy of Cartesian Space Planning — 7 Operational Steps

1. **Specify Cartesian Waypoints** — User (or CAD/CAM system) supplies the sequence of TCP poses:
   $\mathbf{x}_0,\ \mathbf{x}_1,\ \mathbf{x}_2,\ \dots,\ \mathbf{x}_N$.

2. **Choose an Interpolation Law** — Linear (`Lin`), Circular (`Circ`), or Spline between successive waypoints.

3. **Generate the Geometric Path $\mathbf{x}(s)$** — Parameterize the path by a normalized scalar $s \in [0, 1]$.

4. **Impose a Time Law $s(t)$** — Choose trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profile so that $|v(t)| \le v_{max}$.

5. **Apply Inverse Kinematics at each $s$** — Solve $\mathbf{q}(s) = \text{IK}(\mathbf{x}(s))$.

6. **Validate Joint Limits, Velocities, Accelerations** — Reject / rescale segments if joint limits are violated.

7. **Stream to Servo Loops** — Send $\mathbf{q}(s(t))$ to the joint controllers at the sampling rate.

## 2.3 Linear Interpolation in Cartesian Space (Lin)

Between two Cartesian waypoints $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_{i+1}$, the straight-line interpolation law is:

$$\mathbf{x}(s) = (1 - s)\,\mathbf{x}_i + s\,\mathbf{x}_{i+1}, \quad s \in [0, 1]$$

Differentiating with respect to time $t$ (where $s = s(t)$) gives the TCP velocity:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = (\mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i)\,\dot{s}(t)$$

The magnitude of the path velocity is:

$$\vert\dot{\mathbf{x}}\vert = \vert\mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i\vert \cdot \dot{s}(t)$$

The total path length of the linear segment is:

$$L_i = \sqrt{(p_{x,i+1}-p_{x,i})^2 + (p_{y,i+1}-p_{y,i})^2 + (p_{z,i+1}-p_{z,i})^2}$$

A common KTU problem is to compute the travel time of a linear segment under a constant path velocity $v$:

$$T_i = \frac{L_i}{v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orientation must also be interpolated**, typically via SLERP (Spherical Linear intERPolation) for quaternions, or via shortest-arc rotation in SO(3). A common simplification in KTU problems is to interpolate each Euler angle linearly.

## 2.4 Circular Interpolation in Cartesian Space (Circ)

For an arc passing through three Cartesian points $A$, $B$, $C$, the planner must:

1. Compute the circle center $\mathbf{c}$ and radius $r$.
2. Define a parametric arc angle $\phi \in [0, \phi_{end}]$.
3. Express the TCP position as:
$$\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \mathbf{c} + r\,\big(\cos\phi\,\mathbf{u} + \sin\phi\,\mathbf{v}\big)$$
where $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are orthogonal unit vectors in the plane of the circle.

4. Impose $\dot{\phi}(t)$ such that $\vert\dot{\mathbf{p}}(t)\vert = r\,\dot{\phi}(t) = v_{path}$.

> [!TIP]
> **For 2D problems** (planar robots), circular interpolation reduces to plain 2D circle math. KTU frequently asks for the arc time $T = r\,\phi_{end}/v_{path}$.

## 2.5 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Trajectory Generation — Tradeoff Summary

| Issue | Joint-Space | Cartesian-Space |
|---|---|---|
| Path predictability | Joints follow smooth profiles; world path is **not** a straight line | World path **is** a straight line (or arc); joint profiles may be irregular |
| Singularity handling | No singularity | Must avoid / cross carefully; near singularities $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \to \infty$ |
| Replanning if obstacle appears | Easy — just adjust joint waypoints | Hard — must re-do path in world frame, then re-IK |
| Computation | Closed-form interpolation | IK + interpolation (computationally expensive) |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *authoritative* formula bank for KTU 2024 Scheme Module-4 questions on this topic. Memorize every entry.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}(s) = (1-s)\,\mathbf{x}_i + s\,\mathbf{x}_{i+1}$ | Linear interpolation between two Cartesian waypoints, $s \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | $L_i = \vert\mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i\vert = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ | Length of a linear segment in 3D |
| 3 | $T_i = L_i / v_{path}$ | Time to traverse a linear segment at constant speed |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = (\mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i)\,\dot{s}(t)$ | Cartesian velocity from time law $\dot{s}(t)$ |
| 5 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Joint velocities from Cartesian velocities via Jacobian inverse |
| 6 | $\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \mathbf{c} + r(\cos\phi\,\mathbf{u} + \sin\phi\,\mathbf{v})$ | Circular arc parameterization |
| 7 | $T_{arc} = r\,\phi_{end} / v_{path}$ | Arc traversal time |
| 8 | $s(t) = $ trapezoidal or S-curve profile | Time-parameterization law for $s \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | $\vert\dot{\mathbf{x}}\vert \le v_{max},\ \vert\ddot{\mathbf{x}}\vert \le a_{max}$ | Path velocity / acceleration bounds |
| 10 | $\text{SLERP: } \mathbf{q}(t) = \dfrac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\,\mathbf{q}_1 + \dfrac{\sin(t\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\,\mathbf{q}_2$ | Quaternion interpolation; $\Omega$ = half-angle between $\mathbf{q}_1,\mathbf{q}_2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In the rendered notes above, the absolute-value / magnitude notation uses $\vert \cdot \vert$, not the raw `|` symbol, to preserve the markdown table structure. In your own answer sheets you may write either.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Welding:** Modern welding robots (KUKA, FANUC, ABB) operate almost exclusively in Cartesian CP mode because the weld seam is a known 3D path extracted from the CAD model of the car body.
- **Additive Manufacturing:** 6-axis robotic arms extrude material along toolpaths that are natively linear/circular in world space.
- **CNC Machining Analogy:** A 5-axis CNC mill interpolates the *tool tip* in Cartesian coordinates using G-code (G01 linear, G02/G03 circular) — the same paradigm as robotic Cartesian CP planning.
- **Surgical Robotics:** The da Vinci system displays the surgical tool tip in Cartesian space to the surgeon, while internally converting to joint commands via inverse kinematics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Parameterized Linear Cartesian Trajectory

**Problem Setup (canonical KTU problem):** A robot TCP must move in a straight line from waypoint $A = (0, 0, 0)\,\text{m}$ to waypoint $B = (2, 1, 2)\,\text{m}$. The path is parameterized by $s(t)$ which follows a *trapezoidal velocity profile* with maximum path velocity $v_{max} = 1\,\text{m/s}$ and maximum path acceleration $a_{max} = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the path length:**

$$L = \sqrt{(2-0)^2 + (1-0)^2 + (2-0)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time to accelerate to $v_{max}$:**

$$t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance covered during acceleration:**

$$d_a = \frac{1}{2}\,a_{max}\,t_a^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2)(0.5)^2 = 0.25\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Since $L = 3\,\text{m}$ and $2 d_a = 0.5\,\text{m} < L$, there is a constant-velocity cruise phase:**

$$t_c = \frac{L - 2 d_a}{v_{max}} = \frac{3 - 0.5}{1} = 2.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Total motion time:**

$$T = 2 t_a + t_c = 2(0.5) + 2.5 = 3.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Position as a function of $s$:**

$$\mathbf{x}(s) = (1-s)\,\mathbf{A} + s\,\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 2s \\ s \\ 2s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Time law for $s(t)$ (trapezoidal):**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2}\,a_{max}\,t^2 / L, & 0 \le t \le t_a \\[6pt]
\dfrac{d_a + v_{max}(t - t_a)}{L}, & t_a < t \le t_a + t_c \\[6pt]
1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\,a_{max}\,(T - t)^2 / L, & t_a + t_c < t \le T
\end{cases}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{t^2}{3}, & 0 \le t \le 0.5 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{0.25 + (t - 0.5)}{3}, & 0.5 < t \le 3.0 \\[6pt]
1 - \dfrac{(3.5 - t)^2}{3}, & 3.0 < t \le 3.5
\end{cases}$$

**Step 8 — TCP position at $t = 1\,\text{s}$ (cruise phase):**

$$s(1) = \frac{0.25 + (1 - 0.5)}{3} = \frac{0.75}{3} = 0.25$$

$$\mathbf{x}(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.25) \\ 0.25 \\ 2(0.25) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.25 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{m}$$

**Step 9 — TCP velocity magnitude check at $t = 1\,\text{s}$ (cruise):**

$$\vert\dot{\mathbf{x}}\vert = v_{max} = 1\,\text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Logic:** Full marks awarded only when (a) path length is computed first, (b) trapezoidal phase classification is justified by comparing $2 d_a$ with $L$, (c) the final numerical $s(t)$ is explicit, and (d) a check / verification step is included.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Joint Velocities from Cartesian Velocities (Jacobian Bridge)

**Problem Setup:** A 2-DOF planar robot has joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2$. The forward kinematics give:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
p_y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

The Cartesian planning module commands $\dot{p}_x = 0.3\,\text{m/s}$ and $\dot{p}_y = -0.4\,\text{m/s}$ at the instant when $\theta_1 = 30^\circ,\ \theta_2 = 45^\circ,\ L_1 = L_2 = 1\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Build the Jacobian matrix by partial differentiation:**

$$J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial p_x}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial p_x}{\partial \theta_2} \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\partial p_y}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial p_y}{\partial \theta_2}
\end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each entry:

$$\frac{\partial p_x}{\partial \theta_1} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial p_x}{\partial \theta_2} = -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial p_y}{\partial \theta_1} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial p_y}{\partial \theta_2} = L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical angles:**

$$\theta_1 = 30^\circ,\quad \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75^\circ$$

$$\sin 30^\circ = 0.5,\quad \cos 30^\circ = 0.866,\quad \sin 75^\circ = 0.9659,\quad \cos 75^\circ = 0.2588$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Jacobian:**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix}
-1(0.5) - 1(0.9659) & -1(0.9659) \\
1(0.866) + 1(0.2588) & 1(0.2588)
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
-1.4659 & -0.9659 \\
1.1248 & 0.2588
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute determinant (to confirm non-singular):**

$$\det(J) = (-1.4659)(0.2588) - (-0.9659)(1.1248)$$

$$\det(J) = -0.3794 + 1.0865 = 0.7071$$

Since $\det(J) \ne 0$, the Jacobian is invertible (robot is not in a singular configuration).

**Step 5 — Compute the inverse Jacobian:**

$$J^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(J)} \begin{bmatrix}
0.2588 & 0.9659 \\
-1.1248 & -1.4659
\end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.7071}\begin{bmatrix}
0.2588 & 0.9659 \\
-1.1248 & -1.4659
\end{bmatrix}$$

$$J^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.3660 & 1.3660 \\
-1.5908 & -2.0728
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute joint velocities:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} = J^{-1}\,\begin{bmatrix} \dot{p}_x \\ \dot{p}_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3660 & 1.3660 \\ -1.5908 & -2.0728 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = (0.3660)(0.3) + (1.3660)(-0.4) = 0.1098 - 0.5464 = -0.4366\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\dot{\theta}_2 = (-1.5908)(0.3) + (-2.0728)(-0.4) = -0.4772 + 0.8291 = 0.3519\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 7 — Verification (forward substitution):**

$$J\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & -0.9659 \\ 1.1248 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -0.4366 \\ 0.3519 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\dot{p}_x^{recovered} = (-1.4659)(-0.4366) + (-0.9659)(0.3519) = 0.6400 - 0.3400 = 0.3000\ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\dot{p}_y^{recovered} = (1.1248)(-0.4366) + (0.2588)(0.3519) = -0.4911 + 0.0911 = -0.4000\ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Note:** The verification step (Step 7) is *not optional*. KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for verifying $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Circular Arc Time for a 2-DOF Welding Robot

**Problem:** A welding robot must trace an arc of radius $r = 0.25\,\text{m}$ through an angle $\phi_{end} = 90^\circ = \pi/2$ rad at constant path velocity $v_{path} = 0.05\,\text{m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Arc length:**

$$L_{arc} = r \cdot \phi_{end} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\pi}{8} \approx 0.3927\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Arc traversal time:**

$$T_{arc} = \frac{L_{arc}}{v_{path}} = \frac{0.3927}{0.05} \approx 7.854\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Parameterization with $\phi(t) = (v_{path}/r)\,t$:**

$$\phi(t) = \frac{0.05}{0.25}\,t = 0.2\,t \quad [\text{rad}]$$

**Step 4 — TCP position in the arc plane:**

$$\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \mathbf{c} + r\,[\cos\phi,\ \sin\phi]^T$$

For a circle centered at origin:

$$\mathbf{p}(t) = 0.25 \begin{bmatrix} \cos(0.2\,t) \\ \sin(0.2\,t) \end{bmatrix}\ \text{m}, \quad 0 \le t \le 7.854\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — TCP velocity magnitude check:**

$$\vert\dot{\mathbf{p}}\vert = \sqrt{\dot{p}_x^2 + \dot{p}_y^2} = r \cdot \dot{\phi} = 0.25 \cdot 0.2 = 0.05\ \text{m/s} = v_{path} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipeline of Cartesian Space Planning — Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *complete data flow* of a Cartesian-space planner, from user-defined waypoints down to the joint servo loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User or CAD System] --> B[Cartesian Waypoint List x0 x1 ... xN]
    B --> C[Segment Classifier: Linear vs Circular vs Spline]
    C --> D1[Linear Interpolator x of s]
    C --> D2[Circular Interpolator p of phi]
    C --> D3[Spline Interpolator x of s]
    D1 --> E[Path Geometry x of s]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Time Law Selector: Trapezoidal or S-Curve]
    F --> G[Path Velocity v of t and Acceleration a of t]
    G --> H{Inverse Kinematics Solver IK at every sample}
    H --> I[Joint Trajectory q of t]
    I --> J{Joint Limit and Velocity Checker}
    J --> K[Validated Joint Commands]
    K --> L[Joint Servo Loops]
    L --> M[Robot Physical Motion]
    M --> N[Encoder Feedback]
    N --> L
    H --> O{Singular or Unreachable}
    O --> P[Replan or Skip Segment]
    P --> C
```

## 4.2 PTP versus CP Planning — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PTP[Point to Point PTP Planning]
        A1[Initial Pose Pi] --> B1[Intermediate Path UNSPECIFIED]
        B1 --> C1[Final Pose Pf]
        C1 --> D1[Goal Achieved]
    end
    subgraph CP[Continuous Path CP Planning]
        A2[Initial Pose Pi] --> B2[Mathematical Path p of s]
        B2 --> C2[Trajectory Tracker]
        C2 --> D2[Path Velocity Regulated v t]
        D2 --> E2[Final Pose Pf on path]
    end
```

## 4.3 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Planner Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Task Definition] --> Q1{Is path geometry critical?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{High speed short moves?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Straight line / Arc / Spline needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| JS[Use Joint Space PTP Planner]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Robot near singularity?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| JS
    Q4 -->|No| CS1[Use Joint Space CP Planner]
    Q3 -->|Straight Line| CS2[Cartesian Lin Planner]
    Q3 -->|Arc| CS3[Cartesian Circ Planner]
    Q3 -->|Complex Curve| CS4[Cartesian Spline Planner]
    CS2 --> IK1[Real time IK at every sample]
    CS3 --> IK1
    CS4 --> IK1
    IK1 --> END[Joint Servo Commands]
    JS --> END
    CS1 --> END
```

## 4.4 Trapezoidal Velocity Profile — Phase Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Phase 0: Rest at s=0] --> P1[Phase 1: Acceleration at a max]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2: Cruise at v max]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Deceleration at minus a max]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Rest at s=1]
    P0 -. s = 0 .-> P1
    P1 -. s increases quadratically .-> P2
    P2 -. s increases linearly .-> P3
    P3 -. s increases quadratically .-> P4
```

## 4.5 Orientation Interpolation — Quaternion SLERP Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Quaternion q1] --> SL[SLERP on unit 4 sphere]
    Q2[Quaternion q2] --> SL
    SL --> QPATH[Intermediate q of t along great circle arc]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why SLERP for orientation?** Linear interpolation of Euler angles gives uneven angular velocity and can hit gimbal lock. SLERP keeps the angular velocity constant along the great-circle arc on the unit quaternion sphere, which is the geometrically correct interpolation in SO(3).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]

> **"Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) control and Continuous Path (CP) control in robot motion planning. Give one industrial application of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PTP Control | CP Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Path definition** | Only initial and final poses are defined; intermediate path is uncontrolled | Entire path between initial and final poses is mathematically specified |
| **Accuracy requirement** | Endpoint accuracy only | Both path accuracy and endpoint accuracy required |
| **Control complexity** | Simple open-loop or servo at the joints | Requires real-time path tracking with velocity regulation |
| **Computational load** | Low | High (real-time interpolation and IK) |
| **Industrial application** | Spot welding, palletizing, pick-and-place | Arc welding, spray painting, laser cutting, glue dispensing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding 3 marks:** 1 mark for a clean two-row contrast, 1 mark for the path-accuracy distinction, 1 mark for two correctly cited applications.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Style Tag]

> **"What is Cartesian Space Planning? Why is an Inverse Kinematics solver required for it?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cartesian Space Planning, also called task-space planning, is the technique of generating a robot trajectory by specifying and interpolating the desired motion of the end-effector in the global Cartesian (XYZ + orientation) coordinate frame, rather than in the joint-variable space.

**Why IK is required (2 marks):**

1. Robots are physically actuated at the **joints**, not at the TCP. The controller can only command joint angles $\theta_i$ or joint torques.
2. A Cartesian planner generates a *Cartesian trajectory* $\mathbf{x}(t)$. To execute this trajectory, every Cartesian pose $\mathbf{x}(t)$ must be converted into a *joint pose* $\mathbf{q}(t)$ using the inverse kinematics relation $\mathbf{q} = \text{IK}(\mathbf{x})$.
3. Without IK, the robot would have no way to translate the geometric intent (a straight line, an arc) into physical motion of its links.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cartesian Trajectory Generation Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module-4 Probability High]**

> A 6-DOF robot arm must move its TCP along a straight line from point $A = (1.0, 0.5, 0.8)\,\text{m}$ to point $B = (1.0, 1.5, 1.6)\,\text{m}$. The trajectory uses a **trapezoidal velocity profile** in the path parameter $s(t)$ with $v_{max} = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$ and $a_{max} = 1.0\,\text{m/s}^2$. Initial and final orientations are $\phi_A = 30^\circ$ and $\phi_B = 60^\circ$ (roll about the tool-Z axis only).
>
> **(a)** Compute the Cartesian path length $L$, the acceleration time $t_a$, the cruise time $t_c$, and the total trajectory time $T$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write the explicit expressions for $s(t)$ during each of the three phases and compute the TCP position and orientation at $t = 1.0\,\text{s}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Path Length and Phase Timings (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Path Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{(1.0 - 1.0)^2 + (1.5 - 0.5)^2 + (1.6 - 0.8)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{0 + 1 + 0.64} \\
  &= \sqrt{1.64} \\
  &\approx 1.2806\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Acceleration Time:**

$$t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Distance Covered During Acceleration (Both Ramps):**

$$d_a = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2\,a_{max}} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{2(1.0)} = 0.125\ \text{m}$$

Total distance covered during both ramp phases (acceleration + deceleration):

$$2 d_a = 0.25\ \text{m}$$

Since $2 d_a = 0.25\,\text{m} < L = 1.2806\,\text{m}$, there is a **cruise phase**. So the trapezoidal profile is valid.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Cruise Time and Total Time:**

$$t_c = \frac{L - 2 d_a}{v_{max}} = \frac{1.2806 - 0.25}{0.5} = \frac{1.0306}{0.5} \approx 2.0613\ \text{s}$$

$$T = 2 t_a + t_c = 2(0.5) + 2.0613 = 3.0613\ \text{s}$$

**Part (b) — Time Law and Position at $t = 1\,\text{s}$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Three-Phase $s(t)$:**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{a_{max}\,t^2}{2\,L}, & 0 \le t \le t_a = 0.5 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{d_a + v_{max}(t - t_a)}{L}, & t_a < t \le t_a + t_c = 2.5613 \\[10pt]
1 - \dfrac{a_{max}(T - t)^2}{2\,L}, & t_a + t_c < t \le T = 3.0613
\end{cases}$$

Substituting $L = 1.2806,\ v_{max} = 0.5,\ a_{max} = 1.0,\ d_a = 0.125$:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{t^2}{2.5613}, & 0 \le t \le 0.5 \\[10pt]
\dfrac{0.125 + 0.5(t - 0.5)}{1.2806}, & 0.5 < t \le 2.5613 \\[10pt]
1 - \dfrac{(3.0613 - t)^2}{2.5613}, & 2.5613 < t \le 3.0613
\end{cases}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Identify Phase Containing $t = 1.0\,\text{s}$:**

Since $0.5 < 1.0 \le 2.5613$, $t = 1.0\,\text{s}$ lies in the **cruise phase**.

$$s(1.0) = \frac{0.125 + 0.5(1.0 - 0.5)}{1.2806} = \frac{0.125 + 0.25}{1.2806} = \frac{0.375}{1.2806} \approx 0.2928$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — TCP Position:**

$$\mathbf{p}(1.0) = (1 - s)\,\mathbf{A} + s\,\mathbf{B}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}(1.0) &= (0.7072)\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix} + (0.2928)\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 1.5 \\ 1.6 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.7072 \\ 0.3536 \\ 0.5658 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.2928 \\ 0.4392 \\ 0.4685 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 \\ 0.7928 \\ 1.0343 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Orientation (Linear Interpolation):**

$$\phi(t) = (1 - s)\,\phi_A + s\,\phi_B = (1 - 0.2928)(30^\circ) + (0.2928)(60^\circ)$$

$$\phi(1.0) = 21.216^\circ + 17.568^\circ = 38.784^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{p}(1.0) = (1.000,\ 0.7928,\ 1.0343)\,\text{m},\quad \phi(1.0) = 38.78^\circ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question A):**
> 1. Students often *skip* the $2 d_a < L$ check and assume cruise phase without justification — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. Using linear interpolation for *orientation* when the question specifies orientation is acceptable, but some KTU papers specifically demand SLERP — read the question stem carefully.
> 3. Rounding too early (e.g. rounding $L$ to 1.28 before substituting in $s(t)$) compounds error — **carry at least 5 significant figures until the final step**.
> 4. Do not confuse "path length" with "Euclidean distance in joint space" — they are different concepts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module-4 Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module-4 Style]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **Cartesian Space Trajectory Generator**. List the major components and describe their roles. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ is commanded to move its TCP along a circular arc of radius $r = 0.3\,\text{m}$ centered at $(0.3,\ 0.3)\,\text{m}$ in the XY-plane. The TCP currently lies on the arc at angle $\phi = 0^\circ$ and must reach $\phi = 180^\circ$ at a constant path velocity $v = 0.1\,\text{m/s}$. Compute the arc length, traversal time, and the joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ at the start, midpoint, and end of the arc. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Cartesian Trajectory Generator Block Diagram (7 marks)**

**Description of Blocks (5 marks):**

1. **Waypoint Specification Module (1 mark):** Stores the user-supplied list of Cartesian poses $\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N$. Each pose contains position and orientation.

2. **Segment Classifier (1 mark):** Determines whether the segment between successive waypoints is linear, circular, or splined, based on user flags or geometric analysis.

3. **Interpolator (1 mark):** Generates the geometric path $\mathbf{x}(s)$ for $s \in [0,1]$ using the chosen law (linear blending, arc parameterization, cubic spline).

4. **Time-Law Generator (1 mark):** Maps the geometric parameter $s$ to time $t$ via a trapezoidal or S-curve profile, enforcing $v_{max}, a_{max}$ bounds on the path velocity.

5. **Inverse Kinematics Module (1 mark):** At every sampling instant, computes $\mathbf{q}(t) = \text{IK}(\mathbf{x}(t))$ using either closed-form or numerical IK.

6. **Joint-Limit / Singularity Checker:** Rejects / rescales segments that produce joint values outside limits or that lie on a kinematic singularity.

7. **Servo Interface:** Streams the validated joint trajectory to the joint servo loops.

**Sketch (2 marks — to be drawn on the answer sheet):**

A block diagram with the sequence:
`Waypoint List → Classifier → Interpolator → Time-Law Generator → Inverse Kinematics → Joint Limit Checker → Servo Interface → Robot`

**Part (b) — Circular Arc Trajectory for 2-DOF Robot (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Arc Length and Time:**

$$L_{arc} = r \cdot \phi_{end} = 0.3 \cdot \pi \approx 0.9425\ \text{m}$$

$$T = \frac{L_{arc}}{v} = \frac{0.9425}{0.1} \approx 9.425\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Arc Parameterization:**

The TCP position on the arc is:

$$\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 + 0.3\cos\phi \\ 0.3 + 0.3\sin\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

At $\phi = 0^\circ$: $\mathbf{p}_0 = (0.6,\ 0.3)\,\text{m}$.
At $\phi = 90^\circ$: $\mathbf{p}_{mid} = (0.3,\ 0.6)\,\text{m}$.
At $\phi = 180^\circ$: $\mathbf{p}_f = (0.0,\ 0.3)\,\text{m}$.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Inverse Kinematics at Each Point:**

For the 2-DOF planar manipulator, the IK is:

$$\theta_2 = \pm\arccos\!\left(\frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$

$$\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(p_y, p_x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$$

**At $\phi = 0^\circ$ — $\mathbf{p}_0 = (0.6, 0.3)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_2 &= \frac{0.6^2 + 0.3^2 - 0.5^2 - 0.5^2}{2(0.5)(0.5)} = \frac{0.36 + 0.09 - 0.25 - 0.25}{0.5} = \frac{-0.05}{0.5} = -0.1 \\
\theta_2 &= \arccos(-0.1) = 95.74^\circ \\
\theta_1 &= \operatorname{atan2}(0.3, 0.6) - \operatorname{atan2}(0.5\sin 95.74^\circ,\ 0.5 + 0.5\cos 95.74^\circ) \\
&= 26.57^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(0.4975, 0.45) \\
&= 26.57^\circ - 47.87^\circ = -21.30^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**At $\phi = 90^\circ$ — $\mathbf{p}_{mid} = (0.3, 0.6)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_2 &= \frac{0.09 + 0.36 - 0.5}{0.5} = -0.1 \\
\theta_2 &= 95.74^\circ \\
\theta_1 &= \operatorname{atan2}(0.6, 0.3) - \operatorname{atan2}(0.4975, 0.45) \\
&= 63.43^\circ - 47.87^\circ = 15.56^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**At $\phi = 180^\circ$ — $\mathbf{p}_f = (0, 0.3)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_2 &= \frac{0 + 0.09 - 0.5}{0.5} = -0.82 \\
\theta_2 &= \arccos(-0.82) = 145.10^\circ \\
\theta_1 &= \operatorname{atan2}(0.3, 0) - \operatorname{atan2}(0.5\sin 145.10^\circ,\ 0.5 + 0.5\cos 145.10^\circ) \\
&= 90^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(0.2865, -0.9102) \\
&= 90^\circ - 162.51^\circ = -72.51^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer Summary (0.5 mark):**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
(\theta_1, \theta_2)_{\text{start}} &= (-21.30^\circ,\ 95.74^\circ) \\
(\theta_1, \theta_2)_{\text{mid}} &= (15.56^\circ,\ 95.74^\circ) \\
(\theta_1, \theta_2)_{\text{end}} &= (-72.51^\circ,\ 145.10^\circ) \\
L_{arc} &= 0.9425\ \text{m},\quad T = 9.425\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Block diagram question trap:** Students write generic blocks like "Input → Process → Output" — this scores **zero**. Each block must be a *specific* sub-module of the trajectory generator (interpolator, time-law generator, IK, etc.).
> 2. **Multiple IK solutions:** A 2-DOF planar robot has two IK solutions (elbow-up / elbow-down). The student must *state which branch is chosen* and justify (e.g., obstacle avoidance, joint-limit proximity).
> 3. **Singularity check missing:** At $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = 180^\circ$ the robot is at full reach or fully folded. The student must verify that none of the computed $\theta_2$ values are at these singularities.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Atan2 must be used — never $\arctan(p_y/p_x)$ alone, because of quadrant ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Common Pitfalls — Quick Reference

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PTP with Joint Space.** PTP is a *control strategy*; joint-space planning is a *reference frame*. They often overlap (PTP is usually implemented in joint space), but PTP *can* be done in Cartesian space too (just specify start and end Cartesian poses).
>
> 2. **Forgetting that Cartesian planning needs IK at every sample.** A common error is writing $s(t)$ but stopping at $\mathbf{x}(s(t))$ without converting to $\mathbf{q}(t)$.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the trapezoidal phase check.** If $L < 2 d_a$, the profile degenerates to a triangular profile — different formulas apply.
>
> 4. **Linear interpolation of Euler angles for orientation.** This is *acceptable* in many KTU problems but is technically incorrect — the proper method is SLERP for quaternions or shortest-arc rotation in SO(3).
>
> 5. **Forgetting Jacobian inversion near singularities.** When $\det(J) \approx 0$, joint velocities blow up. The student must check $\det(J) \ne 0$ before inverting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PTP control** specifies only start and end poses; **CP control** specifies the entire path geometry between them.
- **Cartesian Space Planning** generates trajectories in the global (world) coordinate frame using position $(\mathbf{p})$ and orientation $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{\phi}, \text{ or quaternion})$.
- **Inverse Kinematics** is the bridge between the Cartesian trajectory and the joint-level execution — it must be invoked at every interpolation sample.
- **Linear interpolation:** $\mathbf{x}(s) = (1 - s)\mathbf{x}_i + s\,\mathbf{x}_{i+1},\ s \in [0, 1]$.
- **Circular interpolation:** $\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \mathbf{c} + r(\cos\phi\,\mathbf{u} + \sin\phi\,\mathbf{v})$.
- **Path velocity:** $\vert\dot{\mathbf{x}}\vert = L\,\dot{s}(t)$ for linear segments; $\vert\dot{\mathbf{x}}\vert = r\,\dot{\phi}(t)$ for arcs.
- **Trapezoidal velocity profile phases:** Acceleration (quadratic in $s$), Cruise (linear in $s$), Deceleration (quadratic in $s$).
- **Cruise phase exists iff** $L > 2 d_a = v_{max}^2 / a_{max}$; otherwise the profile degenerates to a triangle.
- **Joint velocities from Cartesian velocities:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$, valid only when $\det(J) \ne 0$.
- **Orientation interpolation:** SLERP for quaternions (constant angular velocity); linear Euler-angle interpolation is a KTU-acceptable but approximate shortcut.
- **Industrial use cases:** Arc welding, spray painting, laser cutting → CP. Palletizing, spot welding, pick-and-place → PTP.
- **Memory hook:** *"PTP = start and stop. CP = what happens in between. Cartesian = the path is in world coordinates. Joint = the path is in motor coordinates."*
